terrainDescription: The Elfer ski area above Neustift offers a long valley run with over 1,000 metres of vertical descent on natural snow slopes. The terrain is dominated by steep, challenging runs, including the 'Freaky Eleven' mogul slope below the Elfer panorama restaurant. The ski area has 2.5 km of groomed slopes and 3.5 km of ski routes, with 65% of slopes covered by snowmaking. The valley run is 5 km long and drops 1,119 metres. The slopes face north-west, with a mix of easy and intermediate runs for beginners and a few steeper red descents for more confident skiers.
valueDescription: A day ski pass for the Elfer area costs €48.50 for adults, €40.50 for youth, and €27.50 for children. Children under 10 ski free when accompanied by a paying parent. The resort offers flexible ticket options including half-day passes, beginner slope passes, and toboggan-only tickets. Multi-day discounts are available, and from a 4-day pass onwards, the Stubai Super Ski Pass grants access to all four ski areas in the Stubai Valley. The village of Neustift has self-catering apartments and hotels, with free parking at most accommodations and ski area car parks.
familyDescription: The Elfer ski area is family-friendly with a manageable size and a dedicated practice area at Krössbach, featuring the mascot Elfi the ski owl. There are two practice lifts in Neustift and Krössbach for beginners. Children under 10 receive a free ski pass at Elferbahnen when accompanied by a paying parent. The slopes are gentle and uncrowded, making it a stress-free place for absolute beginners to learn. The resort also offers three toboggan runs, including an 8 km run, and night tobogganing two to three times a week. The ski bus connects the village to all lifts in the valley.
apresSkiDescription: The village of Neustift is quiet with limited nightlife, though the hotel bar at the foot of the Stubai Glacier is a popular apres-ski spot. Evening activities include night tobogganing on the Elfer runs every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday from 19:30 to 21:00. The resort also offers sunset skiing sessions on selected days. The village has a few restaurants and bars, but the scene is relaxed rather than lively.
resortCharacterDescription: Neustift im Stubaital is a working Tyrolean settlement rather than a purpose-built ski station. The village has a traditional character with a parish church (St. George's Church) that is Tyrol's second-largest village church. The municipality covers 249 km² and is the third largest in Tyrol by area, with over 1 million overnight stays per year. The village is located 25 km south of Innsbruck in the scenic Stubai Valley, with limestone formations at the valley entrance and peaks of gneiss and granite rising above 3,000 metres.
liftSystemDescription: The Elfer ski area has 4 lifts: one gondola (Panoramabahn) and three T-bar lifts. The total lift capacity is 2,980 persons per hour. The gondola starts at the edge of Neustift village next to the paragliding launch site. The lifts operate from 09:00 to 16:30 during the season. The ski area is served by a ski bus that connects to other lifts in the Stubai Valley. Snowmaking covers 65% of the slopes, with 8 snow-making machines.
linkedResortsDescription: The Elfer ski area is one of four ski areas in the Stubai Valley, along with the Stubai Glacier, Schlick 2000, and Serlesbahnen. A short ski bus ride (50 minutes from Innsbruck) connects Neustift to the Stubai Glacier, Austria's largest glacier ski area with 65 km of runs up to 3,210 metres. From a 4-day ski pass onwards, the Stubai Super Ski Pass is valid in all four ski areas of the valley. The Stubai Glacier offers snow guarantee from October to May, while Schlick 2000 has over 25 km of slopes and Serlesbahnen is a family-friendly area. The valley has a total of 42 cable cars and ski lifts across all areas.

terrainDescription: The 4 Vallées ski area spans from 1,350m to 3,330m, with a large portion above treeline. The terrain is divided into sectors: Verbier's Médran and Les Attelas offer varied red and blue runs; Savoleyres provides wide, sunny cruisers; Mont Fort features steep glacier descents; Bruson offers tree skiing; Nendaz and Veysonnaz have long cruising runs. The marked piste network is 412km (though an independent measurement found 164km), with 43% beginner, 47% intermediate, and 10% advanced runs.
offPisteDescription: Verbier is recognised as one of the premier off-piste resorts in the world. Notable off-piste runs include the Backside of Mont Fort, Bec des Etagnes, Stairway to Heaven, Highway, Marlenaz, Croix de Coeur, Bacombe, Col des Mines, Creblets, Couloir de la Banane, Col de la Mouche, The Rocky Garden, The Hidden Valley, Couloir des Dix, and Col de la Banane. The Bec des Rosses face hosts the Freeride World Tour finals. Mont Gelé is a mountain with no pistes, accessible by cable car to 3,023m, offering open faces in every direction. Itinerary routes like Vallon d'Arbi and Gentianes couloirs are marked but unpatrolled. Heli-ski landing spots are at Rosablanche, Petit Combin, and Trient Glacier. Avalanche risk is significant, and a guide is recommended.
valueDescription: Lift passes for the full 4 Vallées area cost CHF 94 for a day adult pass, with a six-day pass around CHF 420. Children under six ski for free. Booking online 10+ days ahead gives 10% off. Accommodation in Verbier is expensive, but the linked resorts of Nendaz, Veysonnaz, Thyon, and La Tzoumaz offer more affordable hotels and apartments. Budget options include ski-hostels in Siviez and a youth hostel in the valley near Le Châble. Self-catering apartments in Nendaz and Thyon can reduce costs.
familyDescription: Family facilities include dedicated children's areas with free baby lifts and magic carpets in Verbier (Les Esserts), Nendaz (Siviez and Tracouet), Veysonnaz, and Thyon. Free snow tubing is available in Veysonnaz, Nendaz, and Thyon. Ski schools offer lessons from age 3, and children up to 6 ski for free. The Tracouet area in Nendaz has a Jardin des Neiges with a conveyor belt and beginner lifts. Thyon's Kinderland at 2,000m features a magic carpet and fun figures. Verbier also has a free beginner lift at Les Esserts.
apresSkiDescription: Verbier's après-ski scene is one of the most renowned in the Alps. The Farinet bar, regularly voted the world's best, features live bands and a retractable roof. Pub Mont Fort, an institution since 1977, offers a large terrace and live music. On-mountain, Le Rouge provides a sunny terrace with DJs. Late-night options include the Farm Club (since 1971), South underground club, and L'Étoile Rouge. The other resorts in the 4 Vallées have quieter, more family-oriented après.
resortCharacterDescription: Verbier is a traditional Swiss village on a sunny terrace at 1,500m, with a mix of old chalets and modern developments, known for its cosmopolitan atmosphere. Thyon is a purpose-built 1970s resort at 2,095m, consisting of apartment blocks. Nendaz retains a Vieux Village with traditional wooden chalets alongside modern ski-in/ski-out complexes. La Tzoumaz and Veysonnaz are more traditional and family-oriented.
liftSystemDescription: The 4 Vallées has 77 lifts, with Verbier's sector featuring modern high-speed chairlifts and gondolas from manufacturers like Leitner and Garaventa. New lifts planned for 2026/2027 include a 6-person high-speed chairlift at Pissevache, a 6-person bubble chair at Greppon Blanc, and a 10-person gondola from Les Esserts to Les Planards. However, some areas like Thyon rely on older drag lifts, and queues can build on the Tortin lifts during peak periods.
linkedResortsDescription: The 4 Vallées is the largest linked ski area in Switzerland, connecting six resorts: Verbier, La Tzoumaz, Nendaz, Veysonnaz, Thyon, and Bruson. A single 4 Vallées pass gives access to all 410km of marked runs and 77 lifts. The connection from Verbier to Nendaz/Veysonnaz goes via the Mont Fort and Siviez sectors, while Thyon and Veysonnaz form a single integrated area. Some connections require using itinerary routes or long traverses.

terrainDescription: The Alta Badia ski area consists of 130km of runs across gentle, sun-drenched plateaus above the tree line and forest clearings in the lower valleys. The terrain is dominated by easy runs, particularly on the Pralongià plateau between La Brancia, Pralongià, and Cherz, with 57% of slopes rated easy and 36% intermediate. The most difficult piste is the Gran Risa in La Villa, a 2.5km World Cup giant slalom run with a 644m vertical drop that hosts annual FIS races. Valley runs descend to all villages, with the longest run being the 7.2km Lagazuoi-San Cassiano-Saré descent featuring a 1,055m vertical drop. The descents to San Cassiano, La Villa, and Corvara pass through forest clearings in their lower sections, while areas like Piz Sorega, Pralongià, Sponata, and Santa Croce offer notable scenic runs across the UNESCO World Heritage Dolomite landscape.
offPisteDescription: The Dolomites' abrupt limestone geology limits off-piste terrain compared to other Alpine regions, with the resort itself offering minimal dedicated off-piste beyond marked runs. The primary exception is the Val Mesdì couloir, which requires a local guide to access safely. From the Lagazuoi cable car at 2,770m, skiers can descend through a dramatic landscape of rock towers and pillars on unmarked terrain when conditions permit. Skiers visiting via the ski taxi to Passo Falzarego can access the Cortina d'Ampezzo ski area for additional off-piste opportunities. Multiple terrain parks provide alternative off-piste experiences, including the Biok Park in La Villa with jumps, rails, and boxes, and the gentler Funslope near Piz La Villa featuring banked turns.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are integrated into the Dolomiti Superski system, with Alta Badia noted as having no deposit required for lift tickets according to skiresort.info. Standard dining at pizzerias in the villages offers reasonably-priced pasta and pizza, while the slopeside restaurants carry premium pricing reflecting the resort's gourmet positioning. Accommodation in La Villa is described as having very reasonable prices compared to neighboring Corvara. Travelers from North America report the area as significantly cheaper in terms of food and accommodation than comparable resort destinations. Bolzano Airport offers direct flights from London, Berlin, Hamburg, Düsseldorf, Copenhagen, Rotterdam, and Brussels, with the shortest transfer time to the resort at approximately one hour.
familyDescription: The compact nature of the villages allows children to develop independence without straying far, while the efficient lift system reduces queuing time and maximizes family ski time together. Dedicated snow gardens and kids' clubs operate in each main village, with qualified instructors combining learning and play in enclosed, safe environments. Nursery slopes are wide, gentle, and well-separated from faster traffic, allowing parents to watch progress from nearby mountain restaurants. Older children and teenagers can progress to the Sella Ronda circuit as intermediates, with terrain parks providing additional challenges. Off-slope activities include tobogganing, ice skating, swimming, and winter hiking trails, with family-friendly restaurants throughout the villages. Colfosco is noted as particularly suitable for families seeking scenic breaks, being beginner-friendly in itself yet only two lifts from the extensive blue runs above Corvara.
apresSkiDescription: Alta Badia's après-ski scene centers on small, cozy bars and taverns with drinks and snacks, while two venues stand out for mountain-based partying. Club Moritzino at the mountain station of Piz la Ila (2,077m) combines excellent food with DJ-spun music and dancing until the last run down to the valley. Las Vegas Lodge at the Ciampai chairlift mountain station (2,017m) hosts live bands on its terrace several times per week, though skiers must begin the valley run by 5pm as rooms fill quickly. At resort level, Corvara offers the liveliest scene with L'Murin drawing the returning après crowd, while Edelweiss mountain hut above Colfosco provides a more relaxed alternative for sunset drinks. The overall après-ski offering is rated 3 out of 5 stars, with the atmosphere described as understated family fun rather than high-energy nightlife.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort encompasses six traditional Ladin villages—Corvara, Colfosco, La Villa, San Cassiano, Badia, and La Val—each retaining an old village core that has been tastefully expanded without large purpose-built blocks or concrete apartment complexes. The area sits within the UNESCO World Heritage-listed Dolomites, where jagged limestone peaks rise above rolling meadows and traditional Ladin villages. Corvara is the most developed village with the largest concentration of shops, restaurants, and bars, while San Cassiano sprawls along the valley floor and attracts non-skiers specifically for its Michelin-starred dining. La Villa is smaller and quieter with reasonable accommodation prices, while Badia and La Val offer the most peaceful settings with access to winter hiking trails and traditional Ladin culture. The overall atmosphere is refined and relaxed, appealing to those seeking quality without pretension.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of modern infrastructure throughout, with eight-seater gondolas serving the Pralongià plateau (Col Alt, Piz Sorega, and Piz la Ila connections) and two eight-seater gondolas operating in the Edelweisstal. The valley linking Corvara uses the eight-seater Borest gondola, while an eight-seater gondola system (Plans and Frara) connects back toward the Grödner Joch. Detachable high-speed four-seater chairlifts serve multiple sectors including Colz (linking La Villa and Badia), Santa Croce, Doninz, and Pre dai Corf. A total of 143 lifts across the ski area can uplift approximately 78,100 skiers per hour. Some older slow chairlifts and fixed-grip lifts remain in quieter sectors, and skiresort.info notes that connection lifts between Crep de Mont and Pralongià could be improved. The resort is credited with top ratings for lift quality by skiresort.info.
linkedResortsDescription: Alta Badia forms part of the Dolomiti Superski network, offering direct access to the famous Sella Ronda circuit—a day-long ski tour around the Sella massif connecting four valleys and four mountain passes, offered clockwise (orange direction) and counterclockwise (green direction). The Sella Ronda links Alta Badia with Val Gardena (Gröden), Arabba/Marmolada, and Val di Fassa. From Armentarola, a ski taxi provides access to the Cortina d'Ampezzo ski area via the Lagazuoi cable car. Bus connections operate to Kronplatz, while the Grande Guerra circuit connects to Ski Civetta. The Dolomiti Superski pass covers 12 interconnected ski resorts totaling approximately 1,200km of pistes and 450 lifts, including Alpe di Siusi/Seiser Alm, Alta Pusteria, Arabba, Bruneck-Kronplatz, Civetta, Cortina d'Ampezzo, Marmolada, Obereggen, Predazzo, San Martino di Castrozza, San Vito di Cadore, Val Gardena, Val di Fassa, Val di Fiemme, and Valle Isarco.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Ankogel Mallnitz is located above the treeline, with runs predominantly of intermediate (48%) and advanced (33%) difficulty. The terrain consists of wide natural snow slopes, with a vertical drop of 1,349 m from the base at 1,287 m to the summit at 2,636 m. The slopes are south-east facing, providing sunny conditions throughout the day. The longest run is 7 km. The resort has 12.8 km of groomed pistes, with 50% covered by snowmaking.
offPisteDescription: The resort offers 5 km of designated freeride terrain. Off-piste areas include deep-snow descents in the high alpine zone, popular with ski tourers and freeriders. The terrain is above the treeline, so powder conditions can be found after snowfall. No specific couloirs or glades are mentioned, but the open slopes provide ample opportunities for off-piste skiing.
valueDescription: Lift passes are reasonably priced. A one-day adult pass costs €54, with youth at €38 and children at €27. The Kärntner Skipass offers a 6-day adult pass for €323 in high season, covering 30 ski areas in Carinthia and East Tyrol. Accommodation options include family-run hotels like Bergkristall and Eggerhof, with rates generally lower than in larger Austrian resorts. Self-catering apartments are also available.
familyDescription: The resort is family-friendly, with a dedicated kids' club for children from age 3 at the lower cable car station. The club has its own ski area with a magic carpet (conveyor belt), provides lunch, and includes a final race after lessons. The nursery slopes are separate from faster traffic. The village is small and walkable, with ski school and rental shops nearby. The resort is uncrowded, making it easy for families to navigate.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key, centered around mountain huts and village restaurants serving local Carinthian cuisine. The valley station has a few bars, but the resort is not known for nightlife. Most visitors enjoy a quiet dinner and early evening. There are no large clubs or late-night venues.
resortCharacterDescription: Mallnitz is a traditional Carinthian mountain village with authentic alpine charm, located in the Hohe Tauern National Park. The resort is not purpose-built; it has a relaxed, uncrowded atmosphere with cozy restaurants and family-run hotels. The village is small and walkable, with direct train connections from Salzburg, Klagenfurt, and Vienna. The resort emphasizes sustainability and eco-tourism.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 6 lifts, including a scenic cable car (Ankogelbahn) that ascends over 1,300 vertical meters from the village to the high alpine zone. There are also chairlifts and surface lifts. The lifts are modern and well-maintained, with a total capacity of 4,880 persons per hour. Queues are rare due to low visitor numbers. The cable car operates in both winter and summer.
linkedResortsDescription: Ankogel Mallnitz is part of the wider Mölltal region. A combined ski pass is available with the Mölltaler Gletscher resort, located 20 km away. The Kärntner Skipass provides access to 30 ski areas in Carinthia and East Tyrol. The resorts are not physically linked by lifts; travel between them requires a car or shuttle.

terrainDescription: The Anzère ski area sits on a south-facing plateau above the Rhône Valley, with slopes that catch full sun from December to April. The terrain is dominated by long red runs suited to advanced intermediates, including the combination of pistes 20 (Combe d'Hérémence) and 21 (Les Rousses) which forms one of the longest descents in the resort. The lower section of Les Rousses is lined by trees. The black run Les Masques (pistes 1 and 5) drops 920 vertical metres from Pas de Maimbré back to the village. Two dedicated beginner zones exist: one at the village level with a magic carpet and rope tow, and a larger plateau at the top of the gondola at Pas de Maimbré (2,300 m) serviced by magic carpets and a small ski lift, offering a gentle gradient for first turns. The upper mountain Maimbré plateau provides beginner terrain with panoramic views. Three marked but ungroomed itineraries offer a first off-piste experience.
offPisteDescription: Anzère has several easy-to-reach off-piste zones, some of which are steep and avalanche-prone, so a guide is recommended. The rocky high-alpine landscapes around the Wildhorn Peak and Wildhorn Glacier (3,247 m) offer steep terrain for ski touring and randonnée. Helicopter skiing and ski touring on the Wildhorn are available. Within the resort, three marked and secured but ungroomed routes provide a first off-piste experience. Dedicated ski touring routes are marked on the piste map, including the Anzère–La Brune–Tsalan route (450 m vertical gain) and the Rousses route (670 m vertical gain). Night ski touring is also possible on the Masques piste. The lower Les Rousses sector has gladed off-piste routes on its more north-facing section.
valueDescription: Anzère is included in the Magic Pass, which costs 399 CHF and grants access to over 80 Swiss ski areas, making it an affordable option for multi-resort skiing. Six-day lift pass prices for the 2021/22 season were 289 CHF for adults and 179 CHF for children in both low and high seasons, with children up to age 5 skiing free. The resort offers free parking in the blue zone and metered parking. Self-catering apartments and budget-friendly accommodation options are available, and the compact pedestrian village reduces the need for paid transport. Midweek travel (Monday to Thursday outside school holidays) offers quieter slopes and potential savings on transfers.
familyDescription: The village centre is entirely pedestrian, allowing children to move freely and parents to avoid long walks with ski gear. Two dedicated beginner zones exist: a free slope in the village with a magic carpet and rope tow, and the secure 'La Cuvette' area at 2,300 m with guaranteed snow, a snow garden, blue slope, magic carpet, and small ski lift. Ski school offers lessons for children from age 2. The Pas de Maimbré restaurant provides a kids' menu with nuggets, burgers, and spaghetti, plus half-portions. A Fun Park in the village square offers outdoor play and seasonal activities. Weekly events include scavenger hunts, torchlight walks, night ski touring, and snow volleyball. The spa has a kiddy pool. Marked winter hiking trails and snowshoe routes are available for non-skiing family members.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Anzère centres on the Place du Village, the pedestrian hub with several bars and restaurants. Why Not features a tipi at the foot of the Masques slope with DJ sets. Zaza Bar offers concert nights and a wide selection of world beers. The Hawaiian-themed Tiki Bar provides a tropical atmosphere. Peter Lounge Bar serves cocktails. Karaoke nights are held at Anja and Hubi's, and La Grange has dart-throwing. The resort's spa and wellness centre is a glass-fronted facility with indoor and outdoor heated pools, jacuzzis, hammams, saunas, and treatment rooms, offering mountain views. The spa includes a kiddy pool, though the wellness and relaxation areas are adults-only quiet zones.
resortCharacterDescription: Anzère is a purpose-built ski resort created on the drawing board in 1965, with most hotels and chalets built from scratch. The village features a colourful pedestrian zone in the centre, landscaped with large chalet-style buildings housing restaurants and shops on the ground floor. The compact village sits below the gondola, with ski-in/ski-out access from many accommodations. The architecture is modest Swiss alpine style, lacking the luxury of nearby Crans-Montana or the traditional charm of villages like Grimentz, but offering convenience and a skier-oriented layout. The car-free centre and direct slope access make it easy to navigate.
liftSystemDescription: The Anzère ski area has 12 lifts, including two modern gondolas (the main village gondola and the Malo gondola, named for local World Cup skier Malorie Blanc), several quad chairs, a slow three-person chair at Les Rousses, and a two-person Duez–Le Bâte lift that forms a crossroads with the Grillesses quad. The majority of lifts are fast and modern. A new 10-person gondola, Grillesses–Bâté, opened in December 2025, replacing an old two-seater lift and improving access to the red run Les Rousses. A further new line connecting the village to Grillesses is planned for December 2028, allowing direct access to the highest point without a detour via Pas de Maimbré. The main bottlenecks occur at the Les Rousses three-person chair, which also serves the toboggan run. The lift system has a capacity of 10,750 persons per hour.
linkedResortsDescription: Anzère is not directly lift-linked to other ski areas, but it is part of the Magic Pass network, which includes over 80 Swiss resorts such as Grimentz-Zinal, Lauchernalp, Leukerbad, Ovronnaz, Nax Mont Noble, Vercorin, and St-Luc/Chandolin. The nearby Crans-Montana ski area (160 km of pistes) is a short drive away and can be visited on a day trip; some sources mention that skiers often cross into Crans-Montana, though no direct lift connection exists. Discussions about a potential future link between Anzère and Crans-Montana, and further to Adelboden, have occurred but are not implemented.

terrainDescription: The ski area is located in the Monti Sibillini range, with slopes between 1,350m and 2,000m. Currently, three lifts operate: a detachable quad chair (Schiancio-Saliere) serving a 1,073m easy run, a fixed-grip quad (Belvedere) serving a 910m intermediate run, and a magic carpet at the Campo Scuola beginner area (200m). A snowpark is situated near the Cornaccione refuge. Several historic pistes, including the 2,000m Canalone and 1,350m Cornaccione, remain closed after the 2016 earthquake. The terrain is a mix of easy (58% blue), intermediate (20% red), and advanced (22% black) runs according to recent trail maps.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste opportunities include the slopes of Monte Bicco with a small couloir, a descent from Cornaccione towards Canalone, and numerous itineraries in the Val di Bove valley. These areas are within the Monti Sibillini National Park and are suitable for experienced skiers and snowboarders. Many variants off the groomed runs lead back to the lifts.
valueDescription: A day ski pass costs €30 for adults and €25 for children during the main season, making it one of the more affordable options in Italy. The resort is small, with limited slopes, which keeps costs down. No artificial snow is currently installed, so natural snow conditions determine the season.
familyDescription: The resort offers a Babyland mini club with recreational activities for children. A magic carpet (Tapis Roulant) at the Campo Scuola provides a safe learning area for beginners. Ski lessons are available through local ski schools. The village of Frontignano is small and quiet, suitable for families.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski options include Bar Ristorante Cima del Monte and Ristorante Il Rifugio for drinks and traditional dishes. The Saliere Sibillini Mountain Lounge at 1,500m also offers a place to relax after skiing. The scene is relaxed and low-key.
resortCharacterDescription: Frontignano di Ussita is a small mountain hamlet in the Monti Sibillini, dominated by the peak of Monte Bove. The resort is not purpose-built; it retains a traditional character with stone buildings and a quiet atmosphere. The ski area is located 0.5 km from the village centre. The area was heavily affected by the 2016 earthquake, and the ski resort partially reopened in 2022.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system currently consists of three operating lifts: a modern detachable quad chair (Schiancio-Saliere) with a capacity of 2,400 persons per hour, a fixed-grip quad chair (Belvedere), and a magic carpet. A new 10-person gondola is under construction to replace older lifts, with a 2,069m length, 558m vertical rise, and capacity of 1,200 persons per hour. The gondola is planned for the 2026/2027 season.

terrainDescription: The terrain consists of approximately 6 km of marked pistes, predominantly intermediate (90%), with a few beginner runs (10%) and no advanced runs. The slopes run from Mount Fabei (around 1600 m) down to Passo dei Due Santi (1250 m), offering wide, gentle cruising runs with views of the Ligurian Sea on clear days. A 12 km cross-country skiing loop is also available, and there is a small snow park and a baby playground area.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are low, with a full-day adult ticket costing €30 on weekends and €22 on weekdays, with further discounts for residents and members of local associations. The resort is small and family-run, offering good value for beginners and intermediates who want a low-cost ski day without the crowds of larger resorts.
familyDescription: The resort is specifically designed for families, with a dedicated nursery slope (campo scuola), a baby playground area, and a snow park for children. A ski school offers lessons for all ages. The slopes are easy and medium, suitable for beginners and progressing intermediates. The compact layout means everything is within walking distance, and the refuge provides warm shelter and food.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is limited to the on-site refuge, which serves food and drinks during operating hours. There is no dedicated nightlife; the resort is quiet and family-oriented, with most visitors leaving after skiing.
resortCharacterDescription: Zum Zeri is a small, traditional ski area located at the Passo dei Due Santi in the Lunigiana region, surrounded by chestnut groves and pastures. It is not a purpose-built resort but rather a cluster of facilities around a mountain pass, with a single refuge and lift base. The atmosphere is rustic and low-key, appealing to locals and families seeking a quiet day on the snow.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of three lifts: one fixed-grip double chairlift and two surface lifts (a magic carpet and a ski lift). The lifts are old but functional, serving a small vertical drop of around 300 metres. Queues are rare due to low visitor numbers.

terrainDescription: The ski area consists of 16 runs totaling 19 km of skiable terrain, with a distribution heavily weighted toward novice (13 km) and intermediate (10 km) slopes, plus 2 km of expert runs. The slopes are well-groomed and uncrowded, offering gentle gradients ideal for beginners and intermediates. The solitary double chairlift serves a mix of easy and moderate pistes, while the surrounding pine forest provides safe off-piste routes. Snow often remains powdery until late afternoon due to low traffic.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is a highlight, with ample space for freeriding in the surrounding pine forest. The uncrowded slopes and powdery snow that lasts until late afternoon make it popular for ski mountaineering and off-piste adventures. The area around the chairlift offers safe, accessible routes through the woods, and the resort is known for excellent off-piste conditions, as noted by local reviews.
valueDescription: Lift passes are very affordable: a day pass costs approximately €22 and a six-day pass around €94, making it one of the cheaper options in the Piedmont region. The on-site restaurant and pizzeria offer reasonably priced meals, and accommodation options are budget-friendly. The resort provides good value for cost-conscious skiers seeking a quiet, uncrowded experience.
familyDescription: The resort is well-suited for families, with gentle, groomed slopes that are ideal for beginners and children. The tranquil and uncrowded atmosphere provides a safe learning environment. The on-site restaurant and pizzeria offer convenient dining, and the village has a few cozy spots for meals. However, the lift system is limited to a single slow chairlift, which may require patience during peak times.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene is modest and laid-back. The Bar des Neiges offers a cozy atmosphere with local wines, and La Vignole serves Italian cuisine. For late-night entertainment, Club 32 provides a lively option. The resort also has an on-site pizzeria run by hospitable locals. Overall, the nightlife is quiet and reflects the resort's relaxed charm.
resortCharacterDescription: Argentera is a small, traditional Italian ski village with rustic charm and a tranquil atmosphere. It is not a purpose-built resort but a natural mountain setting with a few amenities. The village is known for its enchanting natural beauty, with marmots and waterfalls in warmer months. The resort offers a down-to-earth, peaceful retreat away from crowds.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of one double chairlift and two surface lifts, with a total uplift capacity of about 4,000 skiers per hour. The chairlift is dated and slow, but the resort is uncrowded so queues are rare. Some facilities have been closed since 2020, but the remaining lifts are well-maintained. The limited lift network means the ski area is small and self-contained.

terrainDescription: The Jafferau sector is a high-altitude ski area with terrain predominantly above 2000m, consisting of long red and blue runs above the treeline. The runs are wide and groomed, with a focus on intermediate cruising. The reds are steeper than those on the Colomion side, and the snow tends to hold better due to the altitude. The return run to the base can be tricky at the end of the day. There are no advanced runs, but the terrain includes open bowls with bumps and gullies above treeline.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain on Jafferau consists of north-facing bowls above the treeline that hold powder well after storms. These open bowls offer bumps, jumps, and gullies for advanced skiers. Access is from the top lifts, and a short hike can reach the summit for additional lines. Local guides can show the best stashes.
valueDescription: The Jafferau sector is covered by the Bardonecchia lift pass, which is noted for being good value compared to other Italian resorts. Accommodation and dining in Bardonecchia are reasonably priced, and the resort offers a traditional, affordable atmosphere.
familyDescription: Jafferau is not suitable for families with beginners, as the terrain is entirely intermediate and above treeline. There are no nursery slopes or easy runs. Families with intermediate skiers may find the long reds enjoyable, but the area lacks the gentle slopes and facilities needed for young children.
resortCharacterDescription: Jafferau is a high-altitude ski sector with an alpine character, featuring open terrain above the treeline and panoramic views of the Dauphiné Alps. It is accessed by a free bus from Bardonecchia town, and the atmosphere is quiet and uncrowded, especially on weekdays.
liftSystemDescription: The Jafferau sector has 5 lifts, including chairlifts and surface lifts, serving the high-altitude terrain. The lifts are generally older but queues are rare on weekdays. The area is accessed by a free bus from Bardonecchia town.
linkedResortsDescription: Jafferau is connected to the Colomion-Les Arnauds-Melezet sector of Bardonecchia by a free shuttle bus. The two sectors are not linked by lifts, so transport is required between them. Bardonecchia is a separate ski area from the nearby Via Lattea (Milky Way) ski area.

terrainDescription: The ski area is dominated by intermediate red runs, with wide, sunny descents and some more technical sections. Notable runs include the black Diego at Belvedere and the 3-Tre run, which drops 3,300 metres from Col Rodella down to Pian de Frataces. The terrain spans open snow-covered meadows on Belvedere and the lower ridges of Pordoi, with most pistes facing south-east. The Ciampedie area above Vigo di Fassa offers gentler beginner terrain, while the Buffaure-Ciampac area provides excellent red runs and a black descent to Alba.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available from the top of Sass Pordoi (2,950 m), with routes such as the Val Mezdi descending to Passo Pordoi. A mountain guide is recommended. The area also offers freeride opportunities on north-west facing slopes beneath Sella Brunech, and guided off-piste tours on Sass Pordoi are offered by mountain guides.
valueDescription: Accommodation in Canazei and the Val di Fassa valley is generally more affordable than in neighbouring resorts like Selva or Cortina. The Val di Fassa/Carezza lift pass is cheaper than the full Dolomiti Superski pass, though the latter is needed for the Sellaronda. Self-catering apartments and campsites are available, and the FlySki Shuttle offers discounted transfers for online bookings. Some reviews note that prices have risen in recent years, but the area still offers good value compared to other Dolomiti Superski resorts.
familyDescription: The ski area has dedicated beginner areas near the valley stations in Canazei and Campitello, with ski schools offering English-speaking instruction. However, the local pistes are predominantly intermediate red runs, so beginners may need to progress quickly. The Ciampedie area above Vigo di Fassa offers gentler terrain and is less crowded. Accommodation options include family-friendly hotels and apartments, and the resort scores highly for family friendliness in user reviews. The ski schools are well-regarded, and there are off-slope activities such as the alpine coaster in Bardonecchia (nearby) and thermal spas in Pozza di Fassa.
apresSkiDescription: Canazei has a relaxed après-ski scene with around 16 bars and pubs and one club. The atmosphere is authentic Italian rather than rowdy, with bars in the village centre offering drinks and live music. Mountain huts serve food and drinks until late afternoon. The village is not wild for après but lively enough with a good balance of bars, cafes, and restaurants. Some venues offer live music on weekends.
resortCharacterDescription: Canazei is a traditional Ladin village with stone and wood buildings, set in the Fassa Valley. It retains an authentic alpine character with a pedestrianised centre and historic church. The village is not purpose-built but has grown around tourism, with modern lift infrastructure. The surrounding villages of Campitello, Alba, and Pozza di Fassa also offer traditional charm, with a mix of hotels, apartments, and B&Bs.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes modern gondolas and cable cars, such as the new 3S cable car from Campitello to Col Rodella (opened winter 2025/26) with a capacity of 2,800 people per hour. There are also chairlifts and surface lifts. Queues are generally manageable except at peak times, especially in the morning from Alba to Belvedere. The lifts connect the Belvedere and Col Rodella areas and provide direct access to the Sellaronda circuit. The system is efficient and well-maintained, with a total of 23 lifts in the local area.
linkedResortsDescription: The Belvedere-Col Rodella-Passo Pordoi ski area is directly linked to the Sellaronda circuit, providing access to Arabba-Marmolada, Val Gardena, and Alta Badia. It is also part of the Dolomiti Superski network, which includes 12 ski areas and 1,220 km of slopes. A free shuttle bus connects to other Val di Fassa ski areas such as Ciampac-Buffaure and Ciampedie. The nearby Tre Valli ski area (Alpe Lusia and Passo San Pellegrino) is accessible by bus and is covered by the Dolomiti Superski pass.

terrainDescription: The terrain is northwest-facing, with 19 pistes (4 green, 4 blue, 7 red, 4 black) ranging from 1,000 m to 2,000 m. The lower slopes feature gentle greens and blues for beginners, while the upper mountain offers reds and blacks with steeper pitches. The Lac de Pelluaz sector provides descents on the Pelluaz, La Variante, and Les Chalets Verts runs. The longest run is 4 km. Snowmaking covers 11 km of slopes with 90 snow guns. From the top, there are panoramic views of Mont Blanc and Lake Geneva.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass costs €156, and a family package for 2 adults and 2 children starts at €101 for one day. The daily adult pass is €28.80. The resort is consistently described as one of the least expensive in the region, with budget-friendly accommodation including self-catering apartments and a hotel. Reviews highlight excellent value for money compared to larger Chablais resorts.
familyDescription: Bernex is a family-friendly resort with a dedicated beginners' zone at La Mouille, served by a conveyor belt and a child lift. The ESF ski school has 50 instructors offering lessons for all ages. Childcare is available from 4 months, and the resort has a mascot (Piou Piou et Blancheau). Additional family facilities include a natural ice rink, a 4-season toboggan run, and a cinema. The resort is certified as family-oriented with a relaxed atmosphere.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene is low-key with 9 restaurants and 2 bars/cafés. The village has a cinema and a natural ice rink that hosts disco evenings during February holidays. Dining options include Savoyard cuisine. There are no discos or large nightclubs.
resortCharacterDescription: Bernex is an authentic Alpine village with traditional wooden chalets and old stone houses, centered around the historic Church of Bernex (L'église Saint-Ours) on a hill. It retains its village character with a compact centre, artisan shops, and a relaxed atmosphere, unlike large purpose-built resorts. The resort is built around the original mountain community, giving it a lived-in feel.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 12 lifts: 3 chairlifts (including a detachable 4-seater with a loading carpet) and 9 drag lifts. A shuttle train runs from the village centre to the slopes. The resort has over 100 snow guns for snowmaking. The lifts operate from 08:45 to 16:30 daily.
linkedResortsDescription: Bernex is part of the Les Montagnes d'Évian domain, which links Bernex with Abondance and Thollon-les-Mémises. The combined area offers over 80 km of pistes (35 runs: 6 green, 8 blue, 15 red, 6 black) served by 26 lifts including a gondola and 6 chairlifts. A joint ski pass is available for the linked resorts.

terrainDescription: The ski area sits between 1,630m and 1,940m with a vertical drop of 300m. It has 7km of pistes, split 33% beginner and 67% intermediate, with no advanced runs. The main piste is the red 'Standard' run, and there is a steep run called 'Hundschopf' that leads to the old chairlift top station. A chairlift built in 2007 opened up new longer pistes. The outer run 'Hinter Weissenboden' was closed during a visit in February 2022.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain includes unmarked slopes beside the chairlift and in the area of the closed outer run 'Hinter Weissenboden'. A February 2022 visit reported fresh snow in hollows made off-piste skiing enjoyable, with some hard patches. Freeriders can find numerous unmarked slopes, and ski tours from Biel offer powder descents.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs 40 CHF for adults and 26 CHF for children, making it one of the cheaper Swiss ski areas. The resort is small with only 7km of pistes, but the low price reflects its size. Accommodation is available in the Alphotel Biel-Kinzig and holiday apartments.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as family-friendly, with a dedicated children's ski lift (Kinderskilift Weissenboden) and a children's pony lift. The ski area is small and easy to navigate, with beginner slopes making up 33% of the pistes. The cable car from Bürglen takes 15 minutes, and the area is largely fog-free.
resortCharacterDescription: The ski area is located on the Biel mountain in the Schächental valley, accessed by a small 8-person aerial cableway in two sections from Bürglen. It is a natural, family-friendly area with panoramic views of the Uri Alps, not a purpose-built resort village. Accommodation is limited to a few mountain huts and holiday apartments.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a small 8-person aerial cableway in two sections (Brügg-Riedlig and Riedlig-Biel) as the only access, plus a chairlift (built in 2007), a ski lift (550m long), and a children's ski lift. The cableway operates every 20 minutes with a midday break. The chairlift replaced an older ski lift and opened new terrain.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Bielmonte consists of 19 km of pistes between 1,220 m and 1,620 m, with a vertical drop of 402 m. The slopes are predominantly intermediate (71%), with 22% beginner and 7% advanced. The most challenging run is the black-rated 'Dosso Grande', which has a 65% gradient and descends to 1,300 m. The resort is exposed to the sun all day, with slope orientations of 38% north, 27% south, 19% east, and 16% west. The longest run is 3 km. The resort also offers 20 km of cross-country trails, including a 9 km loop through beech and spruce forests at Bocchetto Sessera.
familyDescription: Bielmonte is marketed as family-friendly, with two ski schools, three moving carpets for beginners, and covered parking at the base of the slopes. Equipment hire is available from two shops on site. A free shuttle bus runs from Biella railway station on weekends in December. The resort also offers night skiing on illuminated slopes and a 9 km cross-country loop through forests. User reviews describe it as ideal for families and perfect for beginners.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski at Bielmonte is very low-key. The Monte Marca refuge offers a 360° view and serves local drinks like bombardino and Menabrea beer, along with dishes such as pizzoccheri and local cured meats. However, user reviews indicate that nightlife is essentially non-existent, with no bars or clubs beyond the mountain huts.
resortCharacterDescription: Bielmonte is a small ski station located within the Oasi Zegna Nature Park, a 100 km² reserve created by Ermenegildo Zegna. The resort is purpose-built with a covered car park, ticket office, and hospitality area at the base. It is not a traditional alpine village but rather a day-trip destination, with most visitors coming from the nearby Po Valley. The setting is scenic, with views over the Po Valley and Monte Rosa glaciers on clear days.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Bielmonte consists of 8 lifts, including several double chairlifts and a single-chair lift (Monte Marca), plus surface lifts and moving carpets for beginners. The lifts are older, with some being biposto (two-person) and one monoposto. User reviews mention the lifts are obsolete ('obsolescenza degli impianti'). The resort also has a covered moving carpet for beginners.
linkedResortsDescription: Bielmonte is a standalone ski resort not connected by lifts to other areas. Nearby resorts include Oropa (9 km away) and Alpe di Mera (9.3 km away), but they require separate lift passes. The resort is part of the Oasi Zegna Nature Park.

terrainDescription: The terrain is dominated by wide, gently pitched cruising runs on south-facing slopes above the treeline. The ski area connects the slopes of Pez d'Artgas mountain between the villages of Brigels, Waltensburg and Andiast. A terrain park with a funslope and skimovie route is available. Off-piste terrain between the prepared trails offers easy-to-ski free riding. The slopes are sunny and suitable for intermediate-level skiers.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is possible between the prepared trails across hundreds of acres of easy-to-ski terrain. The area is not steep, making it accessible for intermediate skiers looking to venture off-piste. Freeride is allowed and there is a designated freeride area. The south-facing slopes can become sunbaked, so powder conditions are best shortly after a storm.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass costs 252 CHF, and a six-day child pass costs 138 CHF. Lunch on the mountain at a chalet like Burleun Brigels costs under 20 CHF for a full meal. The resort is smaller and less expensive than nearby major resorts like Laax, offering good value for a family ski holiday.
familyDescription: The resort is family-oriented with a Snowli-Kinderland learning area directly at the valley station, featuring magic carpets and gentle slopes. The ski school offers lessons for children. The village is compact with short distances to lifts and kid-friendly lodging and dining. A free local bus connects the village districts to the valley station. The slopes are quiet and uncrowded, providing a stress-free environment for beginners and families.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is relaxed with 7 bars and pubs and one club in the village. The Pipa Lounge at Hotel Mulin offers a convivial atmosphere. Evening dining is available at several restaurants within a short stroll. The scene is low-key and family-friendly rather than rowdy.
resortCharacterDescription: Brigels is a traditional Swiss village with a compact cluster of chalets, hotels, and farms. It has a lovely old town centre with a romantic feel. The resort is not purpose-built; it retains an authentic alpine character.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 4 chairlifts, 2 T-bar lifts, 2 rope lifts, 1 conveyor belt, and 2 magic carpets, with a total capacity of 6,550 persons per hour. Some lifts are older, but the system includes modern features like a funslope and timed race course. The main chairlift from Brigels to Crest Falla undergoes regular maintenance.

terrainDescription: The ski area sits on a high plateau at around 1,429 m, with slopes rising to Colle del Caprio and higher zones near Monte Miletto. Runs are distributed across five main sectors: Capo d’Acqua, Del Caprio, Anfiteatro, Lavarelle, and San Nicola. Beginner-friendly pistes such as Scuola, Lavarelle, and San Nicola are located near the base, offering gentle gradients for learning. Intermediate runs like Capo d’Acqua and Lupo Grigio provide a gradual progression, while more technical descents—Cresta Plattner, Cristallo, Del Caprio, and Olimpica—demand solid technique and offer sustained pitches. The terrain is set within a natural amphitheatre of mountains, and on clear days the views extend to the Adriatic Sea to the east and the Gulf of Naples to the west.
familyDescription: Campitello Matese is rated the top family destination in Molise. The resort offers dedicated beginner slopes (Scuola, Lavarelle, San Nicola) near the base, providing a safe environment for children and first-timers. The compact layout and proximity of accommodation to the lifts make it easy to manage with kids. Several ski schools operate on-site, including Scuola Sci Campitello 2000, Scuola Sci Muccilli, and Scuola Sci Riccardo Plattner, offering lessons for all ages. The atmosphere is relaxed and family-friendly, with a human scale that avoids the overwhelming feel of larger resorts.
resortCharacterDescription: Campitello Matese is a purpose-built ski resort that developed from the 1970s onward, originally a shelter for shepherds. It lies on a broad plateau at 1,450 m, surrounded by the Matese massif. The architecture is functional, with accommodation and services clustered near the lifts rather than forming a historic village centre. The setting is a natural amphitheatre of mountains, and on clear days the panorama reaches both the Adriatic and Tyrrhenian seas. The atmosphere is authentic and low-key, focused on outdoor activities rather than commercial entertainment.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of seven lifts: three chairlifts (Capo d’Acqua, Del Caprio, Anfiteatro), a magic carpet (nastro trasportatore), a chairlift at La Piana/Lavarelle, and a surface lift at San Nicola. The Del Caprio chairlift also carries pedestrians. Lifts operate daily from 8:00 to 16:45. The network is modern but modest in scale, serving the main sectors efficiently. Queues are generally short except on peak weekends. The resort also has snow-making facilities covering most slopes, helping to maintain conditions during marginal weather.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 9 km of pistes across 12 runs, with a mix of beginner (47%), intermediate (20%), and advanced (33%) terrain. The longest run is 8 km. Specific slopes include the 3.5 km Panoramica (a red run) and the 2 km Vallone (a black run). A dedicated nursery slope is available for beginners and children. The resort is known as the top beginner terrain in Lazio.
valueDescription: A daily lift pass costs €20 on weekdays and €30 on weekends, with reduced rates for children up to 14 years at €15, and free for children under 5 when accompanied by an adult with a pass. Afternoon passes (from 12:30) are available for €20. Special rates exist for groups and schools, and multi-day passes are offered. Accommodation is available in nearby Filettino, with options such as self-catering apartments at Le Conifere Garden Residence and hotels like Hotel Olimpia.
familyDescription: The resort features a dedicated nursery slope (campo scuola) for beginners and children. 47% of runs are beginner-friendly (blue runs), and there are activities such as sledding, snow tubing, snowscoot, and a snow park. The ski school offers lessons for children. The atmosphere is calm and family-oriented, with a focus on a relaxed mountain experience.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski options are limited to a few mountain restaurants and rifugi, such as Ristorante Genziana (known for funghi and carni alla brace), La Perla dei Simbruini (traditional ciociara cuisine), and Lo Chalet (on the slopes, serving zuppe, polenta, and salsicce). There is no dedicated nightlife scene; the resort is quiet in the evenings.
resortCharacterDescription: Campo Staffi is a small ski resort located in the Monti Simbruini Natural Park, in the province of Frosinone, at an altitude of 1,692m. The resort consists of a ski area with a few facilities, and most accommodation is in the nearby town of Filettino, 15 km away. The setting is natural and forested, with a focus on outdoor activities and a relaxed, authentic mountain atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 4 lifts: 2 double chairlifts and 2 surface lifts. The resort claims a capacity of 8,000 skiers per hour, and queues are generally short except on peak weekends. The lifts serve a vertical drop of 500m from 1,700m to 2,000m.
linkedResortsDescription: Campo Staffi is not part of a larger lift-linked ski area. However, a 20 km cross-country ski circuit connects Campo Staffi to Monte Livata. Nearby small ski areas include Cappadocia - Campo Rotondo and Monte Livata, but these are separate resorts.

terrainDescription: The terrain is dominated by gentle beginner and intermediate slopes, with a few steeper red and black runs. The resort is set in a forested bowl, with runs winding through larch trees. The south-facing aspect ensures good sunshine. Night skiing is a highlight, with a 3 km floodlit run offering a 400-metre vertical drop.
valueDescription: Lift passes are very affordable, with a one-day adult pass at €25 and a six-day pass at €130 in high season. Accommodation is also budget-friendly, with a studio for two people costing around €320 per week and a four-person apartment around €500. Ski equipment rental is €90 per week for adults and €40 for children. Overall, the resort offers one of the lowest total stay costs in the region, making it an excellent choice for budget-conscious families.
familyDescription: The resort is well-suited for families, with a dedicated beginner area and a Club Piou Piou snow garden with a rope tow for children aged 3 and above. Childcare is available from 3 months old, and the ESF ski school offers lessons. The village is quiet and safe, with ski-in/ski-out access from some accommodations. The slopes are mostly gentle, and the resort's small scale means no long queues.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key, with 8 restaurants and 6 bars/cafes, plus a nightclub. The atmosphere is relaxed and family-oriented, with no rowdy party scene. Dining options include local restaurants like Les Alisiers and La Gaudina, serving Savoyard and regional dishes.
resortCharacterDescription: Chabanon-Selonnet is a traditional mountain village with a population of around 450, set in the Blanche Valley. The village has a historic core with an old church and castle tower, and the ski area was developed in 1970. The resort is car-free in parts, with a compact layout. The atmosphere is quiet and authentic, with larch forests and a river running through.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 12 lifts, including one double chairlift and 11 surface lifts (drag lifts). There are no gondolas or detachable chairlifts. The lifts are adequate for the small ski area, and queues are rare except during peak periods. The resort has modern snowmaking covering 30 hectares.
linkedResortsDescription: Chabanon is a standalone ski area with no direct lift connection to other resorts. The nearest ski areas are Saint Jean Montclar (8.3 km), Le Grand Puy (8 km), and others, but they are not linked by lifts.

terrainDescription: The ski area extends from 1,109 to 1,600 meters on the eastern side of Pierre-sur-Haute, alternating between forest and wild highland moors (les hautes Chaumes). The resort has developed its ski area mainly in the forest, which provides natural protection against the weather. The 12 km of downhill runs include a 2,800-meter Granges piste, trails at the foot of Pierre-sur-Haute, and a more technical Couzan sector. The slopes are exposed to the northwest and southwest. The sequence of trails from Gentianes, Replat, Bruyères, and Sangliers forms a route from the top to the snow front. The longest run is 2 km. The ski area is modest in size due to the recent classification of certain forested regions, and the Conseil général de la Loire focuses on quality with sodding, snow barriers, and 90 snow guns.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass costs €105, and a one-day pass is €21 in high season. Ski equipment rental for a week is €90 for adults and €40 for children. A studio for two people rents for €320 per week during school holidays, and a family apartment for four costs €500. The total cost of a stay for two adults (excluding transport and food) is €710, and for two adults and two children it is €1,086. Average property prices are €1,350 per m² for apartments and €1,100 per m² for chalets. These prices are significantly lower than in large Alpine resorts.
familyDescription: The resort has a dedicated beginners' area with two slopes (blue and green) and a safe tobogganing area for the whole family. The ESF ski school offers lessons at the foot of the slopes. The discovery area provides safety conditions for a first skiing experience. The resort is described as a family-friendly mid-range mountain resort, and visitor reviews consistently call it a great family resort. Summer activities include an accrobranche park, mountain biking, and hiking via the chairlift.
resortCharacterDescription: Chalmazel is the only downhill ski resort in the Monts du Forez, located in the Massif Central. The resort is situated at 1,120 meters in a place called Les Bois, 4 km from the village of Chalmazel and linked by a 6 km departmental road. It was created in 1953 and developed in the 1960s with support from the Conseil Général. The snow front includes reception, rental stores, and apartments. The resort mainly attracts daily visitors from the Forez plain, Roanne, and Saint-Étienne, all within 80 km. The medieval Château de Chalmazel, built from 1231, is in the village and offers guest rooms and reception rooms. The setting is a mix of forest and wild highland moors, with a Natura 2000 site restricting expansion.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 1 detachable bubble chairlift (the Jasseries, installed in 2002 replacing a 1967 gondola) and 7 drag lifts. The Jasseries chairlift is equipped with a liquid shock absorber system to limit wind effects. The Couzan ski lift was modernized in 2003, the Granges lift in 2006, and the Cimes lift was replaced in 2008 with a longer layout. The Pierre-sur-Haute gondola, built in 1967, was one of the first 'egg' lines by Poma. The lift park was developed alongside piste layout and snow front construction. The resort has 8 lifts total.

terrainDescription: The Christlum ski area offers 27 km of pistes spread across 950 to 1,800 metres elevation, with 22% of runs rated easy, 59% intermediate, and 19% advanced. The terrain is north-facing, which helps preserve snow quality into spring. Easy runs include the Mosenabfahrt and Brandtalabfahrt, both blue, served by surface lifts. Intermediate skiers can cruise the Zimmererabfahrt and Südabfahrt, which combine to form a 4 km descent from the Cabrio Flitzer chairlift. The advanced Gföllalmgraben run drops 500 metres over 2 km from the summit, with three variant lines. The slopes are predominantly forested, with wide, well-groomed pistes that offer views over Lake Achensee. Snowmaking covers 100% of the terrain, with 50 snow cannons ensuring consistent coverage from late November through March.
offPisteDescription: Marked powder areas are available for freeriders, and there are ski touring routes that explore the surrounding Karwendel and Rofan mountains. The Gföllalmgraben black run includes an ungroomed red variant that provides off-piste-like conditions when fresh snow falls. However, the resort is compact and primarily focused on groomed pistes, so off-piste opportunities are limited compared to larger areas.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs €68 for adults, with reduced rates of €55 for ladies, pupils, students, and those over 50. Family days on Saturdays and Sundays offer child-friendly pricing outside holiday periods. Parking at the base station is free. The resort is self-contained, so visitors do not need to buy a regional pass unless they want to ski the other Achensee areas. Accommodation ranges from luxury hotels like Posthotel Achenkirch to budget guesthouses such as Pension Christina, offering options for different budgets.
familyDescription: The Christlum ski area is designed for families, with a dedicated children's zone called the Dinopark at the mountain station. It features a button lift named DinoJet, a magic carpet, and playful learning areas. The Kinderland at the mountain station has a children's ski school. There are two ski schools with English-speaking instructors. A 3.5 km natural toboggan run from the Riederberg chairlift is illuminated on Wednesday evenings for night tobogganing. The slopes are uncrowded and the village is compact, making it easy to walk between accommodation, ski school, and lifts.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is relaxed and centred on a few mountain huts and bars. The Christlum Alm at mid-station serves traditional Austrian food. The Salettl offers music and a lively atmosphere, while the Skilehrerstüberl at the base station is a meeting point. The Christlum Lounge provides deckchairs for sunbathing. A toboggan evening runs every Wednesday from 19:00 with lift service until 21:00. The resort is not a party destination; the focus is on quiet evenings.
resortCharacterDescription: Christlum is a traditional Tyrolean ski area located on the northern shore of Lake Achensee, nestled between the Karwendel and Rofan mountain ranges. The village of Achenkirch is a small, authentic settlement with a mix of hotels, guesthouses, and holiday apartments. The resort is not purpose-built; it has grown organically around the lifts. The setting offers panoramic views of the lake and surrounding peaks. The atmosphere is peaceful and local, attracting families and those seeking a quiet alpine experience.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 10 lifts: four chairlifts (including the Christlum Express high-speed six-pack, the Cabrio Flitzer high-speed quad, the Riederberg double chair, and the Christlum double chair) and six surface lifts (Mosen, Brandtal, Nordhang, and others). The lifts operate from 08:30 to 16:00 daily. Queues are rare due to low visitor numbers. Snowmaking covers all pistes, with 50 snow cannons. The lifts are modern but not brand-new; the Christlum Express and Cabrio Flitzer are high-speed models.
linkedResortsDescription: Christlum is one of three ski areas in the Achensee region, alongside Rofan near Maurach and Zwölferkopf above Pertisau. The standard Christlum lift pass only covers the Christlum–Achenkirch area, but a regional Achensee ski pass provides access to all three areas, offering a combined 52 km of pistes. The areas are not physically connected by lifts; visitors must travel by road or bus between them. Christlum itself is not linked to larger ski networks like SkiWelt or Zillertal.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Chäserrugg-Alt St. Johann is a mix of forested slopes and high alpine areas above the treeline, with most runs facing north, which helps preserve snow quality. The longest run is the Winterreise, a 7.9 km descent from the summit to the St Johann valley. The East run (Ostabfahrt) is a 5 km intermediate run offering panoramic views of the Churfirsten mountains. The resort also features a ski cross park (Ruestel Cross) with waves, jumps, and banked turns. The summit area has a wide, gentle plateau suitable for beginners and intermediates, while steeper sections like the Sureboden and Warmtobel pistes provide challenges for advanced skiers.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is limited but available. According to user ratings, there are a few off-piste routes that can hold fresh snow for a day or two after a storm. The steep natural terrain around the Churfirsten offers some opportunities for advanced skiers, but the resort is not known for extensive off-piste areas. Most off-piste skiing is done in the trees and on the north-facing slopes.
valueDescription: Lift passes are moderately priced compared to larger Swiss resorts. A day pass for adults ranges from CHF 45 to 63 depending on demand, with a six-day pass around CHF 246. A beginner ticket covering the Iltios practice area is available for CHF 27-40. Children under 6 ski free. Accommodation options include budget hostels and guesthouses, as well as a variety of hotels. The resort offers dynamic pricing, so booking in advance can yield lower rates.
familyDescription: The resort is well-suited for families. A dedicated beginner area at Iltios features a snow garden with a magic carpet and gentle T-bar slopes. The ski school offers lessons for children from age 4, with English-speaking instructors available. A discounted beginner ticket covers the practice lifts. The 5.6 km sled run from Stöfeli to Unterwasser is popular with families, and a 3 km winter walk along the summit cliffs provides a non-skiing activity. Child-care facilities include a nursery. The resort is generally uncrowded, making it a relaxed environment for families.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key. The Zinggen pub near the Ruestel chair lift valley station and a bar at the St Johann valley station offer drinks and a social atmosphere. On Fridays, a night run from Alt St Johann to Alp Sellamatt is open from 6:30 to 9:30 pm, with a lively bar at the valley station. In March, the Espel pavilion hosts music on Saturday evenings. There are three bars and pubs in total.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is based in the traditional Toggenburg villages of Unterwasser and Alt St. Johann, which retain an authentic Swiss alpine character. The main access is via a historic funicular (Iltiosbahn, opened 1934) from Unterwasser to Iltios, followed by a cable car (1972) to the summit. The summit features a modern restaurant designed by the renowned architects Herzog & de Meuron, offering panoramic views. The area feels uncrowded and relaxed, with a mix of traditional and modern infrastructure.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 14 lifts, including a funicular, a cable car, several chairlifts, and T-bars. The funicular from Unterwasser to Iltios dates from 1934, while the cable car to the summit was added in 1972. The system has a capacity of 13,190 persons per hour. Lifts are modern and efficient, with queues rare except on weekends and school holidays. The resort is mostly in forest, so skiing is possible even in poor visibility or windy conditions, and lifts rarely close.

terrainDescription: The Cimone ski area covers the northern and eastern slopes of Monte Cimone (2,165 m) and Monte Cimoncino (2,000 m), with runs both above and below the tree line. The resort comprises six linked zones: Passo del Lupo, Polle, Cimoncino, Lago Ninfa, Montecreto, and Pian del Falco. Slopes are predominantly intermediate (red) runs, with notable black runs such as Direttissima (1.7 km) and Catinaccio (3 km). The longest run is 3.6 km. A large snowpark at Passo del Lupo features a halfpipe, jumps, rails, and boxes for freestyle skiing and snowboarding.
offPisteDescription: The northern and eastern slopes of Monte Cimone offer off-piste terrain popular with ski tourers and snowboarders. The area above the tree line provides open powder fields, while lower sections have wooded glades. The resort's snowpark at Passo del Lupo includes a halfpipe, jumps, and rails for freestyle skiing and snowboarding.
valueDescription: A day ski pass for adults costs €55 in the main season, with youth passes at €47 and children at €20. Some reviewers report finding day passes for under €40 on certain days. Accommodation in the valley villages offers a range of prices, and self-catering options are available.
familyDescription: The resort features dedicated beginner areas with babyparks at Cimoncino and Cimonelandia, where children can learn in a safe environment. An ice rink in Fanano and ski schools with instructors for children from a young age are available. The villages of Sestola and Fanano have traffic-free zones, making it easy for families to walk around.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski activities include a swimming pool with hot tub in Sestola, an ice rink in Fanano, and several spas and wellness centres. There is a cinema and numerous restaurants, such as Rifugio La Tana del Lupo at Le Polle. The atmosphere is relaxed and family-oriented, with no major nightclubs.
resortCharacterDescription: The ski area is spread across several traditional Apennine villages: Sestola, Fanano, Montecreto, and Riolunato. Most accommodations are in these valley villages, with a few ski-in/ski-out options near the lifts. The area lies within the Frignano Regional Park, offering a natural, non-purpose-built character.
liftSystemDescription: The lift network comprises 20 lifts: 1 gondola, 2 high-speed six-packs, 3 high-speed quads, 3 triple chairs, 4 double chairs, and 7 surface lifts. The main zones are connected by chairlifts, and a ski bus links the Pian del Falco area. However, some reviews indicate that certain chairlifts are poorly maintained and the cable car is often closed.
linkedResortsDescription: The Cimone ski district consists of six interconnected zones: Passo del Lupo, Polle, Cimoncino, Lago Ninfa, Montecreto, and Pian del Falco. All are accessible with a single ski pass, and the slopes and lifts link them together, allowing skiers to move between areas without removing skis.

terrainDescription: Claviere's local Monti della Luna ski area offers around 33 km of mostly short and wide intermediate pistes. The runs are tree-lined and wind through woods, with the lower slopes suitable for beginners and the upper slopes mostly red or black. Two chairlifts from the village base area lead to a further chairlift that accesses the upper sectors. Highlights include the longer descents from Col Saurel and the runs down to the mid-altitude hamlet of Sagnalonga. The westernmost base-area chairlift serves short red and black pistes on forested slopes directly in front of the village, as well as a red-classified link to Montgenèvre. There is also an easier link to Montgenèvre via a separate chairlift at the upper western end of the village.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain in Claviere is modest but includes some very nice tree skiing on the lower slopes. There is scope for sidecountry play, but serious powder seekers should head deeper into the Milky Way area, exploring Sagnalonga and La Montanina on the Italian side, or the peaks between Claviere and Montgenèvre. The off-piste is extensive enough to warrant hiring a guide. For a good challenge, head to Montgenèvre and up the opposite Chalvet side, beyond the Col de l’Alpet.
valueDescription: Claviere is a budget-friendly resort, often featuring as the lead-in price option for ski package holidays to the Milky Way region. Lift passes are affordable: a day ticket for the Montiluna-Montgenevre area costs €54 for adults, and the Montiluna pass (Cesana-Claviere) costs €35.50-42. Accommodation and dining are also competitively priced, making it a good-value gateway to the 400 km Via Lattea ski area.
familyDescription: Claviere is well-suited to families and beginners. The beginners' area is just a short walk from the village centre, equipped with an easy-to-use conveyor-belt surface lift. The compact layout means access to slopes is convenient. The ski school caters for English, French, Spanish, and Italian speaking guests. The tree-lined blue runs back to the base area are great for novices making their first forays away from the nursery slopes. The village is small and quiet, with limited traffic, making it safe for children.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski in Claviere is very low-key. The village has only a couple of basic bars, with a tiny basement bar at the central Passero Pellegrino hotel. Most evening activity revolves around sociable drinks in hotel lounge bars after dinner. For something more exciting, some mountain restaurants host occasional evening snowmobile excursions for dinner followed by torchlit ski descents. There is also a small outdoor natural ice skating rink, usually open during the coldest weeks. For livelier nightlife, the French resort of Montgenèvre is only two kilometres up the road, accessible by taxi or a pisted walking route.
resortCharacterDescription: Claviere is a tiny, traditional alpine village on a mountain pass, with a ribbon of houses, a few hotels, and a handful of shops. The steep sides of Mt Chaberton and proximity to the slopes have left little space for expansion, so it has retained an authentic, uncommercialised feel. It is a small, quiet village with limited facilities, best described as a collection of houses and hotels rather than a purpose-built resort.
liftSystemDescription: Claviere has 8 lifts serving its local Monti della Luna area, including two chairlifts from the village base area and a further chairlift to the upper sectors. The lifts are connected to the larger Via Lattea system, which totals 70 lifts across the international ski area. The local network is modest but functional, with easy links to Montgenèvre via chairlifts. No information on the age or modernisation of the lifts is provided in the source text.
linkedResortsDescription: Claviere is part of the international Via Lattea (Milky Way) ski area, which includes the Italian resorts of Sansicario, Sauze d'Oulx, Pragelato, and Sestriere, as well as the French resort of Montgenèvre. The area offers over 400 km of pistes with 70 lifts. Claviere and Montgenèvre are directly connected by ski lifts and pistes, allowing easy cross-border skiing. From Claviere, skiers can also access the rest of the Via Lattea via Cesana Torinese and the gondola to Sansicario.

terrainDescription: The Corvatsch-Furtschellas ski area offers 120 km of marked pistes, with 11% rated easy, 74% intermediate, and 15% difficult. Glacier slopes start at 3,303 m above sea level. Wide and long runs lead over treeless terrain around the Corvatsch mid-station and Furtschellas, while forest runs descend to the valley stations at Surlej and Sils. The longest run is 6 km from the Furtschellas mountain station to the Corvatsch valley station at Surlej, with a vertical drop of 930 m. The legendary Hahnensee run is a 9 km black slope from Giand'Alva via Hahnensee to St. Moritz Bad, beginning with wide open steep sections and ending in forest. Night skiing is available on Switzerland's longest floodlit piste, 4.2 km, every Friday.
offPisteDescription: Freeriders will find many unsecured off-piste powder opportunities available between the 120 km of groomed runs. The ski area includes 2 km of marked ski routes and 61 km of ski routes in the wider Corvatsch-Diavolezza-Lagalb area. Off-piste terrain exists around the Corvatsch Glacier and in the trees below the treeline sections, but avalanche risk is present and local knowledge is recommended.
familyDescription: The Kinderpark at Corvatsch offers a dedicated area for children to try winter sports for the first time, including a specially designed children's lift. The Swiss Ski School Corvatsch runs structured group lessons for children and beginners. The ski area has 13 km of easy slopes suitable for beginners. The traffic-free village centre in nearby Silvaplana and Surlej allows families to walk to lifts in boots. Ski-in/ski-out accommodation is available at the base stations.
apresSkiDescription: The Hossa Bar, located next to the Alp Surlej chairlift valley station, serves cocktails and hosts parties every Friday with DJs and food until closing time. The Sonnenstübli in St. Moritz Bad at the bottom of the Hahnensee descent offers snacks and drinks in an après-ski atmosphere. During Snow Night on Fridays, the Murtèl restaurant at the middle station serves pizzas from a wood-fired oven until late, with a night bus operating hourly. The Ustaria Rabgiusa ski hut between Furtschellas and Corvatsch serves Italian specialities like flatbread and polenta dishes.
resortCharacterDescription: Corvatsch-Furtschellas is a high-alpine ski area above the villages of Silvaplana, Surlej, and Sils im Engadin in the Upper Engadin valley. The area is not a single purpose-built resort but a collection of traditional Engadin villages with access to the mountain. The ski area features a mix of glacier terrain above treeline and forested lower slopes, with views over the Engadin lakes. The highest restaurant in Graubünden, Panorama Restaurant 3303, sits at the summit of Corvatsch at 3,303 m.
liftSystemDescription: The Corvatsch-Furtschellas ski area has 14 lifts: 5 gondolas, 6 chairlifts, and 3 drag lifts, with a total capacity of 18,510 persons per hour. The wider Corvatsch-Diavolezza-Lagalb area has 19 lifts serving 35 marked pistes. The Corvatsch cable car and the Murtel-Corvatsch gondola provide access to the highest terrain. The Giand'Alva chairlift starts operating at 9:00 am. The Hahnensee Express shuttle bus runs every 30 minutes between St. Moritz-Signal and Surlej-Corvatsch, free for skiing visitors. Snowmaking covers 30% of the slopes with 200 snow cannons.
linkedResortsDescription: Corvatsch-Furtschellas is part of the Engadin St. Moritz winter sports region, which includes 350 km of pistes across four large ski areas: Corviglia/Piz Nair, Corvatsch, Diavolezza/Lagalb, and Zuoz, plus five smaller areas. The Corvatsch-Diavolezza-Lagalb ski area has 35 marked pistes served by 19 lifts. A Snow Safari route connects Sils-Furtschellas-Corvatsch-St. Moritz Bad-Corviglia-Marguns in a single day. The ski areas are not lift-linked but are connected by regular ski buses and trains. The Upper Engadin ski pass covers all areas.

terrainDescription: The resort offers 14 marked pistes, with blue runs (pistes 1, 4, 9, 10) for beginners, red runs (pistes 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 11, 12) for intermediates, and black runs (pistes 13, 14) for advanced skiers. The highest skiing elevation is 2,250 m, and the terrain is considered suitable for intermediate-level skiers. Runs are marked with colored flags indicating difficulty.
resortCharacterDescription: Davraz is a small ski resort located on Mount Davraz in the Taurus Mountains, 25 km from Isparta. The resort is centered around a single main hotel, the Sirene Davras Ski and Wellness Hotel, situated at 1,650 m. It offers a boutique skiing experience with views of Lake Eğirdir.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 7 lifts, including an 8-person gondola, 2- and 4-person chairlifts (some with bubbles), and drag lifts. The lifts provide access from the base at 1,650 m to the highest skiing elevation of 2,250 m.

terrainDescription: The ski area spans from 1,260m to 2,001m, with 24 km of pistes winding through larch forest. 47% of runs are green, 24% blue, 24% red, and 6% black. The longest descent is 6 km. The summit at Le Cuchon offers panoramic views of the Champsaur valley and the Vieux Chaillol massif.
