liftSystemDescription: The lift network comprises 57 lifts (per KitzSki page), including gondolas, chairlifts, and a tri-cable system built by Doppelmayr that has the highest elevation above ground (400 m) in the world. The 3S gondola linking the Pengelstein area to the Kitzbüheler Horn is among the longest in the world. The Resterhöhe sector above Jochberg opens as early as late October due to extensive snowmaking. Lift queues are generally short, though the main Hahnenkamm lifts can be busier on Saturday changeover days; the Bichlalm gondola is a recommended alternative on those days.
linkedResortsDescription: The KitzSki area connects seven municipalities and two federal states (Tyrol and Salzburg): Kitzbühel, Reith, Aurach, Jochberg, Pass Thurn–Mittersill, Hollersbach, Kirchberg, and Aschau in the Spertental. The single KitzSki pass gives access to all 170 km of pistes across these linked sectors. The Kitzbühel Alps AllStarCard extends access to nearby Ski Welt, SkiCircus Saalbach, and other areas, all within 30 minutes' drive. The Pass Thurn sector borders the state of Salzburg and includes the Resterhöhe area, which opens in late October.

terrainDescription: The ski area spans 30 km of groomed pistes between 1,450 m and 1,971 m, with 7 km of easy blue runs, 15 km of intermediate red runs, and 7 km of difficult black runs. The terrain includes five black runs, some of the steepest in Slovenia, and a trio of black runs on the main face. The lower slopes near the gondola station host a wide nursery area with a moving carpet and drag lift, but there are few easy blue runs to progress to beyond the beginner zone. The pistes are south-facing, receiving a lot of sun, and are extensively covered by snowmaking with good grooming. A terrain park with features for beginners and experienced freestylers is also present.
offPisteDescription: Krvavec has a decent freeride reputation with a couple of marked freeride itineraries. After fresh snowfall, the open slopes and tree-lined sections offer good potential for off-piste riding. The resort also serves as a jumping-off point for ski touring, with a designated Ski Alp trail available daily from 8am to 4pm, and on Fridays and Saturdays from 5pm to 7pm. The terrain is dynamic and includes some steep sections that hold powder well when conditions allow.
valueDescription: Adult day passes cost €45 (2026 season), with children aged 6–14.99 paying €27 and children under 6 skiing free. Youth (15–18.99) and seniors over 65 pay €39. On-mountain food is inexpensive: a hot dog costs under €5 and a beer around €3. Car rental from Ljubljana Airport is cheap, and accommodation in nearby Ljubljana or Kranj is significantly lower than in Alpine resorts. The local lift pass also covers Rogla ski area near Maribor, adding further value. Overall, a four-day break with three days of skiing can cost roughly half the average European ski holiday price.
familyDescription: The resort is family-friendly with a dedicated children’s area at the Gospinca station featuring a fun park, moving carpet, and drag lift. A ski school (Krvavec Intersport Snow Sports School) offers lessons for children and adults, and there is a kindergarten. However, the progression from nursery slopes to longer easy runs is limited – there are few easy blue runs beyond the beginner zone, which may frustrate families with children advancing quickly. Most visitors are local families on day trips, and the atmosphere is jolly and relaxed. Night sledding on the Tiha Dolina run (Fridays and Saturdays until 19:00) is a popular family activity.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is centred on the mountain rather than a village. The Plaza Bar at Hotel Krvavec offers on-mountain cuisine and a terrace for drinks. The Tiha Dolina area has a lively bar with music, beers, and cheap food. Night sledding on the illuminated Tiha Dolina run operates from 16:30 to 19:00 on Fridays and Saturdays, providing an evening activity. There is no significant nightlife in the resort itself; most visitors return to Ljubljana or nearby towns after skiing. Occasional food festivals and events add variety.
resortCharacterDescription: Krvavec is not a traditional resort village but a mountain centre with a single hotel (Hotel Krvavec) on the slopes. There is no central village; most visitors are day-trippers from Ljubljana or nearby towns. The base station consists of a gondola, ticket office, and a small cafe. Accommodation on the mountain is limited, with most guests staying in Ljubljana (30 minutes away), Kranj, or Bled. The resort has a functional, no-frills feel focused on skiing rather than alpine charm, though the panoramic views of the Ljubljana basin and the Kamnik-Savinja Alps are striking.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 15 lifts: one high-speed six-seater chairlift (new in 2026 with heated seats on Zvoh), seven quad chairs, four double chairs, and three surface lifts. A circular gondola connects the base (1,450 m) to the main mountain station. Queues are rare except on peak weekends. The new six-seater has reduced waiting times. The lifts are a mix of modern and older models, with recent investment in faster cable cars. The gondola ride takes about 10 minutes.
linkedResortsDescription: The local lift pass for Krvavec also covers Rogla ski area near Maribor. Additionally, the Ski Pass Slovenia allows access to most of the country's 230 km of pistes across multiple resorts, including Kranjska Gora, Vogel, and Cerkno, though these are not directly linked by ski lifts. Krvavec itself is a standalone mountain without direct connections to other ski areas.

terrainDescription: The terrain at La Berra is predominantly beginner-focused, with wide, gentle slopes between 1,000m and 1,640m. The resort is served by a telemix (combination chairlift and gondola) and several T-bars, including one dedicated to beginners. Snowmaking covers 4–5 km of the pistes, ensuring reliable conditions on the lower sections. The longest run is 5 km. The resort is noted as the best beginner terrain in the Fribourg region.
valueDescription: Lift passes are reasonably priced, with a day pass for adults ranging from 39 to 49 CHF depending on the source. Children's day passes are 25–34 CHF, and seniors 34–45 CHF. Hourly rates are also available, making it affordable for short visits. The resort accepts the Magic Pass and offers free skiing for those over 80. Self-catering accommodation is available in nearby La Roche and Bulle, though the resort itself has limited on-site lodging.
familyDescription: La Berra is a family-oriented resort with a ski school and rental on site. There is a dedicated beginner T-bar and a ski treadmill for first-timers. The resort has two restaurants and a picnic room, making it easy for families to take breaks. A free shuttle bus operates during holidays, Wednesday afternoons, and weekends. The slopes are easily accessible from a large tarmacked car park. The resort also offers snowshoe trails and connections to nearby Charmey and Schwarzsee for additional winter activities.
resortCharacterDescription: La Berra is a small, locally-oriented ski area in the Fribourg Pre-Alps, a short drive from the towns of Fribourg and Bulle. The resort consists of a telemix and several T-bars serving slopes on the mountain. Facilities include two restaurants, a picnic room, and a large tarmacked car park. The resort is not a purpose-built village but a day-trip destination, with accommodation available in nearby La Roche and Bulle. The summit offers panoramic views of the Lake of Gruyère region.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a telemix (combination chairlift and gondola) and four T-bars, including one dedicated to beginners, plus a ski treadmill. The lifts are modern and well-maintained, with the telemix providing efficient access to the upper slopes. The resort has snowmaking on 4–5 km of pistes. Lift queues are generally short due to the resort's small size.
linkedResortsDescription: La Berra is connected to the nearby resorts of Charmey and Schwarzsee via snowshoe trails. These connections allow for extended winter outings, though they are not lift-linked ski areas. The resort is part of the La Gruyère region, with other ski areas within a short drive.

terrainDescription: The terrain is set in the Vosges mountains, with runs winding through beech and fir forests. The ski area offers 30 marked trails: 5 green, 12 blue, 12 red, and 1 black, covering 220 hectares. The slopes are generally wide and gentle at the start, with red runs providing variety for more experienced skiers. The longest run is 2 km. The area is known for its tree-lined pistes rather than open alpine terrain.
valueDescription: Lift passes are significantly cheaper than in the Alps, with a one-day adult pass at €40.30 and a six-day pass at €174. The resort offers early-bird (Aurore) passes from 6am and night (Nocturne) passes until 21:45, adding flexibility. Accommodation and dining are also reasonably priced, with self-catering apartments and family-friendly deals. The overall cost of a stay is lower than many Alpine resorts, making it a budget-friendly option for families and weekend skiers.
familyDescription: La Bresse-Hohneck is certified Famille Plus, with dedicated facilities for children including a crèche/daycare, an ESF kindergarten, and a play area with sledding. The resort has wide, gentle nursery slopes and a snowfield (Wiidoo'Gliss) with a conveyor belt for first-timers. The village is compact and pedestrian-friendly, with accommodation close to the slopes, reducing the need for transport with children.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key and family-oriented, with a few bars and pubs in the village offering drinks and live music on certain evenings. The resort also offers night skiing sessions until 21:45, which provides a different atmosphere. Mountain restaurants like Le Slalom serve Savoyard dishes. The scene is relaxed rather than rowdy.
resortCharacterDescription: La Bresse is a traditional Vosges mountain town, not a purpose-built resort. The village has a warm, authentic character with fir forests, peaceful valleys, and rounded ridges. Accommodation includes chalets, hotels, and apartments in the village and near the slopes. The resort is compact and pedestrian-friendly, with a lively village centre.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 15 lifts, including 2 detachable chairlifts (one high-speed six-pack and one high-speed quad), 3 fixed-grip quad chairs, 1 triple chair, and 9 surface lifts (including 2 magic carpets for beginners). The lifts are modern and efficient, with the detachable chairs reducing queue times. The resort also offers early-bird and night skiing, extending lift operating hours.
linkedResortsDescription: La Bresse-Hohneck is the main alpine sector of the La Bresse ski area, which also includes the Lispach and Brabant sectors. Lispach offers gentler slopes and a Nordic area, while Brabant provides a more intimate family setting. The three areas are linked by road and shuttle, but not by ski lifts. Together they form the largest ski area in the Vosges.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Ladurns consists of wide, north-facing slopes that are predominantly blue and red, with two black runs: the Ladurns IV slope and the Patrick Staudacher slope, named after the local 2007 Super-G World Champion. The longest run is 7.5 km, and the total skiable terrain is 18 km. The slopes are well-groomed and suitable for beginners and intermediates, with the black runs providing challenge for advanced skiers. The valley run was widened a few years ago.
valueDescription: A day ski pass costs €57 for adults and €40 for children, with a six-day pass at €286 for adults and €200 for children (2026 prices). The resort is part of the Wipptal Ski Pass (covering Monte Cavallo, Racines-Giovo, and Ladurns) and the Südtirol Skiarena Pass, offering good value for families and budget-conscious skiers. The resort has been awarded five stars as an insider tip for ski resorts under 20 km by Skiresort.de, highlighting its price-performance ratio.
familyDescription: Ladurns is specifically designed for families, with a 5,000 sqm children's area called Fichti's Kinderland located at the base of the gondola, featuring a dedicated beginner area and magic carpets. The ski school has been awarded the Gold seal of quality for children's instruction. The slopes are wide and manageable, and there is a 6.5 km toboggan run. The resort has been awarded five stars as an insider tip for ski resorts under 20 km by Skiresort.de.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key and centered on three mountain huts within the ski area: the Ladurner Hütte, Pfeifferhuiserle, and Edelweiß Hütte. These huts serve South Tyrolean specialties such as dumplings, shepherd-style macaroni, homemade blueberry schnapps, and fresh apple strudel. The Pfeifferhuiserle is noted for its après-ski atmosphere. There is no vibrant nightlife; the scene is relaxed and family-oriented.
resortCharacterDescription: Ladurns is a compact, family-oriented ski area set in the wild and romantic Val di Fleres, a side valley of the Upper Valle Isarco near the Brenner Pass. The resort is not a large purpose-built village but rather a collection of mountain huts and a gondola base station, with the nearest town being Vipiteno (Sterzing) 10 km away. The area retains a traditional South Tyrolean character with alpine huts and scenic views.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of three main lifts: a modern gondola (Ladurns Cable Car), the Wastenegg Chairlift (designed by Pininfarina), and a surface lift, plus three magic carpets in the beginner area. The lifts are modern and efficient, with short queues and waiting times. The gondola starts at the car park on the road to Fleres, 10 km from Vipiteno.
linkedResortsDescription: Ladurns is part of the Upper Valle Isarco ski alliance, which includes Monte Cavallo and Racines-Giovo, together offering around 50 km of slopes accessible with the Wipptal Ski Pass. It is also part of the larger Südtirol Skiarena Pass, which covers multiple ski areas in South Tyrol. The nearest linked resorts are Monte Cavallo (near Vipiteno) and Racines-Giovo, both within a short drive.

terrainDescription: La Fouly's ski area covers 20 km of pistes with 10 named runs: 4 black, 2 red, 2 blue, plus several yellow (ungroomed) itineraries. The main chairlift rises to 2,200 m, where a broad open red piste offers wide carving lines and space for side hits. A narrow blue run zigzags through forest from the mid-mountain, while the black Les Bassins and Le Chamois runs provide steep, direct descents to the base. The lower mountain features a free learner area with a magic carpet and a T-bar serving gentle practice slopes. Yellow itineraries such as Les Mélèzes and Les Luis cut through trees on the skier's right, offering ungroomed terrain when open.
offPisteDescription: The resort has three patrolled freeride routes, and the yellow (ungroomed) itineraries from the Arpalle summit—Les Mélèzes and Les Luis—provide steep, varied descents through trees that hold fresh snow for several days after a storm. The Snow-Forecast community rates off-piste quality at 4.2 out of 5, noting a 'vast range of off-piste routes that can remain monitored for several days.' The natural terrain and quiet backcountry feel make it popular with freeriders and touring skiers.
valueDescription: A single-day lift pass at La Fouly costs CHF 43–46 (adult), with half-day passes at CHF 38 and children's passes at CHF 30. The standout value is the annual Pays du Saint-Bernard pass at CHF 159, which grants access to La Fouly, Champex-Lac, and Vichères–Liddes for the entire winter season, and also includes early- and late-season access to Verbier (up to early December and from early April). Budget accommodation is available at Ski Hostel in Liddes, offering half-board packages from €950 per week including transfers and tuition. The free learner area (magic carpet) requires no lift pass, reducing costs for beginners.
familyDescription: La Fouly is well-suited to families: the learner area at the base is free to access via a magic carpet, and a T-bar serves gentle practice slopes. The ski school offers lessons for all levels, with two dedicated beginner zones equipped with obstacles along the snow escalator and the Petite Combe lift. Childcare facilities are rated 4.5 out of 5 by visitors, with at least one reasonably priced nursery available. The resort's quiet, uncrowded slopes and wide beginner runs make it a calm environment for children to learn, and the village is compact with direct slope access.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski at La Fouly is low-key. The Auberge de Glaciers at the base station offers fondue and views of the final steep red run, and gets busy at lunch. A small bar at the top of the chairlift plays house music and serves beer with panoramic valley views. Night skiing is available on the Petite Combe run on Wednesdays and Saturdays during the season, with evening admission at CHF 5. The village itself has a few restaurants but no dedicated nightclub scene.
resortCharacterDescription: La Fouly is a traditional Alpine village at 1,600 m in the Val Ferret, at the foot of Mont Dolent (3,823 m) and the Tour Noir (3,836 m). The village consists of wooden chalets and a few small hotels, with direct access to the ski slopes. The atmosphere is quiet, authentic, and uncrowded, described as 'very Swiss Chocolate box vibey' by visitors. It is not a purpose-built resort; the ski area is integrated into the existing village, and the surrounding landscape includes glaciers and alpine pastures listed as UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at La Fouly consists of four installations: one double chairlift (described as relatively old but swift), two T-bar surface lifts, and a magic carpet for beginners. The chairlift rises from the base to mid-mountain, where a T-bar continues to the Arpalle summit at 2,200 m. Lifts are rarely busy, with virtually no queues even during peak periods. The system is functional rather than modern, but adequate for the resort's size.
linkedResortsDescription: La Fouly is part of the Pays du Saint-Bernard ski region, which includes three other small ski areas: Champex-Lac (with scenic lakeside runs and wide intermediate pistes), Vichères–Liddes (offering a more local, slightly steeper feel), and Bourg St Pierre (a single run used mainly by ski schools). The entire network covers approximately 113 km of pistes served by 17 lifts. A single Pays du Saint-Bernard ski pass grants access to all areas, and the annual pass also includes early- and late-season access to Verbier, located 20–30 minutes away by car.

terrainDescription: The ski area spans Monte Agaro (2,060 m) near Passo Brocon (1,616 m) in Trentino, offering mostly intermediate and easy wide slopes with one difficult run (Slope No. 5 Strafaiole) completing the range; the longest run is Slope No. 6 Paradiso at 2.4 km with a 342 m elevation drop; 100% of slopes have snow-making capabilities, and three valley runs lead to Chalet Heidi at the base station, with parts of the lower slopes illuminated for night skiing; runs are located both just above and below the tree line.
offPisteDescription: The source rates off-piste terrain at 3.7/5 in visitor evaluations; ski touring and freeride opportunities are available at Passo Brocon, with dedicated freeride activities and ski mountaineering in the surrounding valley; an ARVA (avalanche) safety course area is present on the mountain.
valueDescription: Day tickets cost €45 for adults and €35 for children (from skiresort.info), with the Trentino source listing daily ski passes at €40-€47; partial-day passes (2-hour at €27, 3-hour at €32, half-day at €36) and night skiing passes at €19 are also available; parking at the base station is free of charge.
familyDescription: The resort is rated 4/5 for family-friendliness, featuring the Agarotta Park at the valley station with a covered conveyor belt, practice slopes, children's carousel, adventure course, and magic carpet; ski schools offer children's courses and child care; parents can monitor children from Chalet Heidi's proximity; the Skilandia children's club and Snowpark Lagorai provide additional family activities.
apresSkiDescription: After-ski is described as a relaxed affair rated 2/5 stars; the sun terrace at Chalet Heidi provides the main venue for winding down after skiing; no nightlife or clubs are mentioned, indicating a quiet atmosphere focused on leisurely slope-side relaxation rather than active entertainment.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is located in the Sugana Valley (Valsugana) in Trentino, described as a family-friendly, clearly laid-out area with intact nature and panoramic Dolomite views; the nearby towns include Marande (0.1 km), Castello Tesino (15 km), and Pieve Tesino (18 km); accommodation is available in the surrounding Tesino basin with ski bus connections from Borgo Valsugana.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 4 lifts according to skiresort.info (3 chairlifts and 1 conveyor belt) or 6 lifts according to snow-forecast.com; skiresort.info notes as a negative point that there are no fast lifts; a covered conveyor belt serves the valley station for beginners; lifts operate from 09:00 to 16:30 daily.

terrainDescription: La Norma's ski area covers 65 km of pistes across 27 trails, with 7 green, 7 blue, 11 red, and 2 black runs. The terrain is predominantly easy and intermediate, with wide, well-groomed slopes that wind through fir trees. The north-facing aspect helps preserve snow quality, and the resort's compact layout means most runs return to the village. The longest run is 12 km, and the vertical drop is 1,400 m from 2,750 m to 1,350 m.
offPisteDescription: La Norma offers diverse off-piste terrain on its north-facing slopes, including couloirs, alpine bowls, tree runs, ridges, and gullies. The marked itinerary routes provide a controlled introduction to unpisted terrain, while experienced skiers can access more remote zones with a guide. North-facing aspects hold powder well after snowfall, and the relatively low traffic means tracks are not skied out quickly. The resort's proximity to the Vanoise National Park creates additional off-piste possibilities.
valueDescription: La Norma is one of the more affordable ski resorts in the French Alps. A one-day adult lift pass costs around €42.50 (2024/25 prices), with child passes at €35.20. The pass is valid at both La Norma and the linked Valfréjus area, giving access to 135 km of pistes. Accommodation is mostly ski-in/ski-out and reasonably priced. The resort's pedestrian village reduces the need for car hire or shuttle costs. Overall, it offers good value for families and budget-conscious skiers.
familyDescription: La Norma is a pedestrian-only resort, making it safe and easy for families with young children. The village is car-free, with accommodation, ski school, and slopes all within walking distance. The nursery area at the base has gentle slopes separate from through-traffic, and the ESF offers children's programmes including a snow garden for ages 3 and up. The ski area's compact size means families can ski together without getting lost, and the wide, well-groomed blue runs provide natural progression. Off the slopes, there is a sledging track, ice skating, and a terrain park for older children.
apresSkiDescription: La Norma's après-ski scene is quiet and family-oriented, with a handful of bars and restaurants in the pedestrian village. There are about 10 restaurants and 2 bars/pubs, offering local Savoyard cuisine and a relaxed atmosphere. Evening entertainment includes occasional live music and themed events. The resort is not known for a lively nightlife; it suits those looking for a calm end to the day rather than partying.
resortCharacterDescription: La Norma is a purpose-built ski resort opened in 1971, but it was designed as a pedestrian village with wooden buildings and a central shopping area, giving it a cosy, alpine feel. The resort is car-free, with accommodation clustered around the slopes. It is not a historic mountain village but has a charming, family-friendly atmosphere. The architecture is traditional Savoyard style, and the resort is set on a wooded plateau overlooking the Vanoise massif.
liftSystemDescription: La Norma has 13 lifts, including 1 gondola, 5 chairlifts, and 6 drag lifts, with a total capacity of 16,000 persons per hour. The lift system is modern and efficient, with minimal queues even during peak periods. The main gondola from the village provides quick access to the upper mountain. The resort's compact layout means most lifts are within walking distance of accommodation. The link to Valfréjus is via a free shuttle, not a direct lift connection.
linkedResortsDescription: La Norma is linked to the neighboring Valfréjus ski area via a free shuttle service for ski pass holders. Together they offer 135 km of pistes. The connection is not by lift but by road shuttle, taking about 10 minutes. A combined ski pass is available, and the two resorts complement each other: La Norma has more gentle terrain, while Valfréjus offers more open slopes.

terrainDescription: The Latemar Dolomites ski area spans the western and southern flank of the Latemar Group, connecting the villages of Obereggen, Pampeago, and Predazzo. The terrain is dominated by intermediate red runs, which make up 67% of the 50 km of pistes, with 19% blue and 14% black. The slopes are well-groomed, with highlights including the Oberholz red run (#2) and the Maierl red run (#5) in the Absam Maierl area. The connections between Obereggen and Predazzo are seamless, with no long flat traverses, making it suitable for snowboarders. The longest run is 3.5 km. The black 'Agnello' slope in Pampeago is 2.6 km long with a 420-metre vertical drop. The resort also features two timed slopes in Pampeago. The views are less dramatic than other Dolomites resorts, but the skiing is varied across the three sectors.
valueDescription: Daily lift passes for the 2025/26 season cost €64–€71 for adults, €58–€64 for seniors, €45–€50 for juniors, and €23 for babies (free with an adult pass). Afternoon passes from 12 pm are cheaper. Seasonal passes are also available. Accommodation ranges from ski-to-door hotels in Obereggen (e.g., Sporthotel Obereggen, Hotel Cristal) to budget-friendly guesthouses in Predazzo. The resort is part of the Dolomiti Superski network, but a local Val di Fiemme-Obereggen pass is available for those staying within the area.
familyDescription: The Latemar Dolomites is well-suited for families, with dedicated beginner areas at each access point. Obereggen (1,530 m) has ski school, rental shops, and a ski pass office on site. Pampeago (1,757 m) also offers direct slope access and facilities. Predazzo (1,018 m) requires a 10-minute cable car ride to the slopes. The resort features a Baby Park, the Fun Park Latemar MontagnAnimata with the Alpine Coaster Gardonè, and the 'La Foresta dei Draghi' forest path. There are sledge runs and a 550-metre-long snowpark for older children. The slopes are 67% red, providing gentle progression for intermediates, and the resort is rated as family-friendly by OnTheSnow.
apresSkiDescription: The apres-ski scene in Latemar Dolomites is subdued, with a relaxed atmosphere. Night skiing is available on a 2 km floodlit slope at LOOX – The Alpine Club in Obereggen. The mountain huts, such as In.Treska and Oberholz Hütte, offer gourmet dining with panoramic views, but the village nightlife is quiet. The resort is more focused on daytime skiing and mountain dining than vibrant evening entertainment.
resortCharacterDescription: The Latemar Dolomites ski area is a collection of three distinct villages: Obereggen in South Tyrol, and Pampeago and Predazzo in Trentino. Obereggen is a modern ski village with ski-to-door accommodation and contemporary architecture, including the striking Oberholz Hütte, an eco-sustainable timber structure. The villages are functional rather than picturesque, with a subdued atmosphere. The resort lacks the dramatic scenery of other Dolomites areas but offers convenient slope access and well-maintained infrastructure.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 18 lifts, including 2 gondolas, 13 chairlifts (with several high-speed quads and six-packs), and 2 surface lifts. The infrastructure is modern and efficient, with seamless connections between Obereggen and Predazzo. The resort has no long flat connecting runs, which is advantageous for snowboarders. The lifts are well-maintained, and the resort has been open for 26 years. Snowmaking covers 48 km of the 50.8 km of skiable terrain.
linkedResortsDescription: The Latemar Dolomites ski area connects the three villages of Obereggen, Pampeago, and Predazzo within a single lift network. It is part of the Val di Fiemme-Obereggen ski area, which is one of 12 areas in the Dolomiti Superski pass. The Dolomiti Superski pass provides access to 1,200 km of slopes across the Dolomites, but the other areas (e.g., Val Gardena, Cortina) are not directly lift-linked and require road travel. The resort is also planning a future connection to Thurnthaler in Austria and Skiarea Val Comelico, which would expand the terrain to 160 km.

terrainDescription: The ski area is located on the south-west and south-east slopes of the Nob mountain in the Bregenz Forest Mountains, with altitudes from 1,035 m to 1,771 m. It offers 27 km of marked pistes: 11 km easy, 12 km medium, and 4 km difficult, plus three ski routes that are the longest descents and require advanced skill. Most runs face south (56%) and west (31%), so they receive good sun exposure. The main pistes are groomed and 6 km are covered by snowmaking. The longest continuous descent is 5 km.
valueDescription: The resort is consistently described as one of the cheapest in Austria. A day ski pass for adults costs €46, a six-day pass €202, and a child day pass €24 (2026 prices). Accommodation and dining are also reasonably priced, making it a budget-friendly option for families and groups.
familyDescription: The resort is family-oriented with a dedicated Kinderland area near the Falba Stuba restaurant, offering free childcare and a magic carpet lift for beginners. Practice slopes at Pizzaeck are separate from main runs, and the compact layout allows parents to keep children in sight. A 4 km natural toboggan run and a children's restaurant add to the family appeal.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is a traditional Austrian village in the Laterns Valley, a side valley of the Alpine Rhine Valley. The ski area is centered around the hamlet of Innerlaterns, with a compact, non-purpose-built layout. It offers a relaxed, family-friendly atmosphere typical of a genuine alpine village.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a 4-seater chairlift, a modern 6-seater chairlift, two T-bar lifts, a practice lift, and a magic carpet. Total uphill capacity is 9,000 persons per hour, ensuring minimal queues. The lifts are modern and efficient, with the two chairlifts providing direct access to the ski area without waiting times.

terrainDescription: The terrain in La Thuile is dominated by north- and north-east-facing slopes, with the Italian side generally steeper than the French side. The densely forested slopes directly above the village contain some of the steepest runs in the Espace San Bernardo, including fall-line black runs that serve as home-runs. The longest piste is red run number 7, an 11 km descent from Chaz Dura (2,579 m) that follows the summer road over the Piccolo San Bernardo pass, passing ancient buildings and winding through woodland. Above the Les Suches gondola, a bowl offers wide blue runs for progressing skiers, while the Belvedere area provides high-altitude red runs with views of Mont Blanc. The Mont Valaisan sector, added in 2018/19, introduced five new red runs at up to 2,800 m. The slopes on the French side of La Rosière are gentler and sunnier, adding variety to the cross-border ski area.
offPisteDescription: La Thuile is known for its rugged off-piste terrain, particularly on the north-facing slopes that hold snow well. The Mont Valaisan sector, opened in 2018/19 with two new six-person chairlifts, created a designated freeride area accessible from the lifts. Heli-skiing is available from the Italian side, with drop-off points on Mont Miravidi (3,058 m) and the Rutor Glacier (3,486 m), offering routes into France. A snowkiting zone exists at Colle del Piccolo San Bernardo (2,188 m), lift-accessed with views of Mont Blanc. Since the 2022/23 season, new cross-border ski touring trails connect La Thuile to La Rosière, with a prepared track from Les Suches to Belvedere Pass and onward to the Chardonnay chairlift. Off-piste terrain can also be found on the wooded lower slopes bordered by piste number 4, and in the Combe des Moulins area near La Rosière.
valueDescription: La Thuile is generally more affordable than comparable French resorts, with accommodation and dining prices lower than in La Rosière or Courmayeur. Lift passes for the Espace San Bernardo cover both the Italian and French sides, and the pass also includes two days of skiing at other Aosta Valley resorts such as Courmayeur and Pila, with free bus connections. The village has a range of accommodation from rustic lodges to modern hotels, with many small family-run options near the lifts. Dining is competitively priced, with traditional Italian staples like pasta, pizza, polenta, and local Valdostana dishes. The resort's low profile means fewer crowds and lower costs compared to more famous destinations.
familyDescription: La Thuile offers nursery slopes in the village centre serviced by a magic carpet, with a special low-cost pass for beginners. The Les Suches area at 2,200 m provides gentle blue runs and snow gardens for progressing skiers, though novices must return by gondola as the runs back to the village are steep. The Scuola Sci La Thuile offers small group lessons, though English-speaking support is more limited than in French resorts. Off the slopes, the resort has an ice skating rink, a dedicated snow playground, and the Pyramid recreational area with table football, ping pong, and games consoles. Dog sledding, snowshoeing, and a sledging hill are also available. The village is compact and quiet at night, making it easy to navigate with children.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in La Thuile is low-key and Italian in character, with bars like Le Petit Skieur and Le Privé offering friendly, music-filled evenings. The Roxy bar at the Planibel complex is a lively base-area spot. Most bars are quiet and laid-back, with local grappa and wines rather than loud parties. Dining options include wood-fired pizzas, polenta, fontina, and wild boar stew at restaurants such as Lo Riondet and Maison de Neige. The nightlife is subdued due to the low number of hotel beds (approximately 2,000), but the Cage Aux Folles bar attracts a livelier crowd. Many visitors prefer to relax after skiing rather than party.
resortCharacterDescription: La Thuile is a former mining town that has expanded tastefully, blending traditional Italian alpine architecture with modern developments. The old centre features cobbled streets, stone houses, and a riverside square with shops, cafés, and restaurants. The Planibel complex at the base of the slopes is a modern focal point with a sports centre, swimming pool, and ski-in/ski-out accommodation. Most hotels and chalets are within walking distance of the Bosco Express or Les Suches gondola, though few are directly ski-in/ski-out. The village has a quiet, local feel with strong Italian hospitality, and is less crowded than nearby Courmayeur.
liftSystemDescription: The Espace San Bernardo has 38 lifts, including 1 gondola, 21 chairlifts, 11 ski lifts, and 5 magic carpets. The main uplift from the village is a gondola and a fast chairlift, with a combined capacity of over 5,000 passengers per hour, ensuring minimal queuing even on peak days. The gondola also serves as a download for beginners. In 2018/19, two new six-person chairlifts were installed on Mont Valaisan, extending the ski area to 2,800 m. The lift network is efficient, with a total capacity of 50,000 skiers per hour relative to only about 2,000 hotel beds, so queues are rare. The link to La Rosière involves a long drag lift (Bellecombe) which can be challenging for snowboarders.
linkedResortsDescription: La Thuile is part of the international Espace San Bernardo ski area, lift-linked to the French resort of La Rosière. The combined area offers 152 km of pistes across Italy and France, accessible with a single ski pass. The pass also grants two days of skiing at other Aosta Valley resorts, including Courmayeur (connected by free bus) and Pila (by public bus). The link to La Rosière is via the Belvedere chairlift and the Chardonnet lift to Col de la Traversette, though it can close in high winds. The Mont Valaisan sector, added in 2018/19, provides additional terrain on the French side.

terrainDescription: The ski area spans 150 hectares from 1,160 m to 1,850 m on the slopes of the Cantal volcano, with 44–45 marked runs (10 green, 14–15 blue, 14–15 red, 5 black) covering 60 km of downhill pistes. The terrain is predominantly forested with pine and beech trees, providing shelter in poor visibility and a natural, varied layout. Beginners have a dedicated 2-hectare zone with two magic carpets, while intermediates can cruise long blue and red runs through the trees. Advanced skiers find steeper sections on the upper mountain, including black runs and two designated freeride areas at Font d’Alagnon and La Combe. The volcanic landscape creates natural rollers and playful terrain, particularly appreciated by snowboarders.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available in two designated freeride zones: Font d’Alagnon and La Combe, which offer natural terrain shaped by the volcanic landscape. The forested slopes between marked runs provide tree-skiing opportunities, especially in the pine and beech stands. The upper mountain near Plomb du Cantal (1,855 m) has open bowls and steeper sections that can hold powder after storms, though the resort’s moderate altitude means snow conditions vary. A snowpark and boardercross run are also present for freestyle skiing. No guided off-piste tours are explicitly mentioned in the sources, but the freeride areas are clearly marked.
valueDescription: Le Lioran is one of the most affordable ski resorts in the Massif Central. A full-day adult lift pass costs €35 (as of the 2025/26 TER promotion), with reduced rates for children (€27) and students (€29). Accommodation and dining prices are lower than in the Alps, with budget-friendly self-catering apartments and family-run hotels available. The resort’s own train station at the foot of the slopes eliminates the need for expensive transfers, and free shuttle buses connect the different sectors. Equipment rental discounts of 20% are offered to TER train passengers, further reducing costs.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as 100% family-friendly, with a dedicated 2-hectare beginner zone equipped with two magic carpets and a telebaby lift, separate from main traffic. The ESF (French Ski School) offers group and private lessons for children, and the village is compact and car-free, making it safe for young children to walk in ski boots. Off-slope activities include an ice rink, toboggan runs, pony sledging, electric snowmobiles for children, bouncy castles, and a giant zip line. The train station at the base of the slopes allows families to arrive directly without a car, and free shuttles connect the accommodation areas to the lifts.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski at Le Lioran is low-key and family-oriented. The village has a handful of bars and restaurants, such as those near the Prairie de Sagnes centre, but there are no nightclubs or large-scale party venues. Evening entertainment centres on the ice rink, toboggan runs, and occasional events organised by the resort. The atmosphere is relaxed, with most visitors retiring early after a day on the slopes. For those seeking more lively options, the nearest towns (Murat, 13 km away) offer additional dining and bar choices.
resortCharacterDescription: Le Lioran is a mid-altitude resort in the Massif Central, consisting of the original valley settlement (Le Lioran village at 1,152 m) and the purpose-built higher station of Super-Lioran (Prairie de Sagnes), where most accommodation, shops, and lifts are concentrated. The architecture is a mix of traditional Savoyard-style stone chalets and modern apartment blocks from the 1960s and 1970s. The resort is set within the Parc Naturel Régional des Volcans d’Auvergne, giving it a natural, forested setting rather than an alpine village feel. It is car-free in the main pedestrian zones, with free shuttles connecting the different sectors.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 19 lifts (18 according to some sources), including one cable car (Téléphérique du Plomb du Cantal, built 1967, recently renovated), two detachable chairlifts, six fixed-grip chairlifts, six surface lifts, and two magic carpets. The total lift capacity is 24,010 passengers per hour. While the cable car and some chairlifts are modern, several lifts are older fixed-grip models. Queues can form on peak weekends and during school holidays, but the network is generally efficient for the resort’s size. Snowmaking covers approximately 30–50% of the ski area with 240–280 snow cannons.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Le Sauze is undulating, with runs weaving through open glades and larch forests. The ski area spans from 1,400m to 2,400m, offering 65km of pistes. The majority of runs are beginner (59%) and intermediate (36%), with only 6% advanced. The resort has two base levels: Le Sauze 1400 and Super Sauze 1700, both with direct slope access. The piste breakdown includes 6 green, 9 blue, 17 red, and 4 black runs.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste enthusiasts can access avalanche-free couloirs within a 30-minute hike from the ski area, offering fine powder conditions. The resort also offers marked, safe ski touring itineraries accessible to all levels.
familyDescription: Le Sauze is marketed as a family resort, with two snow gardens, a fun zone, and a pedestrian-only piste front at Le Sauze 1700. The variety of slopes includes gentle greens and blues for beginners, and there are numerous off-slope activities for children, such as sledging and ice skating.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski venues include La Taverne and Le Blanchon bars, and La Table de L'Ours restaurant serving hearty local cuisine. The resort also has a swimming pool and daily entertainment.
resortCharacterDescription: Le Sauze is a village resort with two levels: the original Le Sauze at 1,400m and Super Sauze built in the 1960s at 1,700m. It holds 'Station Verte' (Green Resort) status, emphasizing authenticity and natural surroundings. The resort features chalets and traditional farmhouses, with a pedestrian-friendly piste front at Super Sauze.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 21 lifts, including drag lifts and chairlifts, with an uplift capacity of 15,510 skiers per hour. Key lifts include the Télésiège de la Tête de l'Aigle and the Téléski des Clots. Snow-making is available with 25 snow cannons.

terrainDescription: The ski area is divided into four sectors: Tierces, Roselette, Hauteluce, and Montjoie. The Tierces section faces south and is the highest, containing the resort's hardest black runs, Grevettaz and Rebans, along with easier blue and red runs such as Coins and Tierces. The Roselette section is north-facing with runs from blue to black, including the black Olympique, and features steep couloirs off the Aiguille de Roselette for off-piste. The Hauteluce section lies on the other side of the Col du Joly in the Val Joly, offering varied difficulty. The Montjoie section comprises the lower runs. The terrain includes wide open bowls above the treeline, sheltered tree runs lower down winding through alpine farms and fir trees, and forest-lined trails with panoramic mountain views. Most runs face south-east, so conditions soften early in the afternoon.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste potential exists in the steep couloirs of the Aiguille de Roselette and around Aiguille Croche. The Col du Joly and slopes towards the Italian border offer extensive backcountry routes when conditions are right. Local guides recommend hiring a guide from Saint-Gervais or Chamonix for safe exploration. The off-piste opportunities are plentiful between pistes and further afield, and remain reasonably untracked for several days after a snowfall. The resort's proximity to serious mountain terrain provides hidden stashes for advanced skiers, though avalanche terrain requires local knowledge.
valueDescription: Les Contamines-Montjoie offers excellent value with lower accommodation and dining costs compared to nearby mega-resorts. Lift passes are more affordable: a 6-day adult pass for the Les Contamines/Hauteluce area costs €277 (2025-2026 season), while a 1-day adult pass is €54.50. The Evasion Mont-Blanc pass covering 445 km is €318 for 6 days. Self-catering apartments and chalets keep accommodation costs down, and the quality-to-price ratio is appealing for families and couples. Food and drinks on and off the slopes are noted as very good value.
familyDescription: The resort holds the Famille Plus label, indicating facilities designed with families in mind. The village is compact and easy to navigate with a relaxed pace and lack of rowdy après-ski. Dedicated nursery slopes at village level (Les Loyers and Nivorin) are separated from faster traffic, and the Galipette nursery and ESF ski school offer excellent childcare and tuition for children from age four. The Ludo Park provides a playful course for the whole family, and the green play trail 'Les Ecureuils' and Ecureuils Park add fun extras. Most runs end in a central point, making it easy to keep track of children. The gentle terrain close to the base area allows kids to learn without feeling overwhelmed.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Les Contamines is mellow and quiet. Cosy bars like Le Ty Breiz and Le Saxo offer drinks and mountain charm, but the scene is not rowdy. Dining options are hearty and traditional, with standouts like L'O à la Bouche and Le Signal. For those seeking more lively nightlife, Saint-Gervais is reachable by free shuttle bus or a 10-minute drive. The resort is more geared towards sporty people and families, with a few bars and restaurants but not party central.
resortCharacterDescription: Les Contamines-Montjoie is a traditional Savoyard village that has preserved its historic aspect. More than 70% of old farm buildings have been restored in respect of local Savoy architecture, with stone chalets, wooden farmhouses, quiet lanes, and a Baroque church. The village retains its rural charm with horse-drawn sleighs and a strong community feel. It is a picturesque, authentic alpine village rather than a purpose-built resort, offering a peaceful atmosphere away from the mega-resorts.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes 25 lifts: 4 gondolas, 2 detachable chairlifts, 3 fixed-grip chairlifts, 13 drag lifts, and 1 cable car. The system features gondolas and high-speed six-packs that keep things moving efficiently. Lifts are generally new and efficient, though older lifts are slowly being replaced. Queues are rare outside peak weeks, and the initial gondola up can be busy first thing on school holiday weeks. The resort has a capacity of 45,000 persons per hour.
linkedResortsDescription: Les Contamines is part of the Evasion Mont-Blanc ski area, which includes Saint-Gervais, Saint-Nicolas de Véroce, Megève, Combloux, and La Giettaz, offering a total of 445 km of slopes and 108 lifts. However, Les Contamines is not directly connected by skis to all these resorts; the Hauteluce section is linked via the Col du Joly, but access to other resorts like Megève or Saint-Gervais requires a bus or car. The Evasion Mont-Blanc pass provides access to all these areas. Additionally, the resort is close to Chamonix and Saint-Gervais-les-Bains, opening up day-trip possibilities.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Le Semnoz comprises 20 marked pistes: 9 green, 5 blue, and 6 red runs, covering 35.2 km of downhill skiing. A snowpark is available. The terrain ranges from 1,450m to 1,704m altitude, with the longest run being 3 km. From the top, views extend to Mont Blanc, Lake Annecy, and Lake Bourget.
valueDescription: An adult day lift pass costs €16.50, with a 5-day pass at €72.50 and a 6-day pass at €87. Children aged 0-16 pay €13.50 for a day pass, €57.50 for 5 days, and €69 for 6 days. Season passes are €200 for adults and €130 for children. The resort also offers a free ski pass for children under 16 (though child prices are listed).
familyDescription: The resort is designed for families, with a snowpark and a ski school that teaches from age 4. 66% of the runs are green and blue, suitable for beginners. A sledding run of 80 meters with a conveyor belt is available. The resort is located 20 km from Annecy, accessible by bus. Cross-country skiing offers 30 km of trails.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski venues include Le Refuge for drinks and casual dining, and La Grange for a livelier atmosphere. On-site restaurants serve Savoyard dishes such as tartiflette, fondue, and raclette. The Hôtel Les Rochers Blancs offers Savoyard cuisine with views of Mont Blanc and Lake Annecy. An Eco-Bivouac provides campfire meals and guided snowshoe walks.
resortCharacterDescription: Le Semnoz is a functional, purpose-built ski area with a reception centre, located just outside Annecy. It lacks traditional alpine chalet architecture, with modern facilities. The resort is part of the Massif des Bauges Regional Natural Park.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 12 lifts: 1 high-speed six-pack chairlift, 1 quad chairlift, and 10 surface lifts (drag lifts). The lifts provide access to the ski area between 1,450m and 1,704m.

terrainDescription: The ski area spans from 1,550 m to 2,720 m with a vertical drop of 1,170 m. The terrain is north-west facing, which helps preserve snow quality, and consists of wide, undulating slopes that run through larch forests. The upper mountain offers 360° panoramic views of the Écrins massif and Serre-Ponçon lake. The resort has 100 km of pistes: 36 runs in total (9 green, 5 blue, 17 red, 5 black). The lower slopes are wide and gentle for beginners, while the upper sectors feature steeper red and black runs such as Grand Cabane and Myrtilles. The Bois Méan sector (1,800 m) and the historic centre (1,650 m) are connected by fast lifts.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs approximately €44–€44.50, which is considered reasonable for the Southern Alps. The resort offers a range of accommodation from self-catering apartments to hotels, with many ski-in/ski-out options. Parking can be expensive and complicated, with paid parking in some areas. The resort has a special 'Premières Traces' pass for beginners that limits access to lower chairlifts at a reduced price. Overall, the resort is seen as good value for families and intermediate skiers compared to larger Alpine resorts.
familyDescription: Les Orres is certified with the Famille Plus label, indicating strong family-friendly facilities. There are two snow gardens, magic carpets (one free access), and dedicated beginner zones above the Prélongis chairlift. The ski schools (ESF and ESI) offer English-speaking group lessons for children from age four. The resort has three village levels (1550, 1650, 1800) connected by free shuttle buses, making it easy for families to move around. Wide blue runs like Edelweiss are ideal for family skiing, and there are two toboggan areas at 1650 and 1800. The resort also offers childcare for children from 36 months, with 8 hours of care per day.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has 16 restaurants and 16 bars/cafes, a cinema, a bowling alley, and a nightclub. The après-ski scene is relaxed, with sunny terraces and local cuisine, though some reviews note that the resort can be noisy at night due to partygoers. There is an outdoor heated pool for relaxation after skiing. The resort also offers activities such as dog sledding, snowmobiling, and paragliding. The atmosphere is more family-oriented than rowdy, with live music at some bars on weekends.
resortCharacterDescription: Les Orres is a purpose-built resort designed in the 1960s as a ski-in/ski-out village. It consists of three levels: Les Orres 1550, 1650, and 1800. The 1650 sector retains a 1970s architectural style with concrete apartment blocks, while the 1800 sector (Bois Méan) features contemporary wood and stone residences that blend into the natural environment. The resort centre is car-free, with free shuttle buses connecting the sectors. It is set in a larch forest with panoramic views of the Serre-Ponçon lake and the Écrins National Park. The atmosphere is relaxed, family-friendly, and unpretentious.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 17 lifts: 7 chairlifts (including a new 6-seater Pic Vert chairlift installed for the 2025/26 season), 8 drag lifts, and 2 magic carpets. The lifts are modern and efficient, with fast connections between the 1650 and 1800 sectors. Queues are generally manageable except during peak holiday weeks. Some reviews mention that the ridge lifts can be slow. The resort also operates pedestrian lifts for non-skiers to access high-altitude restaurants.
linkedResortsDescription: Les Orres is a standalone ski area not linked to other major resorts. It is within driving distance of nearby resorts such as Crevoux, Vars, and Pra Loup, but there is no interconnected lift system. The resort's ski pass only covers its own slopes.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Lurisia – Monte Pigna offers 30 km of marked runs (35 km per some sources) spread across 15 pistes, with a composition of 27% beginner (blue), 47% intermediate (red), and 27% expert (black) terrain. The slopes wind through larch woods and provide panoramic descents with views of the Cuneo plain, the Matterhorn, and Monte Rosa on clear days. The exposure is predominantly sunny, keeping slopes bathed in light for most of the day. The longest run is 3 km, and the vertical drop is 922 m (base 836 m, summit 1,758 m). The historic cable car from the 1960s serves as the main access, complemented by four surface lifts.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are among the lowest in the Piedmont region: a day pass costs €27–€28 for adults and €22 for children. The resort is small and family-run, with limited on-mountain dining and no large commercial developments, keeping overall costs down. Accommodation is available in nearby Roccaforte Mondovì and at the historic Terme di Lurisia, though self-catering options are limited. The resort appeals to budget-conscious skiers seeking a quiet, uncrowded experience away from mass tourism.
familyDescription: The resort has 8 km of novice slopes (27% of total terrain), providing a safe area for beginners to learn. The sunny exposure and gentle gradients on the lower mountain are suitable for families, and the historic cable car offers a nostalgic ride. However, there is no dedicated ski school, childcare, or children’s entertainment mentioned in the sources. The quiet, low-key atmosphere and proximity to the Terme di Lurisia (spa) may appeal to families seeking a relaxed holiday, but facilities for young children are minimal.
resortCharacterDescription: Lurisia is a traditional, small-scale ski station set in the Ligurian Alps, originally developed around the 1960s. The centrepiece is a historic cable car that gives the resort a nostalgic, old-fashioned character. The village itself is a spa town (Terme di Lurisia) with a handful of hotels and restaurants, including the Baita Monte Pigna mountain hut serving local Piedmontese dishes. The atmosphere is quiet and uncrowded, far from the purpose-built concrete resorts of larger areas. The surrounding landscape is naturalistic, with larch forests and views of the Monte Rosa massif.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 5 lifts: one historic cable car (gondola) and four surface lifts (ski lifts or carpets). The cable car dates from the 1960s and provides access from the base to the upper slopes. The total uplift capacity is modest, reflecting the small scale of the resort. In 2026, the lifts were taken over by a new manager after the previous company’s bankruptcy, ensuring continued operation. The system is not interconnected with any other ski areas, and there are no high-speed detachable chairlifts or modern gondolas.

terrainDescription: The ski area is split into two non-connected sectors: Belvedere and Monte Moro. Belvedere is a lower, tree-lined area with north-east aspect, offering gentle slopes and larch-lined pistes above the mid-station, flattening dramatically below for novices. Monte Moro is a high-altitude, south-facing sector with treeless alpine terrain above the chairlift, providing intermediate and advanced pistes. The Meccia run in Monte Moro offers a 1,700m vertical drop when conditions allow. The terrain is dominated by long, sustained intermediate runs, with 69% of the 32.1km groomed network classified as intermediate. The Belvedere sector has a school camp at the base of the Pecetto chairlift with two conveyor lifts for first-timers.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available in both sectors. In Belvedere, larch forests provide tree skiing opportunities. In Monte Moro, long alpine off-piste descents into larch are possible. The glaciers on the Monte Rosa massif attract seasoned mountain travellers, and guides can take skiers over to Saas Fee in Switzerland via ski touring routes. Backcountry options are extensive but generally for the adventurous, with some of the best alpine descents from Monte Rosa ending in the valley above Macugnaga, though these are often accessed from the adjacent Alagna valley.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are low compared to other Italian resorts: a day ticket for adults costs €30-34, children €22-25, and seniors €28-32. The six-day adult pass is €147. Accommodation in the villages of Staffa and Pecetto is affordable, with a range of hotels and apartments within walking distance of the lifts. The resort is known for being good value, with a ridiculously cheap lift pass price for the vertical drop available.
familyDescription: The Belvedere sector is ideal for families and beginners, with a school camp at the base of the Pecetto chairlift equipped with two conveyor lifts. The Fontanone slope is a 2km easy run from the Alpe Burki mid-station down to the village, suitable for progressing beginners. The slopes are uncrowded, and the village atmosphere is relaxed and safe. The Monte Moro sector also has a few beginner slopes at the top cable car station, but these are only suitable on fine weather days. The resort is considered one of the most family-friendly in Piedmont.
apresSkiDescription: The village of Staffa has a lively centre with a surprising number of restaurants, bars, and a nightlife scene, especially on weekends. There are 7 bars and pubs and 1 club in the resort. Mountain rifugi focus on authentic Piedmontese polenta and local cheeses. The atmosphere is relaxed and traditional, with no high-volume catering. The resort also offers snowshoeing and other non-ski activities.
resortCharacterDescription: Macugnaga is a traditional Walser village with 13th-century stone and wood architecture, located at the foot of the Monte Rosa massif. It consists of two hamlets: Staffa (base of Monte Moro lifts) and Pecetto (base of Belvedere lifts), separated by less than a kilometre. The resort feels like a mountaineering outpost rather than a purpose-built ski station, with a quiet, remote atmosphere. The valley is forested and scenic, and the resort retains a sense of tradition and authenticity.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 9 lifts: 2 cable cars, 3 double chairlifts, 2 T-bars, and 2 surface lifts. The Monte Moro sector is served by a small 15-person cable car from Staffa to Alpe Bill (1,700m) and a larger 38-person cable car from Alpe Bill to Passo Moro (2,806m). The Belvedere sector uses three slow double chairlifts (Pecetto-Burki, Burki-Belvedere, and others) and two T-bars. The lifts are old, with some dating back to the 1950s, and there are no high-speed gondolas or heated seats. The system is well-maintained but slow, contributing to a quiet, uncrowded mountain experience.
linkedResortsDescription: The Monte Moro sector sits on the Swiss border, and from the top of Passo Moro (2,806m) it is possible to ski tour into the Saastal towards Saas Fee, Switzerland. However, there is no direct lift connection; returning to Italy requires skinning or a guided traverse. The resort also has ski touring links to the Monterosa Ski area via the glaciers, but these are not lift-served.

terrainDescription: The ski area is split into two sectors: Belvedere and Monte Moro. Belvedere, accessed by chairlift from Pecetto, offers 550m of vertical on north-east aspect, tree-lined slopes with larch forests, and a flat lower section ideal for novices. Monte Moro, accessed by a 15-person cable car from Staffa to Alpe Bill (1,700m), then a modern cable car and chairlift, provides 1,100m of vertical on sunny south-facing slopes with treeless high alpine terrain above the top chairlift and intermediate to advanced pistes. The Belvedere sector has a school field with two conveyor lifts at the base. Monte Moro has two short surface tows near the top cable car station for beginners on fine days. There is no valley trail from Monte Moro back to the village.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain is available in both sectors. In Belvedere, larch forests provide tree skiing opportunities. In Monte Moro, long alpine off-piste descents into larch forests are possible. Backcountry options are extensive, with guided heli-skiing to various peaks, ski touring, and ski mountaineering available. Mountain guides can take skiers over the Monte Moro Pass into Saas Fee, Switzerland. The area is known for freeride potential, though the most challenging descents from the Monte Rosa massif are typically accessed from the adjacent Alagna valley.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are low compared to major Alpine resorts: a day ticket for adults costs €30–34, for children €22–25, and for seniors €28–32. Six-day passes are around €147. Accommodation in the villages of Staffa and Pecetto is affordable, with a range of hotels and apartments within walking distance of the lifts. Mountain restaurants serve reasonably priced local food. The low lift pass price reflects the older lift infrastructure and limited piste length.
familyDescription: The Belvedere sector is well-suited for beginners and families. It has a dedicated school field at the base with two conveyor lifts, and the Fontanone slope (2 km, easy) runs from the Alpe Burki mid-station down to the village, providing a safe learning environment. The slopes are uncrowded, and the village is quiet. Ski schools are available, and the area offers a snowpark and cross-country ski trails. The Monte Moro sector has limited beginner terrain, only two short surface tows at the top, which are weather-dependent.
apresSkiDescription: The village of Staffa has a lively restaurant, bar, and nightlife scene, particularly on weekends, with 7 bars and pubs and 1 club reported. The atmosphere is relaxed and traditional, with local eateries serving Italian cuisine. The resort is not a party destination but offers a social scene for those who want it. Mountain rifugi provide lunch stops with local food and drinks.
resortCharacterDescription: Macugnaga is a traditional, unpretentious Italian ski resort set in the Valle Anzasca at the foot of Monte Rosa's east face. The commune consists of two villages: Staffa (1,327m) and Pecetto (1,369m), separated by less than a kilometre, with a historic 'dorf' of traditional Walser buildings dating from the 16th century. The resort is not purpose-built; it retains an ancient village character with narrow streets and traditional architecture. The setting is remote and scenic, with views of glaciers and the Himalayan Wall of Monte Rosa.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 9 lifts (2 cable cars, 3 chairlifts, 2 ski lifts, 2 conveyor belts) across two sectors. The infrastructure is old: the cable car from Staffa to Alpe Bill has a capacity of only 15 people and can cause queues. Chairlifts are slow, with no high-speed or heated-seat options. However, all lifts are well-maintained. The Monte Moro sector uses a modern cable car from Alpe Bill to Passo Moro (2,800m) and a chairlift. The Belvedere sector uses two-seater chairlifts. A free skibus connects the two base areas.
linkedResortsDescription: Macugnaga is not part of a large interconnected ski area. The Monte Moro Pass (2,853m) provides a hiking/skinning route to Saas-Almagell in Switzerland, and guided tours can cross into Saas Fee. The resort is separate from the Monterosa Ski area (linking Champoluc, Gressoney, and Alagna), though those resorts are nearby in adjacent valleys. There is no lift connection to other resorts.

terrainDescription: The Kranzberg ski area offers 15 km of marked pistes, with 6 km of easy (blue) runs, 7 km of intermediate (red) runs, and 2 km of advanced (black) runs. The longest descent is a 3 km blue run from the summit to the valley. The only black run descends from the Kranzberg peak (1,350 m) with a vertical drop of 189 m over 2 km. Intermediate terrain is concentrated around the Wildensee T-bars, providing consistent gradients. The ski area is situated on the Kranzberg mountain within the Karwendel and Wetterstein ranges.
valueDescription: A one-day adult lift pass costs €41, a youth pass €39, and a child pass €28. Family rates are available. The Top Snow Card, which is the standard lift pass, includes access to a wider region of 213 km of pistes across Garmisch-Partenkirchen and the Tyrolean Zugspitz Arena, served by 78 lifts. Accommodation in Mittenwald ranges from guesthouses to apartments, with the Alpenwelt Karwendel tourist office listing over 650 hosts.
familyDescription: The Kranzberg ski area is marketed as a family paradise. Bruno's children's ski park at the base of the Luttensee lift features a magic carpet, a kids' lift, a carousel, and a Bruno mobile. The nursery slopes at the Luttensee area are separate from main runs. Two ski schools operate: Erste Skischule Mittenwald and Alpenwelt Karwendel ski school, offering group and private lessons. The Alpenwelt Karwendel ski bus runs free of charge from Mittenwald, Krün, and Wallgau to the lifts for guests with a guest card. Night skiing is available on Tuesdays and Thursdays from 18:30 to 21:30 on the floodlit blue run at Luttensee.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options include the sports bar Kioxx at Wildensee, Peppi's Schiglu (an igloo bar) at the Luttensee parking lot, and the Alpenstüberl and Schwabenhof bars in the village. For dining, Gasthaus zur Post serves traditional Bavarian cuisine. Mittenwald also has the Collage and dance studio Rischon for late-night entertainment. Garmisch-Partenkirchen, 26 km away, offers additional clubs and cinemas.
resortCharacterDescription: Mittenwald is a traditional Bavarian market town with a historic centre, located in the Karwendel mountains. The Kranzberg ski area is on a mountain adjacent to the village, with the base station approximately 2 km from the town centre. The village has a train station with direct connections to Munich, a swimming hall, an ice skating rink, and 60 km of cross-country trails. The resort has been operating for 57 years.
liftSystemDescription: The ski area is served by 7 lifts: one double chairlift and six surface lifts (T-bars), with a total uphill capacity of 5,850 persons per hour. The Luttensee T-bar is floodlit for night skiing on Tuesdays and Thursdays from 18:30 to 21:30. Snowmaking covers 60% of the pistes, with 9 snow cannons. The highest lift reaches 1,350 m.
linkedResortsDescription: The Top Snow Card lift pass provides access to a wider ski region of 213 km of pistes, including Garmisch-Classic and the Tyrolean Zugspitz Arena, served by 78 lifts between 732 m and 2,720 m. Additionally, the resorts of Garmisch-Classic, Zugspitze, Rosshütte, and Gschwandkopf in Seefeld are within 20 km of Mittenwald.

terrainDescription: The ski area comprises 16 pistes of varying difficulty, including a black FIS run (No. 10) starting at the top of the Eissee gondola, a terrain park on Schareck slope No. 3 with waves and a boardercross course, and wide, perfectly groomed upper mountain pistes. The Alteck sector offers two red pistes and one black slope accessed by chairlift. Piste 9 is a blue beginner trail. The resort is used as a training ground by the Austria Ski Team and the German Alpine Ski National Team, and benefits from above-average sunshine hours.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain includes 6.7 km of marked freeride routes, with a highlight being the descent from the east wall of Schareck down to Stübele See lake. The best lift-accessed freeride is below Schareck summit, and a short bootpack from Alteck peak leads to steep terrain. Backcountry descents from the top of the glacier extend toward Gasteinertal and Sportgastein. The off-piste areas can remain untracked for several days after snowfall, and the resort has a freeride rating of 4.7 out of 5.
valueDescription: Children under 6 ski for free. A one-day adult lift pass costs €69, youth (12–17) €52, and children (6–11) €35. Online advance purchase offers discounts. Parking at the resort is free, and free ski buses run from Ankogel and Mallnitz. Accommodation options include the Alpenhotel Badmeister 600 m from the funicular and a youth hostel at 2,800 m on the glacier. The resort also offers family packages and a Gopass app for best-price ski passes.
familyDescription: The Bobo Children's Club operates the Zwergenlöwenland children's area with magic carpet lifts, a wavy run, and a bobsleigh track. Children under 6 ski for free. Ski schools in Flattach and Mallnitz offer English-speaking group lessons. The Ankogel ski area, covered by the same lift pass, has a Kinderland directly at the valley station. Free ski buses connect the villages, and the resort is rated among the 10 most family-friendly in Carinthia.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski starts at the Europabar at the intermediate station (2,234 m) and continues at the MölliBar inside the Gletscher Express building. In Flattach, the Flattacher Hof has a bar in a 500-year-old vault, and the Hütten Wirt offers party atmosphere until early morning. The resort has 10 bars and pubs and 1 club. The last gondola down ends mountain parties relatively early.
resortCharacterDescription: Mölltaler Gletscher is a high-altitude glacier ski area accessed via the Gletscher Express funicular from the valley near Flattach. The village of Flattach is a small, tranquil community 9 km from the glacier base. The ski area sits within the Hohe Tauern National Park, offering panoramic views of over 30 peaks above 3,000 m. The resort is purpose-built for skiing, with on-mountain facilities including the Eissee panorama restaurant at 2,800 m and a youth hostel at the same altitude.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 8 lifts: the Gletscher Express funicular (8-minute ride to the intermediate station at 2,234 m), the Eisseebahn gondola to 2,800 m, the Gletscher Jet 6-seater chairlift to Schareck summit, the Alteck chairlift, the Schwarzkopflift, and the Klühspies 2-seater chairlift. Total lift capacity is 15,610 persons per hour. The funicular is underground, providing reliable access in all weather.
linkedResortsDescription: The Mölltaler Gletscher lift pass is valid at the nearby Ankogel-Mallnitz ski area, which offers 18 km of pistes and is accessible via free ski buses. The glacier is also connected to the wider Hohe Tauern region, with backcountry routes leading toward Sportgastein in Salzburg. The Ankogel mountain railroads provide an alternative ski area with a Kinderland and deep-snow variants.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 30 km of slopes, comprising 4 blue runs, 1 red run, and 1 black run, plus a marked freeride route. The summit slope is known as the 'Fribourg challenge'. A dedicated beginners' area at the village level features mini towbars. Two snowparks cater to beginners and advanced riders. Night skiing is available on selected evenings, and the slopes are served by a panoramic funicular and cable car. The terrain is predominantly gentle, with 70% of runs classified as beginner-friendly, and the resort's south-facing slopes benefit from sun exposure.
valueDescription: A one-day adult lift pass costs 42 CHF, with reduced rates for young people (33 CHF), children (25 CHF), and seniors (35 CHF). Half-day passes are also available. The Magic Pass, which grants access to over 100 resorts including Moléson, offers free skiing here. The resort is located 75 minutes from Geneva Airport, making it accessible for day trips. Accommodation options range from hotels to self-catering apartments, with a free parking area at the base of the lifts.
familyDescription: The resort features a village-level beginners' area with mini towbars, a 4 km toboggan run open day and night, and two snowparks suitable for all ages. A ski school offers lessons for children. The funicular operates until 23:00 on Saturday nights, and all pistes are open for ski touring every Friday evening until 22:30. The traffic-free village centre allows families to walk to the lifts and amenities.
resortCharacterDescription: Moléson-sur-Gruyères is a traditional alpine village situated at 1,132 m at the foot of the Moléson peak. The resort has undergone planned development, with a master plan from 2007–2013 proposing 375 residential units and 1,551 beds, plus an additional 384 beds in an adjacent parcel. The village centre concentrates amenities within walking distance of the ski area. The summit offers a 360° panorama of Lake Geneva, Mont Blanc, and the Swiss Plateau.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 1 funicular, 1 cable car, 1 chairlift, and 5 T-bars, totalling 8 lifts. The funicular runs from the village to Plan-Francey, with the cable car continuing to the summit at 2,002 m. According to the snow report, 3 of 8 lifts were operational in June 2026. The lifts are described as panoramic, and the funicular operates late on Saturday nights for night skiing.
linkedResortsDescription: A 2009 study explored a potential connection between Moléson-sur-Gruyères and the neighbouring resorts of Rathvel and Les Paccots. The proposed connection would add 32 ski pistes and 2 skiways, covering 80.8 hectares with a combined slope length of 31.6 km, supporting up to 5,320 skiers. As of the latest sources, this connection has not been implemented.

terrainDescription: The resort has 6 km of pistes across 9 slopes: 5 red and 4 green. The longest red run is 2,265 meters and offers views of the Adriatic Sea, which is 30 km away. The lower slopes are gentle and suitable for beginners, while the upper slopes provide more challenging terrain. The altitude ranges from 1,429 m to 1,649 m. Snowmaking covers the slopes to supplement natural snow.
familyDescription: The resort has a nursery slope at San Giacomo with a ski school and equipment rental. There are 4 green runs ideal for beginners. The lower slopes are described as suitable for first-time skiers. The resort also has a sledding area and a snow park for children.
resortCharacterDescription: Monte Piselli is a small ski area located on the northern slope of Montagna dei Fiori, within the Gran Sasso e Monti della Laga National Park, near the border between Marche and Abruzzo. The resort consists of a chairlift, a ski lift, and two treadmills serving 9 slopes. The village of Valle Castellana is the nearest settlement. The resort has been closed in recent seasons due to lack of maintenance and snow, with plans for a new cabinovia lift.
liftSystemDescription: The current lift system includes one chairlift, one ski lift, and two treadmills. These lifts are at the end of their operational life, having received no maintenance for 15 years. The resort has been closed for several seasons. A new cabinovia (gondola) from San Giacomo to Rifugio Pizi with an intermediate station at Tre Caciare is planned, with 12 million euros in funding allocated, but completion is not expected before 2027.

terrainDescription: The terrain consists entirely of gentle blue and red runs, with no black pistes. Slopes are wide and well-groomed, suitable for beginners and intermediates. The longest run is 3 km. The ski area has 6 km of pistes, with a mix of beginner (55%) and intermediate (45%) terrain.
valueDescription: Monte Popolo is a low-priced ski area with affordable day tickets. Family deals are particularly attractive: 2 hours for 1 adult with 2 children costs €68, and for 2 adults with 1 child from €85. The resort is part of Ski Amadé, so multi-day passes give access to 760 km of slopes. Free parking at the valley station and a free ski bus reduce additional costs.
familyDescription: The resort is purpose-built for families, with a dedicated Kinderland featuring a magic carpet, animal figures, and an adventure children's slope with talking forest animal figures. Monti's Funpark includes a ski movie giant slalom, rails, boxes, and a wave track. The Andrea Fischbacher timed run with photo point adds excitement. The 6-person chairlift has child safety bars. The ski school Eben offers group and private lessons for children and adults. Free ski bus connects to the slopes.
resortCharacterDescription: Eben im Pongau is a small, idyllic village located directly on the Tauern motorway, with a noise barrier minimizing traffic noise. The resort has a traditional Austrian character with rustic huts and family-run hotels. It is not a purpose-built resort but a genuine alpine village, offering a peaceful and authentic atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 4 lifts: a modern 6-person high-speed chairlift (Popolo II) with weather protection hood and child safety bars, a 2-person chairlift (Popolo I), and two surface lifts. The lifts are modern and efficient, with a capacity of 4000 persons per hour. Queues are rarely an issue due to the small size of the resort.
linkedResortsDescription: Monte Popolo is part of the Ski Amadé network, which includes over 760 km of slopes across multiple resorts. A free ski bus connects Eben to Snow Space Salzburg in Flachau, providing access to additional terrain. Multi-day Ski Amadé passes are valid across all participating resorts.

terrainDescription: The terrain is dominated by wide, well-groomed pistes spread across a broad ridge near the Italian border. The ski area is divided into five main sectors: Millennium/Nassfeld, Tressdorfer Höhe, Gartnerkofel, Madritsche, and Watschinger Alm. Most runs are long blue and red descents, with the longest being the 7.6 km Carnia run from the summit to Tröpolach. The north-facing runs tend to hold snow well, while the Italian-facing slopes offer sunnier conditions. The resort has 110 km of marked pistes, with 30 lifts providing access.
