The slopes include wide, sun-drenched cruisers on the Fraiteve side, such as the long top-to-bottom blue piste 26, and steeper challenges on the Banchetta side, including the Olympic Kandahar Banchetta Giovanni Nasi black run, which drops 1,000 vertical metres from 2,800m to 1,800m with a maximum gradient over 60%. The resort has 12 black slopes, 30 red runs, and 24 beginner slopes, with dedicated baby slopes at the foot of the Kandahar Giovanni Alberto Agnelli run, set in a natural amphitheatre. The Pattemouche red run descends through forest toward Pragelato, while the Sises blue piste offers gentle terrain for improving skiers. The local ski area covers 80–114 km of marked pistes, with 82 marked runs in total.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing in Sestriere is limited but rewarding with a guide. The north-facing slopes off Monte Fraiteve hold powder longest after a storm, as most visitors ski the sunnier aspects first. The less-tracked terrain between pistes offers powder hunting opportunities after fresh snowfall, particularly in the quieter corners of the Via Lattea. The resort's high altitude (village at 2,035m) extends the touring season, and the backcountry terrain around Mount Motta and Banchetta provides additional routes. However, the off-piste is not extensive compared to dedicated freeride resorts, and avalanche risk requires local knowledge.
valueDescription: Sestriere offers good value compared to many French resorts, with lift pass prices in the 2025/26 season ranging from €53 (low season adult 1-day) to €58.50 (high season adult 1-day), and a 6-day pass from €268 low season to €307 high season. Midweek stays provide excellent value as the resort quietens down, and early December and late March fall outside peak pricing windows. Accommodation is predominantly ski-in/ski-out, with many apartments and hotels offering convenient slope access. Mountain restaurants are reasonably priced, with Italian cuisine like pizza and pasta available at affordable rates. The resort's connection to the 400km Via Lattea area adds terrain value without a significant premium.
familyDescription: Sestriere is well-suited for families, with dedicated baby slopes at the foot of the Kandahar Giovanni Alberto Agnelli run, set in a natural amphitheatre where parents can watch from close by. The Anfiteatro area offers gentle, contained slopes perfect for young children, with a warming hut nearby. Ski schools run children's programmes from age four, with small group sizes and English-speaking instructors available. The compact village is easy to navigate on foot, and many accommodations are ski-in/ski-out. Off-slope activities include ice skating, dog sledding, snowmobiles, a sports centre, and a swimming pool. Childcare is provided by private services and selected hotels rather than a single resort-run centre.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Sestriere ranges from relaxed to lively. Energetic venues include Pinky bar and Tabata Disco, while GSK is known for a wild youth scene. The Igloo offers all-day food, entertainment, and good beer choices, and Gargote Fashion Café is popular for banging après-ski. However, the overall scene is more chilled than in nearby Sauze d'Oulx, with limited live music early in the season. Slope-side bars at the base of lifts provide convenient post-ski drinks. Dining options include traditional Alpine flavours at Chalet Raggio del Sole and contemporary Italian at I.Gloo Sestriere, as well as pizza and pasta at numerous mountain restaurants.
resortCharacterDescription: Sestriere is a purpose-built ski resort, developed in the 1930s by the Agnelli family (of Fiat fame) as one of the world's first such resorts. The village is dominated by two iconic hotel towers, designed by Vittorio Bonadè Bottino, which were the first buildings in the village and later served as part of the Olympic Village for the 2006 Winter Olympics. The resort is practical and convenient, with most accommodation offering ski-in/ski-out access, but it lacks traditional alpine charm. The architecture is functional, with concrete apartment blocks and modern facilities. The village centre is compact, with services and lifts within easy walking distance.
liftSystemDescription: Sestriere has 19–20 local ski lifts, including high-speed quads and gondolas, serving terrain from 1,357m to 2,840m. The lift system was modernised for the 2006 Winter Olympics, but some older chairlifts remain without covers or heating, leading to slow rides and potential bottlenecks at peak times. The resort has 69% of pistes covered by artificial snowmaking, and night skiing is available on a floodlit run. The wider Via Lattea area adds 70 lifts total, connecting to neighbouring resorts. Lift queues can be long during weekends and school holidays, particularly when high winds close the links to Sauze d'Oulx.
linkedResortsDescription: Sestriere is the central hub of the Via Lattea (Milky Way) ski area, which connects six resorts: Pragelato, Claviere, Sauze d'Oulx, Cesana Torinese/Sansicario, and Montgenèvre in France. The linked area offers over 400 km of skiable pistes served by 70 lifts, with a single ski pass. The connection to Montgenèvre allows skiing across the French border. The Pattemouche-Anfiteatro cableway, built in 2006, links Sestriere to Pragelato. The Via Lattea also includes the resorts of Sauze d'Oulx (known for lively après-ski), Sansicario (family-friendly), and Claviere (small and quiet). The highest point in the area is Monte Motta at 2,800m, and the lowest lift base is at 1,372m in Cesana Torinese.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 28 km of groomed pistes, with 68% easy (blue) and 25% intermediate (red) runs, plus 12 km of ski routes. Most runs are wide, scenic forest trails under the tree line. The longest run is the 5.5 km valley run to Flachauwinkl with a 980 m vertical drop. A 19 km Family Run crosses the entire area with 19 themed stations. A permanent speed skiing track is located on the Powder Shuttle slope. The resort also has a snow park (Absolut Park) with over 100 obstacles, a halfpipe, and separate areas for beginners, advanced, and pros.
offPisteDescription: Ski routes are spread throughout the ski area, with the Freeride Trail beneath the Powder Shuttle particularly recommended. There are several natural freeride routes, and powder zones exist in steeper sections and off-piste trails. The resort has 12 km of marked ski routes, and advanced riders can find steeper sections and fun off-piste trails in the trees.
valueDescription: A one-day adult lift pass costs €79 (2025/26 season), with youth at €59.50 and children at €39.50. Six-day passes are available for €370.50. The resort is part of the Ski amadé network, so multi-day passes grant access to 760 km of slopes across 5 top ski areas. Free ski buses run at short intervals between Flachauwinkl and Kleinarl. The resort has its own motorway exit (A10 Tauernautobahn), reducing transfer costs.
familyDescription: The resort is very well suited for families. At both valley stations, separate practice areas with magic carpets, snow carousels, and wave tracks are available. In Kleinarl, Pingo's Winter World offers a magic carpet, wave track, snow carousel, playground, and full-day supervision. The Flora Meadow practice area has two magic carpets and a snow carousel. The Family Run is a 19 km fun trail with 19 colorful Crazy Shuttleberg Dolls stations, a scavenger hunt, and weekly events. The Sun House at the Family Shuttle valley station features a drawing corner, comic cinema, sun terrace, and boot drying room. Child-friendly chairlifts with flat exits make it easy for youngsters to load.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Flachauwinkl and Kleinarl is friendly and laid-back, focused on relaxing after skiing. The Chill House at the bottom of the Absolut Park is a popular meeting place for snowboarders and freeskiers, offering music, videos, bouldering, and a mini ramp. Traditional mountain huts like Gasthof Walchau, Frauenalm, Hubertusalm, and Winklalm serve regional food and drinks. The Lumberjack restaurant at the bottom of the Lumberjack Shuttle is a food hall with organic meals and food trucks.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort consists of two small traditional villages: Kleinarl (population 792, elevation 1,014 m) and Flachauwinkl (elevation 989 m). Kleinarl has a Gothic St. Lawrence parish church consecrated in 1443 and a former hunting lodge of the Prince-Archbishops of Salzburg. The ski area itself is modern, with chairlifts and gondolas, and is accessed directly from the A10 Tauernautobahn motorway exit in Flachauwinkl. The villages retain an alpine character with traditional architecture.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 9 lifts, including modern chairlifts and gondolas. All chairlifts have flat exits suitable for children. The Panorama Link, a ten-seater gondola opened in December 2020, connects the resort to Wagrain and Zauchensee in nine minutes. Each lift serves at least two well-groomed runs. The resort has a capacity of 20,400 persons per hour. Lifts operate from 08:45 to 16:00.
linkedResortsDescription: The Shuttleberg Flachauwinkl-Kleinarl is connected via the Panorama Link gondola (opened December 2020) to the Snow Space Salzburg ski area (Flachau, Wagrain, St. Johann-Alpendorf) and the Zauchensee-Flachauwinkl ski area, creating a total of 210 km of interconnected pistes. It is also part of the Ski amadé network, Austria's largest ski association, which includes 5 top ski areas (Salzburger Sportwelt, Schladming-Dachstein, Gastein, Hochkönig, Grossarltal) with 270 lifts and 760 km of groomed slopes accessible on one ski pass.

terrainDescription: The ski area is on the Thurntaler mountain, with a vertical drop of about 1,300 m from the summit at 2,407 m to the valley at 1,100 m. The main runs are long, groomed trails, including a 3,400-foot vertical descent from the top of the two-stage gondola back to the base. The upper mountain features wide, open snowfields between trails, and the terrain is predominantly intermediate, with a sunny southeast-facing aspect. Two valley runs lead down to Sillian and Außervillgraten. A snowpark with a 130 m wave track, 240 m ski cross, and various obstacles is located at 2,200 m on a southeast-facing slope.
offPisteDescription: Open snowfields between the groomed trails offer accessible off-piste skiing, with good snow cover in the upper areas. For more adventurous terrain, guided routes are recommended, particularly to the Hochalm and other unmarked ski routes. The ski area is known for its freeride terrain, and the sunny aspect means conditions can soften quickly, so early starts are advisable.
valueDescription: Lift passes are priced at €64–70 for a day ticket for adults, with children (6–18 years) receiving a 50% discount and children under 6 skiing free. The Ski)Hit tariff offers further family discounts: for large families, only two adults and the two oldest children (up to 17.9 years) pay, while all additional children ski for free. The resort is noted for being more affordable than many better-known Alpine destinations, with a six-day adult pass at €362 and a child six-day pass at €181.
familyDescription: The resort is certified as family-friendly by the Tirol Tourist Board. The Bobo Club Hochpustertal provides a dedicated children's area with a 40 m conveyor belt, igloo, tunnel, carousel, wave run, and special children's lifts. The ski school offers child-oriented lessons for ages 3.5 and up, with a weekly final race and medals. Childcare is available for children from 24 months, and the Dolomiten Residenz Sporthotel offers professional childcare through the 'Häppi Päpi' program. The nursery slopes are separate from faster traffic, and the village is compact and walkable.
apresSkiDescription: The apres-ski scene is quiet and relaxed, with a few bars and restaurants in the village. The Acquarena wellness centre, opened in 2018, offers thermal pools, saunas, and a spa. Mountain huts such as the Pistentreff serve drinks and food. There is no disco or large nightlife venue; the atmosphere is focused on family and outdoor activities.
resortCharacterDescription: Sillian is a traditional Tyrolean market town at 1,100 m, with preserved architecture featuring carved wooden balconies and decorated facades. The old town centre retains its historic charm, and the village is surrounded by the Lienz Dolomites and the Carnic Alps. It is not a purpose-built resort; the infrastructure blends modern amenities with alpine character. The village has about 2,100 inhabitants and 1,200 guest beds.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 7 lifts: 2 cable cars (gondolas), 1 six-seater chairlift with a weather protection hood and seat heating, 2 four-seater chairlifts, 1 T-bar, and 1 ski school lift. The main gondola from Sillian is a two-stage lift ascending 1,000 m vertical. The six-pack chairlift serves the upper mountain. The total lift capacity is 14,390 persons per hour. Snowmaking covers 95% of the pistes, ensuring reliable conditions.
linkedResortsDescription: The Sillian-Hochpustertal ski area is a single connected mountain with two access points: from Sillian and from Ausservillgraten. The slopes on the Thurntaler are served by lifts from both sides, and the valley runs descend to both villages. The Ski)Hit pass provides access to other East Tyrol ski areas, including Großglockner Resort Kals-Matrei, St. Jakob im Defereggental, and the Mölltaler Glacier, though these are not physically linked by lifts.

terrainDescription: The ski area has two distinct sides. The north-facing slopes above Moena feature mainly red and black runs, including the steep Piavac descent with a notorious initial drop and the Canalone Tomba slope used by Alberto Tomba for training. The south-facing area towards Bellamonte is sunny, wide and gentle, with blue and red runs such as the Zirmes run offering views of the Pale di San Martino. The south side is described as a realm for families and first-timers.
offPisteDescription: The Col Margherita area (2,513 m) includes a Freeride Park for challenging off-piste terrain. The north face of Alpe Lusia, with its steep black runs like Piavac and Canalone Tomba, also offers natural off-piste opportunities for experienced skiers. A snowpark and boardercross track are available for freestyle enthusiasts.
valueDescription: Daily lift passes start at around €50 for adults and €35 for children. A 6-day Tre Valli ski pass costs €254 for adults in low season and €197 for children. The resort is part of the Dolomiti Superski network, which offers a single pass for 1,200 km of slopes across 12 areas. Accommodation in nearby villages like Moena and Predazzo is reasonably priced, with self-catering apartments and family-run hotels available.
familyDescription: The south-facing Bellamonte side is specifically designed for families, with wide, sunny nursery slopes, a snow playground, and indoor heated areas for children. The Fraina sledding track (2.1 km) winds through the forest. Themed play areas Giro d'Ali and Frainus are located near the Bellamonte 3.0 gondola. Ski schools offer qualified children's instructors, and the village of Moena is pedestrian-friendly with a relaxed atmosphere.
apresSkiDescription: The resort offers cosy mountain refuges and restaurants, with après-ski and lounge bars at the base areas. Moena hosts the Moena Vintage Ski Revival in late March, featuring non-competitive races on period gear and free concerts at mountain huts. The overall nightlife is relaxed rather than rowdy, with a focus on dining and socialising in the village.
resortCharacterDescription: Moena, known as the 'Fairy of the Dolomites', is a traditional alpine town with historic streets, stone fountains, and wooden barns (tabià) decorated with floral arrangements. It is part of the Alpine Pearls network for sustainable tourism. The ski area itself is not a single purpose-built resort but a collection of villages (Moena, Bellamonte, Passo San Pellegrino) set within the UNESCO-listed Dolomites.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of modern gondolas and covered chairlifts. Key lifts include the Ronchi-Valbona 8-person gondola, Valbona-Le Cune 8-person gondola, Piavac 4-person chairlift with canopy, Campo-Le Cune 4-person chairlift with canopy, Campo-Lastè 4-person chairlift with canopy, Castelir-Fassane 10-person gondola, and Fassane-Morea 10-person gondola. The lifts operate from 08:30 to 16:15 daily.
linkedResortsDescription: Alpe Lusia is part of the Alpe Lusia/San Pellegrino ski area, which includes Moena, Bellamonte, Passo San Pellegrino, and Falcade, offering 100 km of slopes. A free ski shuttle (Skitour Tre Valli) connects Alpe Lusia with San Pellegrino. The resort is also part of the Dolomiti Superski network, granting access to 1,200 km of pistes across 12 ski areas, including the Sellaronda circuit and links to Val di Fiemme/Obereggen via the Fiemme-Obereggen ski pass.

terrainDescription: The ski area features wide, clear slopes that are fully artificially snowed and lit. The longest run is 3.2 km. The terrain is predominantly gentle, with a mix of beginner, intermediate, and advanced runs. The slopes are well-maintained and offer good visibility.
valueDescription: The resort offers free parking at both base stations (Pstruží and near Abertamy). Lift passes are affordable compared to Alpine resorts, making it a budget-friendly option. Ski equipment rental and ski school are available on-site.
familyDescription: The resort has one of the largest children's ski parks in the Czech Republic, equipped with four moving carpet lifts (83m, 42m, 40m, 30m), a children's ski carousel, and snow-tubing. A ski school is available, and there are sledding tracks for families. The children's centre is located near the slopes.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options include the Apres Ski bar U Dostyho on the slope, the Skibar on another slope, and the mountain-top restaurant Plešivec with panoramic views. Additional dining is available at the bistro Stodola and restaurant Fabrika Art in the Zelené Údolí chalet.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is a modern, purpose-built ski area in the Ore Mountains, with a summit at 1,028 m featuring a lookout tower, hotel, and restaurant. The base stations are at Pstruží and near Abertamy, with free parking. It is one of the newest ski resorts in the Czech Republic, using modern technology for snowmaking and lifts.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of three four-seater cable cars (including two express detachable chairs) and six surface lifts, for a total of nine lifts. The cable cars have a high transport capacity of up to 2,400 people per hour each, minimizing queues. The slopes are fully artificially snowed and lit.

terrainDescription: The ski area covers 72km of pistes across 52 runs, with 56% graded red and 8% black, dropping through open alpine meadows and dense forest beneath Monte Civetta's 3,220m rock face; the terrain is linked village-to-village from Alleghe through Selva di Cadore to Val di Zoldo, with the north-facing Val di Zoldo sector holding cold snow and firm conditions into the afternoon after storms, while the Alleghe-facing slopes soften earlier; Gran Zuita descends 1,100m from Col Fioret (2,082m) to Alleghe Lake (1,000m) across three linked slopes with 360-degree Dolomite panoramas including views of the Sella Group, Marmolada, and Croda da Lago.
offPisteDescription: Forest terrain between marked runs holds snow well after storms and provides accessible tree skiing, with the north-facing Pelmo flanks offering powder stashes; above the treeline, steeper bowls and gullies reward exploration when conditions are right, while Monte Civetta's flanks present challenging touring routes for experienced mountaineers; the Marmolada glacier and surrounding Sella and Tofana groups provide high-alpine terrain accessible on the Dolomiti Superski pass, with the Marmolada summit descent being one of the most celebrated in the range; freeriding is prohibited in the resort itself and a guide is recommended for all off-piste exploration.
valueDescription: Day lift passes cost €67 for adults (€62-67 depending on pre-purchase), with children paying €22-47 and seniors €47-61; food and drink prices are described as very reasonable, with Rifugio Palafavera noted for plentiful, cheap, and delicious cuisine; accommodation is mostly family-run hotels and holiday flats that are budget-friendly; night skiing from the Pecol gondola provides additional value; however, parking fees apply in Alleghe.
familyDescription: Nursery slopes with surface lifts exist at each base village, with Palafavera's being the gentlest and most sheltered, though there are no dedicated green-graded runs; the ski area is described as ideal for families with gentle progression terrain for returning beginners, quiet slopes with less pressure from faster skiers, and a family-friendly snowpark at Col dei Baldi (1,470m) with kickers, rails, and boxes for children and beginners; Alleghe has a population of around 1,100, offering a minimarket, several hotels, and an ice rink, with ski buses connecting to other areas twice hourly.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is described as quite relaxed with a rating of 3 out of 5 stars; the only true umbrella bar is Fontanabona, located directly on the Coldai slope at 1,497m, requiring departure before 4:30pm to catch the last lift to the valley; additional bars include Bar at Crep di Pecol and Baita Ru on the valley run to Pecol, with cozy atmosphere at valley stations to end the ski day; evening options are limited with no party bars, though La Botte and Bar Pizzeria Al Sole offer local cuisine and nightlife.
resortCharacterDescription: Alleghe is a traditional mountain village of around 1,100 residents sitting on the shore of Lago di Alleghe, an alpine lake created by a landslide in 1771, with Monte Civetta's thousand-metre rock face reflected in the water; Selva di Cadore is a collective of Ladin-speaking villages in the Val Fiorentina preserving the ancient Dolomite language and Veneto culinary traditions; Val di Zoldo remains less developed for tourism than other UNESCO Dolomites areas, offering authentic historically-rich villages; the overall feel is described as a place tourism hasn't yet reshaped, with the quieter and more authentic side of the Dolomites compared to the more developed South Tyrolean resorts to the north.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 23 lifts including 3 gondolas and 10 chairlifts with 10 surface drags, with six high-speed quads on the main sectors; lift capacity is 34,010 people per hour with opening hours 08:30-16:30; the Alleghe-Civetta gondola and Zoldo Valley lifts service the main verticals; the system links Alleghe, Selva di Cadore, Palafavera, and Val di Zoldo, enabling village-to-village circuit skiing; 98% of slopes are covered by a modern snowmaking system with 285 snow cannons and 14 snowcats maintaining conditions.
linkedResortsDescription: Civetta is part of the Dolomiti Superski network, one of 12 ski areas comprising 1,200km of pistes across the Dolomites; the ski area itself links Alleghe, Selva di Cadore, Palafavera, and Val di Zoldo with ski bus connections to Marmolada glacier, Cortina d'Ampezzo, and the famous Sellaronda ski circuit (accessible by car in about an hour); the area is a major link in the Grande Guerra (Great War) ski tour, a First World War history route; day trips to Arabba, Alta Badia, and Val Gardena are feasible on the Dolomiti Superski pass.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 46 km of slopes, with 54% beginner (wide blue runs), 32% intermediate (red runs winding through forests and open alpine areas), and 14% advanced (including the Grubhörndl black run and Hochwena-Abfahrt). The Family Express chairlift serves the main learning zone, and the Lofi Funline provides a small snow park with waves, jumps, and banked turns. The longest run is 9 km. Snowmaking covers 36 km of slopes, and 90 snow machines with a 135,000 m³ water reservoir support snow reliability.
valueDescription: Day tickets cost €62 for adults, €49.50 for youth, and €31 for children. Family deals include a one-day ticket for 2 adults and 1 child at €143.50 in high season, and a 6-day family pass at €737.50. Children up to 18 ski free on Saturdays when accompanied by two paying parents. A season ticket valid across the 3 Länder Freizeit-Arena (covering Lofer, Steinplatte/Waidring, Winkelmoos, St. Johann in Tirol, Pillersee/Buchensteinwand, Unken/Heutal, Kirchdorf, and Erpfendorf) is available. Self-catering apartments and family-run hotels keep accommodation costs moderate.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as a family paradise. The children's area features a 140-meter-long covered conveyor belt for all-weather comfort. The Family Express chairlift serves gentle blue runs ideal for learning. Two child-safe 8-seater chairlifts are in operation. Ski schools offer English-speaking instructors. The village is compact and walkable, with a ski bus to the slopes. The main road is in a tunnel, keeping the village quiet. Accommodation includes family-run hotels and apartments.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is relaxed and family-oriented, with hotel bars and local pubs such as the Dankl. Mountain huts like the Kechtalm serve traditional Austrian dishes. There is no chaotic nightlife; the scene is quiet and authentic.
resortCharacterDescription: Lofer is a traditional market town in the Saalach valley, with a historic parish church and typical Tyrolean architecture. The village is compact and quiet, as the main road bypasses it through a tunnel. Accommodation is in family-run hotels and apartments. The resort is not purpose-built; it has a working village character.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 10 lifts: 2 gondolas (Almbahn I and II), 2 eight-person chairlifts (including the SenderExpress and Family Express), 1 six-person chairlift, and 5 surface lifts. Three chairlifts have weather shields and heated seats. The gondolas are modern and efficient. The system is well-maintained and queues are rare outside peak periods.
linkedResortsDescription: The ski area is linked with the Heutal lifts in Unken, forming the 'Almenwelt Lofer & Heutal' region. A season ticket (3 Länder Freizeit-Arena) also covers Steinplatte/Waidring, Winkelmoos, St. Johann in Tirol, Pillersee/Buchensteinwand, Kirchdorf, and Erpfendorf. However, the main ski area is a small family resort with a connection to Unken.

terrainDescription: The ski area is served by two chairlifts (Stafelalpe and Glatthorn) reaching 2,000 m, plus four surface lifts including the Guggernülli lift. The main runs are blue and red, with three black ski routes (Hahnenkopf South, East, North) starting from the top of the Stafelalpe lift. The valley area has practice lifts and a ski kindergarten. Night skiing is offered every Thursday from 18:00 to 21:00 on two illuminated blue runs at the Guggernülli lift. The terrain is predominantly gentle, suitable for beginners and intermediates, with a few challenging routes for advanced skiers.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs €56.50 for adults (2025/26 season), with a six-day pass at €226. The resort is small and less expensive than larger nearby areas like Damüls-Mellau. Accommodation includes hotels and apartments directly on the slopes, with 7 hotels and 415 tourist beds. A 3-valley super ski pass is available, covering Faschina, Damüls-Mellau, and Lechtal, offering access to a much larger ski area for a combined price.
familyDescription: The resort is family-oriented with a ski kindergarten and a miniclub for children from age three. The Ski and Snowboard School Faschina-Raggal offers lessons for children and beginners. Practice lifts, a magic carpet, and a children's carousel are located in the valley area. The ski school provides certified instructors who take time with children. The quiet atmosphere and traffic-free village make it suitable for families.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is quiet, with a few mountain huts such as Franz-Josef-Hütte and Café Lari Fari offering drinks and local cuisine. For livelier nightlife, visitors take the ski bus to Damüls, which has several bars and pubs. The resort has 5 bars and pubs in total, but the scene is subdued and focused on relaxation.
resortCharacterDescription: Fontanella is a picturesque small village in the Grosses Walsertal, with traditional Walser architecture. The ski lifts start directly in the hamlet of Faschina at 1,500 m, where hotels and restaurants are located along the slopes. The atmosphere is quiet and family-friendly, with a compact layout that allows easy access to lifts and accommodation.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 6 lifts: two chairlifts (Stafelalpe and Glatthorn) and four surface lifts, including the Guggernülli lift which operates for night skiing on Thursdays. The lifts have a combined capacity of 4,700 persons per hour. Lifts operate from 09:00 to 16:00 daily, with night skiing from 18:00 to 21:00 on Thursdays during the season. The lifts are located directly in the village, providing ski-in/ski-out access for many accommodations.
linkedResortsDescription: Faschina is part of the larger Damüls-Mellau-Faschina ski area, which offers 109 km of pistes and 27 lifts. Although there is no direct lift connection, a regular ski bus runs between Faschina and Damüls. A 3-valley super ski pass is available, covering the Great Walser Valley, Bregenzerwald, and Lechtal, providing access to a combined ski area of over 150 km of slopes.

terrainDescription: The ski area combines three mountains: Walmendingerhorn, Ifen, and Heuberg. Walmendingerhorn offers 15 km of groomed pistes suited to pleasure skiers, with a glass panoramic lift and a viewing platform. Heuberg is known as the family mountain, with over 20 km of easy, wide slopes ideal for beginners and children. Ifen provides over 25 km of varied terrain, including mostly easy pistes for families and beginners, plus challenging black runs such as the Olympiahang (1 km, 320 m vertical drop) and the Kellerloch. Three valley runs are available at Ifen. The Olympiahang is a black run used for the Olympic slope. The terrain is diverse, with 59% beginner, 30% intermediate, and 11% advanced runs according to the resort's own classification.
offPisteDescription: The Walmendingerhorn is a favourite among locals for off-piste skiing, with deep snow conditions when storms approach from the north. The Ifen area also receives significant snowfall, often recording one of the deepest alpine snowpacks in the Alps. The Gottesacker plateau on Ifen features snow dunes that are popular for winter hiking and snowshoeing, but also offer off-piste opportunities. The region's geographic location on the northern edge of the Alps brings heavy snowfall, making it a reliable destination for powder skiing.
valueDescription: A one-day adult lift pass costs €68.70, with youth passes at €52.90 and child passes at €29. The combined ski area lift pass for the Oberstdorf-Kleinwalsertal region is reasonably priced, and the Indy Pass is also valid here. The MyMountainClub Card offers discounts on lift tickets and at partner restaurants, with points that can be redeemed for further savings. Parking at the valley stations is free at Heuberg and costs €5 per day at some locations. The resort is accessible toll-free from Germany via the A7 and B19 motorways.
familyDescription: Heuberg Arena is specifically designed for families, with the Burmi Kinderland featuring mascots and child-friendly features, a ski kindergarten, a children's carpet lift, and a children's restaurant. The Heuberg slopes are wide and easy, with over 20 km of beginner terrain, and floodlit skiing is available at the Hubertuslift on certain evenings. The Ifen area also has predominantly easy pistes suitable for families. The ski school offers lessons for children, and the three villages (Mittelberg, Riezlern, Hirschegg) are connected by a free ski bus, making it easy to access the different areas. The resort is car-free in the sense that the villages are walkable and the ski bus is comprehensive.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is quiet and cosy, with small bars and huts offering drinks under the Hoher Ifen. There are three bars and pubs in the area. For livelier nightlife, visitors need to travel to larger towns in the surroundings. The mountain restaurants, such as Tafel&Zunder at Ifen and Gipfelstuba at Walmendingerhorn, offer evening dining with regional cuisine, but the focus is on relaxed end-of-day drinks rather than partying.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is set in the Kleinwalsertal valley, a traditional Austrian region accessible only from Germany. The villages of Mittelberg (1,250 m), Riezlern, and Hirschegg have alpine charm with wooden chalets and hotels. The ski infrastructure is modern, with the glass panoramic lift at Walmendingerhorn, the new 10-person gondolas Ifen I and Ifen II, and the 6-person Olympiabahn chairlift with weather protection and seat heating. The Heuberg area has surface lifts and a floodlit lift. The valley is not purpose-built but has grown organically, with a mix of traditional architecture and modern lift facilities.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 15 lifts (per known facts) across the three areas, though the combined region has 48 lifts. At Ifen, the modern 10-person gondolas Ifen I and Ifen II and the 6-person Olympiabahn chairlift with weather protection and seat heating provide comfortable access. Walmendingerhorn features a glass panoramic lift that takes skiers from the chairlift to the restaurant. Heuberg has surface lifts and a floodlit lift (Hubertuslift). The lifts operate from 8:30 to 16:30 daily, with last descents between 15:30 and 17:00 depending on the lift. Snowmaking is present, with new cannons installed at Ifen.
linkedResortsDescription: The Walmendingerhorn, Ifen, and Heuberg ski areas are combined under a single lift pass and connected by a free ski bus, forming the largest ski area in Kleinwalsertal with 67 km of pistes. They are part of the larger two-country ski region Oberstdorf Kleinwalsertal, which also includes the German resorts of Nebelhorn, Fellhorn, Söllereck, and Kanzelwand, offering a total of 130 km of slopes and 48 lifts. The Indy Pass is valid in the region. A single ticket grants access to all seven mountains across the border.

terrainDescription: The Ifen ski area offers predominantly easy and intermediate terrain, with 12.6 km of blue runs, 6 km of red runs, and 2 km of black runs. The Gottesacker plateau area provides gentle, wide slopes suitable for beginners and families. Advanced skiers can tackle the 3.5 km black Olympia run, which drops 750 vertical meters, or the steep Kellerloch run. Three valley runs allow long descents back to the base. Most slopes face east, catching morning sun and holding good snow conditions early in the day.
valueDescription: A 6-day adult lift pass costs €342.80 in low season and €360.80 in high season, with children's passes at €90.00 and free entry for children under 5. The Oberstdorf Kleinwalsertal ski pass covers all three areas (Ifen, Heuberg, Walmendingerhorn) and the wider 2-country region. Parking at the valley station costs €5 per day. Student season passes are available for €237. The Indy Pass is also valid in the region.
familyDescription: The Ifen ski area has a dedicated beginner zone at the Gaisbühllift, featuring a magic carpet and the Burmi children's world with a mascot. The slopes are predominantly easy, making it suitable for families. The connected Heuberg Arena offers a ski kindergarten, children's carpet lift, and children's restaurant. A free ski bus connects the areas, and the region has a relaxed, family-friendly atmosphere.
resortCharacterDescription: The Ifen ski area is set against the dramatic Hoher Ifen mountain (2,230 m) and the Gottesacker plateau, a karst landscape designated as a nature reserve. The area is not a purpose-built resort but a natural mountain setting, with the nearest villages being Hirschegg and Riezlern in the Kleinwalsertal valley. The ski lifts were first installed in the 1970s and have been modernized since.
liftSystemDescription: The Ifen ski area has 4 lifts: two 10-person gondolas (Ifen I and Ifen II) and a 6-person chairlift (Olympiabahn) with weather protection and heated seats, plus a surface lift (Gaisbühllift) for beginners. The lifts have a combined capacity of 5,600 persons per hour. The Olympiabahn opens early at 07:45 in March and April. The lifts were first installed in the 1970s and have been upgraded to modern standards.
linkedResortsDescription: The Ifen ski area is part of the Walmendingerhorn-Ifen-Heuberg arena, which offers 67 km of pistes and 18 lifts, connected by a free ski bus. A single ski pass covers all three areas. Additionally, the wider 2-country ski region Oberstdorf-Kleinwalsertal includes seven ski areas (Ifen, Heuberg, Walmendingerhorn, Fellhorn-Kanzelwand, Nebelhorn, Söllereck) with a total of 130–150 km of pistes and 48 lifts, also linked by free ski buses.

terrainDescription: The terrain consists of slopes below the tree line on the Großer Ötscher (1,893 m) and Kleiner Ötscher (1,560 m). There are 7 km of easy blue runs, 8 km of intermediate red runs, and 4 km of difficult black runs, with a total of 19 km of groomed pistes. The longest run is 4 km. The ski area includes a Funslope, a Snowpark, and two FIS race courses for slalom and giant slalom. The Große Ötscherpiste and Riffel Downhill are challenging runs for advanced skiers.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are described as fair, with a 15% discount available when purchasing online compared to at-sight prices. The resort is marketed as an affordable winter vacation destination. A 6-day lift pass for the combined Hochkar-Ötscher area is valued at up to €196 in package deals.
familyDescription: The resort features a Sunny Kids Park with two magic carpets and childcare, along with two ski schools offering lessons for beginners. The Kinderland area is dedicated to young children. The village of Lackenhof is car-free, making it safe for families. There is a family downhill run and a Cross Trail course for older children.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski options include 2 bars and pubs and 1 club. There are 4 restaurants in the resort.
resortCharacterDescription: Lackenhof is a small, car-free mountain village in the Ybbstaler Alps, offering an idyllic alpine setting. The resort is not purpose-built but a traditional village with ski slopes accessible from the village.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of chairlifts and surface lifts, including 2 quad chairlifts, 1 double chairlift, and 3 T-bar lifts, with additional rope lifts and 2 magic carpets in the Sunny Kids Park. The official resort website lists six lift installations.
linkedResortsDescription: The ski area is linked with the Hochkar ski resort through a joint lift pass (Hochkar-Ötscher). Both resorts are managed by Hochkar & Ötscher Tourismus GmbH. The combined area offers more extensive skiing options.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 16 prepared pistes covering 12 km, with a mix of blue, red, and black runs including a FIS downhill course. The slopes are on the sunny side of the Mürztal valley, featuring wide-open pistes and forest trails. The upper area provides steeper terrain, while the lower slopes are gentler. The vertical drop is 386 m from 1,050 m to 1,450 m.
valueDescription: Lift passes are affordable, with a day pass for adults at €36 and a six-day pass at €155. Children's day passes are €19. The resort offers a free magic carpet for beginners. Mountain restaurants serve Austrian specialties at fair prices, and multi-day ski tickets are available at a discount.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as family-friendly, with a dedicated children's area featuring a free magic carpet at Gamsmugl. The slopes are uncrowded, with gentle learning slopes and professional ski instruction. Forest trails and routes provide a safe environment for children. The resort has a relaxed atmosphere and is listed among the top family ski areas in Styria. Accommodation includes the JUFA Hotel with a swimming pool and slide.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is set in a natural alpine landscape on the sunny side of the Mürztal valley, with stunning views of the Hohe Veitsch massif. It is a small, family-oriented ski area with a relaxed, authentic Austrian atmosphere, not a large purpose-built resort. The village of Veitsch offers basic amenities and accommodation.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of one quad chairlift, two T-bar lifts, one platter lift, and a free magic carpet for beginners. The lifts are modern and well-maintained, with minimal waiting times even during peak periods. Snowmaking covers the entire ski area, ensuring reliable conditions.

terrainDescription: Spiazzi di Gromo offers 7 to 8 runs totaling 15–18 km of skiable terrain, with a vertical drop of 600 m from 1,200 m to 1,800 m. The slopes are divided into one easy blue run at the base, five intermediate red runs, and two challenging black runs descending from Monte Corru. The black runs are long and steep, hosting international competitions. The terrain is set in the Orobie Alps, surrounded by dense forests, with a mix of gentle slopes for beginners and more demanding pistes for advanced skiers. The compact layout makes navigation straightforward.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is not a primary focus at Spiazzi di Gromo, but ski touring routes are available along Monte Timogno and Colle Vaccarizza. The resort's location within the Orobie Alps natural park provides opportunities for backcountry exploration, though no specific off-piste runs are marked or described in detail. Snowshoeing is also popular on the edges of the pistes.
valueDescription: Spiazzi di Gromo is one of the most economical ski resorts in Lombardy, with a day lift pass costing €35 on holidays and a six-day pass at €125 for adults (€69 for children). The resort is known for being budget-friendly, especially for day trips from Milan and Bergamo. Accommodation options include affordable hotels, apartments, and agriturismi. The overall cost is low compared to larger Alpine resorts, making it a good value for families and groups.
familyDescription: The resort is family-friendly with a dedicated beginner area featuring a blue run at the base served by a magic carpet. The ski school (Scuola Sci Spiazzi) is highly rated, especially for school groups. Off-slope activities include a 1,000 m bobsleigh track, a natural ice skating rink, and a sledding run for children. However, the resort lacks a dedicated snow park and children's play area, as noted in some reviews. The compact village layout makes it easy to keep track of children.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is relaxed and low-key, centered around the Rifugio Vodala at 1,750 m, which can host up to 300 people and offers typical Lombard cuisine. In the evenings, the rifugio hosts singing and dancing, popular with younger visitors. A few local bars and restaurants in the village serve local dishes, but the scene is quiet rather than lively.
resortCharacterDescription: Spiazzi di Gromo is a small ski resort that has grown organically at the edge of the medieval village of Gromo, in the Val Seriana. The resort consists of a new nucleus of cottages and cozy hotels set in a dense forest, contrasting with the ancient stone buildings of the original village. It is not a purpose-built station but a natural expansion of an existing settlement, offering a picturesque setting in the Orobie Alps.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises five lifts: four chairlifts (one triple and three double chairs) and one surface lift (magic carpet). There are no gondolas or cable cars. The lifts have an hourly capacity of 5,000 people and serve the slopes from the base at 1,200 m to the top at 1,800 m. The system is adequate for the resort's size but not high-speed or modern.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Col de Rousset consists of 26 runs spread across an elevation from 1,255 m to 1,700 m. The terrain is dominated by beginner and intermediate runs, with 46% beginner, 29% intermediate, 18% advanced, and 7% expert according to OnTheSnow. There are two dedicated beginner areas: one in the heart of the resort with small green runs served by a carpet, a snow line, and a small ski lift, and another on the Beure plateau with a second snow line. The resort also features a snowpark and a boardercross for freestyle enthusiasts. The longest run is 1.3 km, and total skiable terrain is 22 km. The slopes are set between forests and cliffs, offering panoramic views over the Diois valley.
valueDescription: Col de Rousset offers good value for a small family resort. A family pass costs €79.60 per day for up to four people (2 adults and 2 children under 18, or 1 adult and 3 children), with additional children at €13 each. Daily adult lift tickets are around €18 on weekdays and weekends, with half-day tickets at €16. A six-day adult pass is €115.90. The resort also offers a snow shuttle from Die for €9 round trip, making it accessible without a car. Season passes (€231 for adults) also grant access to the nearby resorts of Font d'Urle and Lus-la-Jarjatte. Children under 5 ski free.
familyDescription: Col de Rousset is family-friendly with several dedicated facilities. The ESF (French Ski School) offers a Kindergarten for children aged 6-9 and a Piou-Piou initiation club for ages 3-5. There are two beginner areas with carpets and snow lines. Non-ski activities include a rail sled, tubing, dog-sledding, and sledging runs. The resort provides off-bag rooms with microwaves and toilets, one in the heart of the resort and one at the Beure car park. A snow shuttle from Die (€9 round trip) makes access easier for families without a car. Children under 5 ski for free, and the family pass allows four people to ski together at a reduced rate.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski at Col de Rousset is low-key and centered on the resort's dining options. The resort has bars and restaurants serving sweet and savoury crepes, traditional dishes like gratin de ravioles and tartiflette, and hot and cold drinks. At the Beure car park, L'échappée Belle offers ravioles, daily specials, soups, and waffles. A snack bar and bread store operate within the resort. For more variety, the village of Saint-Agnan-en-Vercors (8 km away) has a bakery, bar, and restaurant, while La Chapelle en Vercors or Die (30 minutes by car) offer additional restaurants, bars, and amenities. There is no mention of nightclubs or lively bars.
resortCharacterDescription: Col de Rousset is a small, family-oriented resort situated at a mountain pass in the Vercors High Plateaux Nature Reserve. The resort is set between forests and cliffs, with panoramic views over the Diois valley. It was developed on former communal pastures and has a functional layout with ski-in/ski-out access from accommodation. The resort consists of a central area with shops, restaurants, and lift bases, plus an upper car park at Beure. The architecture is not described in detail, but the setting is natural rather than purpose-built in a traditional alpine style.
liftSystemDescription: The resort has 11 lifts: one high-speed detachable quad chairlift and 10 surface lifts (including 6 téléskis and 1 téléski école). The chairlift provides access to the main slopes, while surface lifts serve beginner areas and upper sectors. Lifts operate from 9:00 to 17:00 daily.
linkedResortsDescription: The Col de Rousset season pass also provides access to the nearby resorts of Font d'Urle and Lus-la-Jarjatte, both in the Drôme region. Additionally, the Grande Traversée du Vercors, a 29 km groomed cross-country trail, connects Col de Rousset to Vassieux and Font d'Urle, linking the resorts for Nordic skiing.

terrainDescription: The terrain consists of four named slopes on the north face of Mount Olympus: the Zeus slope (380 m, advanced, FIS homologated), Hera (350 m, beginner), Aphrodite (150 m, beginner), and Hermes (150 m, intermediate). The total groomed piste is approximately 3 km, with a vertical drop of about 80 m from 1,930 m to 1,850 m. The slopes are short and compact, suitable for a day trip, and the resort is the southernmost ski destination in Europe.
valueDescription: Cyprus is one of the cheapest skiing destinations in Europe. A family of four can ski for a week in late February for a total of £474, including return flights, car hire, and second-hand equipment. Lift passes are inexpensive and can be purchased online or at the resort. Accommodation in nearby villages like Prodromos or Platres is affordable, and the resort itself offers equipment hire and day passes.
familyDescription: The resort has a dedicated baby lift on the Sun Valley slopes, serving the 150 m Aphrodite and Hermes runs, which are suitable for beginners and children. Ski lessons can be arranged on the day at the ski school huts near the hire building. The compact layout and short slopes make it easy for families to stay together, and the resort is less crowded than major Alpine destinations. The Sun Valley Baby Lift is specifically noted for children.
resortCharacterDescription: The ski resort is located on the north face of Mount Olympus, the highest peak in Cyprus, at an elevation of around 1,850–1,930 m. The base area consists of the Cyprus Ski Club building with a shop, hire facilities, and refreshment areas. The nearest villages are Troodos (3 km) and Prodromos (5 km), which offer accommodation and traditional Cypriot tavernas. The surrounding Troodos Mountains are known for Byzantine churches and forested slopes, giving the area a natural, non-commercial character. The resort is not a purpose-built ski village but a mountain-top facility with a laid-back atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of four lifts: one chairlift on the Zeus slope (380 m) and three T-bar surface lifts (Hera 350 m, Aphrodite 150 m, Hermes 150 m). All lifts are equipped with smart gates using chip-and-pin passes, which can be purchased at cash desks at Sun Valley and North Face locations. The lifts operate daily from 10:00 am to 5:00 pm during the season.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 43 km of pistes between 1,400 m and 2,205 m, with 14.5 km of blue runs, 25 km of red runs, and 3.2 km of black runs. The terrain is dominated by wide, gentle slopes around Lake Turrach, suitable for beginners, and sporty red runs on the Kornock, Wildkopf, and Panorama lift sectors. The only black run is the 0.6 km Direttissima (piste 35). A 12 km circuit around the lake allows skiers to cross between Carinthia and Styria multiple times. The slopes are predominantly north-facing, with 44% north, 34% east, 6% south, and 17% west orientation. The highest point is at the Kornockbahn top station at 2,205 m, offering wide cruising runs.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available from the top of the Kornockbahn at 2,205 m, with open terrain on the soft, rounded Nockberge mountains. The backcountry offers opportunities for powder skiing, though no specific routes or couloirs are described in the sources. The area is within the Nockberge Biosphere Reserve, with natural snow conditions.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices for the 2025/2026 season: adult day pass €68, 6-day pass €363; child day pass €35, 6-day pass €182. Children under 6 ski for free. Multi-day tickets are valid throughout Carinthia and East Tyrol. Parking is free at the valley stations. Accommodation ranges from luxury hotels (Hotel Hochschober from €370/night) to mid-range (€150-200/night) and chalets (€200-400/night). The resort is small and uncrowded, with minimal lift queues, offering good value for a snow-sure Austrian resort.
familyDescription: The resort is family-friendly with dedicated nursery slopes by Lake Turrach, served by the Übungswiesenlift, Ottifantenlift, and Mängländerlift. Children under 6 ski for free. Night skiing is available on Wednesdays from 19:30 to 21:00 on the Übungswiesenlift. The Nocky Flitzer alpine coaster (1.6 km) operates year-round. Ice skating is possible on the frozen Turracher See in winter. The Pistenbutler service provides small surprises and assistance on the slopes. Ski schools offer courses for children and adults, and there is a Kids' Slope and a Fun Mountain with a snow park.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options include the K-Alm and Sonnalm bars, and the Nockalm pavilion serving beer on tap. There are 5 bars and pubs and 2 clubs in the resort. The atmosphere is relaxed rather than rowdy, with a focus on family-friendly activities. Night skiing and night sledding (Tuesdays and Thursdays) provide evening entertainment.
resortCharacterDescription: Turracher Höhe is a traditional mountain village on a pass summit at 1,795 m, straddling the border between Carinthia and Styria. The village is centered around Lake Turrach (1,763 m) and consists of two luxury hotels, over 30 small and medium-sized hotels and guest houses, and many vacation houses hidden in stone pine forests. There is no grocery store or gas station. An ecumenical church was built in 1985. The resort is located within the UNESCO Nockberge Biosphere Reserve, with soft, rounded mountain silhouettes. The atmosphere is calm and intimate, with a focus on nature.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 16 lifts: 6 six-seater chairlifts (Kornockbahn, Schafalmbahn, Zirbenwaldbahn, Panoramabahn, Turrachbahn, Wildkopfbahn), one combined chairlift and gondola (Panoramabahn), 8 drag lifts, and various training lifts. The hourly transport capacity exceeds 19,400 people, which is high for the size of the ski area, resulting in minimal queues. The lifts are modern, with the Wildkopf chairlift being a recent addition. The Kornockbahn is the main artery to the summit at 2,205 m. The Panoramabahn is accessible for guests with disabilities and families with strollers.

terrainDescription: The ski area Ginals-Unterbäch offers 20 km of marked pistes, predominantly blue and red runs. 63% of pistes are classified as beginner, 21% intermediate, and 16% advanced. The slopes are wide and uncrowded, with a vertical drop of 1,322 m from base at 1,228 m to summit at 2,550 m. The terrain includes a Swiss Snow Kids Village with a magic carpet, ski carousel, seesaws, jumps, and rails. The highest lift reaches 2,550 m, providing views of the Bietschhorn.
valueDescription: Lift passes are among the cheapest in Valais at CHF 49 per day for adults and CHF 33 for children. Accommodation options range from budget hostels and guesthouses to upscale hotels, with a wide variety available. The resort offers ski-in/ski-out chalets and apartments. A free ski bus connects Unterbäch to Eischoll and Bürchen for pass holders.
familyDescription: The resort is specifically designed for families, with a Swiss Snow Kids Village offering a magic carpet, ski carousel, and small terrain features. The ski school provides lessons for children and adults. The slopes are mostly gentle (63% beginner) and uncrowded. A free ski bus connects Unterbäch to Eischoll and Bürchen. Night skiing is available on Tuesdays during the main season from 4:30 to 6:30 p.m. for CHF 5.00.
apresSkiDescription: The village has two bars that often feature live music. There are five restaurants serving local specialties such as raclette, rösti, and Valais wine. The après-ski scene is relaxed rather than lively, with a focus on dining and drinks.
resortCharacterDescription: Unterbäch is a traditional Valais mountain village situated on a sunny high plateau at 1,200 m. It retains its alpine character with historic buildings, a church, and a municipal library. The village is not purpose-built; it has a genuine Swiss village atmosphere with local shops and restaurants. The entire Turtig/Wandfluh region is designated as part of the Inventory of Swiss Heritage Sites.
liftSystemDescription: The combined Unterbäch-Eischoll area has 11 lifts, including chairlifts, gondolas, and surface lifts. The main chairlift to Brandalp operates daily from 9:00 to 16:30. The aerial cableway from Raron to Unterbäch provides access from the valley, with cabins running every 30 minutes. Lifts are modern and queues are rare. Snowmaking covers 50% of the slopes with 60 machines.
linkedResortsDescription: Unterbäch is linked to the neighboring resort of Eischoll via the Strygge-Erez ski lift and connecting pistes, forming a combined ski area of 35 km of pistes and 7 lifts. The resort is also part of the Magic Pass, which provides access to 69 winter destinations in the region, including areas in Upper Valais, Disentis-Sedrun, Andermatt, and the Bernese Oberland.

terrainDescription: The resort has 41 marked runs: 3 green, 20 blue, 14 red, and 4 black, with a vertical drop of 800 m. The local valley offers mostly gentle blue runs, while the connection to Pra Loup introduces steeper red and black slopes. A dedicated beginner area with magic carpets and a small chairlift provides safe learning space. The upper slopes are above 2000 m, offering open terrain. A terrain park is available for snowboarders.
valueDescription: Lift passes for the Espace Lumière area cost €45 per day for adults and €38 for children (5–17) for the 2023–2024 season. The resort offers free shuttles between villages and slopes. Accommodation is available close to the pistes, with ski-in/ski-out options. Self-catering apartments and residences are common, keeping costs moderate compared to larger purpose-built resorts.
familyDescription: Val d'Allos La Foux holds the Famille Plus Montagne label, with a snow garden, toboggan runs, a children's village, and a play library in the resort centre. There is a 600 m² ice rink, a cinema, and the Verdon Express rail toboggan run. Ski school offers lessons for all ages, and the resort has a daycare for children from 1 to 5 years old. The traffic-free village centre allows families to walk everywhere in boots.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has 15 restaurants and 6 bars. The après-ski scene is quiet, with a few bars and restaurants in the village centre. A cinema, ice rink, and toboggan run provide evening entertainment. High-altitude terraces offer dining with views. A nightclub is also available. The atmosphere is relaxed rather than rowdy.
resortCharacterDescription: La Foux d'Allos is a purpose-built resort with mostly wooden buildings, avoiding large concrete complexes. Accommodation is ski-in/ski-out or a short walk from the slopes. The village is small and convenient, with all amenities within walking distance. It blends authenticity and modernity, set in the Mercantour National Park.
liftSystemDescription: Val d'Allos La Foux has 17 lifts: 7 drag lifts, 8 chair lifts, and 2 magic carpets. The lift system is a mix of older and newer installations, with some slow chairs and drags, but recent upgrades have improved efficiency. Queues are rare outside peak weeks. The link to Pra Loup can close in high winds.
linkedResortsDescription: Val d'Allos La Foux is part of the Espace Lumière ski area, which links it to Pra Loup and Val d'Allos Le Seignus, offering a combined 180 km of slopes with 120 lifts. The connection to Pra Loup is via the Agneliers valley, subject to snow conditions. The linked area includes 136 marked runs and serves a total of 33 lifts across the whole Val d'Allos resort.

terrainDescription: The ski resort has 8.3 km of slopes split across easy (46%), intermediate (29%), and difficult (25%) runs, situated between 1,460 m and 1,729 m elevation with a vertical drop of 269 m. The ski area offers views of the plateau and peaks of the Majella National Park, and also features a cross-country ski trail. Pescocostanzo village is noted as one of the most beautiful villages in Italy with a beautiful old town and ancient craft tradition.
familyDescription: The ski area includes a rope tow/baby lift called Lupetto with a length of 109 m and carrying capacity of 400 passengers per hour, suitable for beginners and children. The resort has ski-in/ski-out accommodation available.
resortCharacterDescription: Pescocostanzo is described as one of the most beautiful villages in Italy, featuring a beautiful old town and ancient craft tradition. The village offers various winter activities beyond skiing, including cross-country skiing on a trail that is one of the most popular in the region.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 4 lifts with a total capacity of 4,217 passengers per hour and total lift length of 1.7 km. The main chairlift Vallefura was installed in 2009 by LEITNER, measuring 929 m with a carrying capacity of 2,010 passengers per hour. Additional lifts include the 2006 Valle Gelata 2-person chairlift (358 m), the 1977 Monte Calvario J-bar (341 m), and the Lupetto rope tow/baby lift (109 m).

terrainDescription: The ski area on the Dachberg is accessed by an 8-seater gondola from the village to Gadastatt at 1,817m, where a practice area (Kleinvalser Arena) with a conveyor belt and platter lift serves beginners. Three T-bar lifts ascend from Gadastatt to nearly 3,000m, offering wide intermediate cruising runs and a blue slope from the Faltschona drag lift. The valley run is a 2.5km advanced descent; most skiers take the gondola back down. The terrain includes high alpine runs with views from the Bernina massif to the Valais 4,000m peaks, and the longest run is 10km over a vertical drop of 1,630m.
offPisteDescription: Freeriders can access powder snow slopes away from the pistes, with the high summit altitude (nearly 3,000m) and reliable snowfall providing good conditions. The area is described as having abundant freeride terrain, with untouched powder off-piste that can remain fresh for days after a storm. The off-piste includes high alpine slopes around the Dachberg and is a key draw for the resort, which markets itself as a freeride destination.
valueDescription: Vals is considered the best value ski resort in Switzerland, with an adult day lift pass costing CHF39 for the 2025/26 season and children under 16 skiing for free. The gondola from the village to the mountain is free of charge. A season ticket costs CHF399. These prices are among the most affordable in the Alps, and the resort offers fair pricing for lift passes and accommodation.
familyDescription: The Kleinvalser Arena at Gadastatt (1,817m) features a conveyor belt and platter lift for beginners, plus a pony lift for children. The Swiss Snowsports School Vals offers children's ski school, private and group lessons. The gondola is free for all, making access easy for families. The valley run is difficult, so families can take the gondola down. Ski rental is available at the valley station and in the village.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key, with a few cozy bars and mountain restaurants such as Bergrestaurant Gadastatt, Dachberghütte, and scHIRMBAR. The main evening attraction is the world-famous Therme Vals thermal spa, a listed architectural masterpiece of stone and light, open until late. Nightlife beyond dinner and drinks is limited, with the village offering a relaxed, tranquil atmosphere.
resortCharacterDescription: Vals is a traditional, picturesque Swiss village with a population of 946, featuring Walser architecture with timber-rich dwellings and sharply pitched roofs. The village centre, Vals Platz, lies secluded behind two gorges, accessed via a single valley corridor. It is not purpose-built; the historic character is complemented by the modern Therme Vals spa (built 1996 by Peter Zumthor) and the surrounding alpine landscape.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of one modern 8-seater gondola from the village to Gadastatt (500m vertical), three older-style T-bar lifts from Gadastatt to the Dachberg summit (remaining 1,130m vertical), and two conveyor belts/platter lifts for beginners at Gadastatt. The gondola is free. Operating hours are 9:00–16:30 for the gondola and 9:15–15:45 for the upper lifts. The system is small but efficient for the terrain.

terrainDescription: The Valtournenche ski area covers 54 km of pistes, with 17 km easy, 35 km medium, and 2 km difficult. The slopes above the Salette gondola terminal at 2,245m are mostly gentle blue runs, well above the treeline. Two chairlifts provide uplift to further straightforward blues, with one linking to the more challenging upper sector and the Cime Bianche col connection to Cervinia. The terrain is predominantly undemanding and intermediate-friendly, with wide, flowing runs ideal for relaxed cruising.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste in Valtournenche benefits from the high-altitude terrain and links to Cervinia and Zermatt. The Theodul Glacier offers some of the best snow in the region. A guide is essential due to the high mountain terrain. Heli-skiing is available. It is illegal to ski off-piste in Italy without full avalanche safety equipment.
valueDescription: Valtournenche is less expensive than neighbouring Cervinia, offering a more budget-friendly base for accessing the same ski area. Lift pass prices for the local Valtournenche area are €43 for one day, €121.50 for three days, and €227 for six days. The village has good-value family-run restaurants and a range of accommodation options. It is considered one of the best-value gateways into the Matterhorn ski region.
familyDescription: Valtournenche offers a dedicated children's area called the Wild West Zone, and an excellent beginners' zone near the Salette gondola. The village has a large leisure centre with a swimming pool, sauna, gym, climbing wall, indoor tennis courts, and a multi-sports hall. Other family activities include snowshoeing, sled dog rides, ice skating, and ice climbing. The relaxed, traditional village atmosphere is suitable for families seeking a quieter base.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Valtournenche is low-key, with a handful of local bars and hotel lounges such as Bar Maquignaz, Bar Pub 4810, and Bar La Copa Pan. The latter sometimes hosts live music. For a livelier scene, Cervinia is 9 km up the valley, but the bus service does not run late and taxis are expensive. Evening entertainment is relaxed rather than rowdy.
resortCharacterDescription: Valtournenche is a traditional Italian mountain village terraced into the west-facing flank of the Valtournenche valley. It has much more traditional charm than neighbouring Cervinia, with narrow streets, local shops, and a relaxed atmosphere. The village is surrounded by softer yet dramatic alpine scenery, and offers a picturesque, authentic base for exploring the wider ski area.
liftSystemDescription: The Valtournenche ski area is served by a 12-person gondola from the village to Salette at 2,245m, with a capacity of 2,400 persons per hour. Above Salette, two chairlifts provide uplift, and a new 6-seater Gran Sometta chairlift (installed for winter 2023/24) improves access to the Cervinia link. The lift system is modern and fast, with minimal queuing. A ski-bus connects the village to the gondola base, and many hotels offer their own shuttles.
linkedResortsDescription: Valtournenche's ski area is linked to Breuil-Cervinia and, via the Theodul Pass, to Zermatt in Switzerland, forming the Matterhorn Ski Paradise with over 360 km of pistes. The link allows skiers to cross the border and access Zermatt's more challenging terrain. A new 6-seater Gran Sometta chairlift (2023/24) improves the connection to Cervinia, and the Matterhorn Alpine Crossing (opened March 2024) allows year-round travel between Cervinia and Zermatt without skis.

terrainDescription: The terrain is centered on the Ciampedie natural terrace at 2,000 m, with slopes facing the Catinaccio massif. Most runs are easy (69% beginner), with wide, gentle blue cruisers suitable for families and intermediates. The valley run to Vigo di Fassa is a long intermediate descent. More challenging options include the Thöni run, a long red with sweeping curves, and the short black Tomba run (almost 1 km) used by World Cup skier Alberto Tomba for training. The highest point, Pra Martin, offers an easy descent. The ski area has 13 km of pistes, with 6.4 km of easy slopes.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are moderate: a one-day adult pass costs €76, youth €52, and child €38. Accommodation in the valley is noted for good value, with many affordable options. The FlySki Shuttle offers free transfers for children under 8 and a 50% discount for ages 8–12, reducing family travel costs. Buying a Val di Fassa/Carezza pass online saves 5%.
familyDescription: Ciampedie is especially suited for families, with a dedicated Kinderpark managed by the Vigo di Fassa Passo Costalunga Ski School, where children can ski and play. The beginner area includes a magic carpet, the Campo Scuola Ciampedie chairlift with a very easy slope, and a Baby Park for first-timers. Two ski schools operate on-site: Scuola italiana di Sci Vigo di Fassa and Scuola Sci Vajolet Pozza. Non-skiers can enjoy snow walks from Ciampedie to Pian Pecei and Gardeccia.
resortCharacterDescription: Vigo di Fassa is a traditional Ladin village in the Fassa Valley, with historic architecture and a compact center. It is not purpose-built; the resort retains alpine charm. The Ciampedie area is a natural terrace at 2,000 m with stunning panoramic views of the Dolomites, including the Catinaccio/Rosengarten group, a UNESCO World Heritage site. The village has a relaxed, authentic atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 6 lifts: a modern cable car from Vigo to Ciampedie (built 1985, rebuilt 2005 by Doppelmayr) and five chairlifts. Four are high-speed detachable chairlifts, most with bubbles: Vajolet 1 and 2 (installed 2017 by Leitner), Pian Pecei-Pra Martin (2003, Doppelmayr), and Pian Pecei-Ciampedie (2018). A fixed-grip chairlift (Campo Scuola Ciampedie, 1995) serves the beginner area. An escalator connects the village center to the cable car base station.
linkedResortsDescription: Vigo di Fassa is part of the Val di Fassa/Carezza ski area, which includes several sectors (Catinaccio, Buffaure, Ciampac, Belvedere, Col Rodella, Carezza) linked by ski bus. With a Dolomiti Superski pass, skiers can access the entire 1,200 km network, including the Sellaronda circuit that connects Val Gardena, Alta Badia, Arabba, and Val di Fassa. The Catinaccio area itself is not directly connected by piste to other sectors, but a ski bus runs from Pera to the Buffaure lift. The Panorama Ski Tour links the area to other valleys.

terrainDescription: The resort has 20 km of slopes consisting of 11 km easy/blue runs (55%) and 9 km intermediate/red runs (45%), with no difficult/black runs. The ski area is located above Lago Ampollino in the Sila National Park in Calabria, between elevations of 1,350 m and 1,650 m. A valley run is available, and artificial snow-making is operational. A snow park and night skiing at Palumbo Sila are also offered.
offPisteDescription: No dedicated off-piste terrain is described in the source material. The resort lacks advanced/difficult runs entirely, and the low-to-moderate altitude (1,350-1,650 m) in Calabria limits natural off-piste opportunities. Skiers seeking off-piste terrain would need to look to nearby larger ski areas.
valueDescription: The ski pass costs €35 per day according to visitor reviews, with complaints that the price increased while only 2 of approximately 20 km of slopes are consistently open during weekdays. Visitors note limited services during the week and describe the resort as small but sufficient for southern Italy.
familyDescription: The resort offers beginner-friendly terrain with easy runs making up 55% of the total slope network. A snow park is available for children and beginners. However, user reviews report that only the main chairlift operates during weekdays, with additional lifts opening on weekends when more visitors arrive, potentially limiting variety for families visiting mid-week.
apresSkiDescription: Visitor reviews describe very limited après-ski options, noting that after the slopes close, the only option is an ice rink (palaghiaccio), then nothing. The resort has Bar Ristorante Il Rifugio and Club Palumbo mentioned as dining and nightlife options, but one reviewer stated it is difficult to even buy groceries in the evening. Another noted that pisteurs use the "pista guido" for snowmobile excursions, creating conflicts with skiers.
resortCharacterDescription: Villaggio Palumbo is a small resort village located 0.1 km from the ski area in the Province of Crotone, Calabria. The area sits in the Sila National Park at an altitude of 1,338-1,651 m. Nearby villages include Caprara (1.3 km), Trepidò (5 km), and Cotronei (21 km). Accommodation options include rental houses, apartments, and a 4-star hotel directly on the slopes.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 3 main lifts: Palumbo Sila I (3-person fixed-grip chairlift, 745 m, 1,500 persons/hour), Palumbo Sila II (2-person fixed-grip chairlift, 744 m, 1,200 persons/hour), and Palumbo Sila III (T-bar lift, 300 m, 1,200 persons/hour). A people mover is also present. Total capacity is 3,900 passengers/hour across 1.8 km of lift length. Fixed-grip lifts indicate an older infrastructure without high-speed capability.
linkedResortsDescription: Villaggio Palumbo is part of the Palumbosila ski area located above Lago Ampollino in the Sila National Park. Nearby ski areas include Lorica (16.9 km), Camigliatello Silano (24.9 km), and Fago del Soldato (27.5 km). The resort is one of 4 ski resorts in Calabria and one of 30 in Southern Italy.

terrainDescription: The ski area is compact and predominantly west-facing (73% of slopes), with 23% facing north. The terrain includes 8 km of novice runs, 8 km of intermediate runs, and 10 km of expert runs, offering a balanced mix for all ability levels. A 7 km toboggan run descends from Giw to the village, and 60 km of winter hiking and snowshoe trails traverse the surrounding landscape. The slopes are well-prepared and the resort's sunny, high-altitude position provides reliable snow conditions.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs CHF53 for adults and CHF35 for children, with a six-day pass at CHF271 for adults and CHF181 for children. The resort is small and less expensive than major Swiss destinations, and self-catering accommodation is available. The compact layout means most amenities are within walking distance, reducing the need for additional transport costs.
familyDescription: The resort is child-friendly with a compact, convenient layout. A 7 km toboggan run, a natural ice rink (free of charge), and 60 km of hiking and snowshoe trails provide off-slope activities. The ski school has 10 instructors and offers lessons for all ages. The predominantly easy and intermediate terrain makes it suitable for families with beginners, and the free ski bus connects to neighbouring resorts for more variety.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene is quiet, with 6 restaurants, 1 bar/café, and 1 disco. Nightlife is limited, focusing on relaxed dining and small-scale evening entertainment. The resort's small size means most venues are within walking distance of accommodation.
resortCharacterDescription: Visperterminen is a traditional alpine village with a relaxed, authentic atmosphere. It has 2 hotels and 69 tourist beds, along with a supermarket and a tourist information office. The village retains its original character, with a compact layout that makes it easy to walk between slopes, accommodation, and amenities. The resort is car-free in the centre, adding to its peaceful charm.
liftSystemDescription: The resort has 5 lifts: 2 chairlifts and 3 drag lifts, with a total capacity of 4,040 persons per hour. Lift lines are typically short due to the small size of the area. The lifts provide access to a vertical drop of 998 m, from the base at 1,340 m to the summit at 2,338 m.
linkedResortsDescription: Visperterminen is part of the Rund um Visp ski area, which includes several small resorts such as Bürchen, Unterbäch, Eischoll, and Gspon. These are connected by a free ski bus service, offering over 100 km of slopes in total. The area is also accessible from Visp, which serves as a base for 20 ski areas within an hour's travel, including the Aletsch Arena, Saas-Fee, and Zermatt.

terrainDescription: The Warth-Schröcken sector has 15 blue pistes, 10 red pistes, 10 black pistes, and 7 ungroomed ski routes. The steepest marked run is black piste 253 (Ochsakopf), which reaches a gradient of 70% in two places. The terrain includes north-facing slopes that hold snow well, and the local slopes are low-lying but benefit from heavy snowfall. The sector is at the northern edge of the Ski Arlberg area, with access to the wider Arlberg via the Auenfeldjet gondola.
offPisteDescription: Warth-Schröcken receives an average of 11 metres of natural snow per winter, making it one of the snowiest resorts in the Alps. The north-facing slopes and high snowfall create deep powder conditions. Ungroomed ski routes and areas under lifts and next to groomed runs offer off-piste opportunities. The Arlberg region is known for its legendary off-piste terrain, and Warth-Schröcken provides access to it via the linked ski area.
valueDescription: The Warth-Schröcken local lift pass is available for 1 to 2.5 days and is cheaper than the full Ski Arlberg pass. For beginners, there are comparatively low-cost day passes or per-ride tickets for the nursery lifts. The resort has a small supermarket and a handful of restaurants with reasonable prices. Accommodation options include self-catering apartments and hotels, with the village being less expensive than nearby Lech or St Anton.
familyDescription: Warth has child-friendly nursery slopes on the edge of the village, with practice areas at Steffisalp-Express (Pauli’s Children’s Land) and at Salober (Pauli's Ski Arena). Ski school standards are high, with English-speaking instructors available. There are toboggan runs, winter hiking trails, and cosy huts suitable for families. Schröcken’s nursery slopes are a bus ride away at the Saloberjet base station. The village is car-free in parts and has a quiet, safe atmosphere.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Warth-Schröcken is low-key, with a handful of local bars in the village. The resort is not known for lively nightlife; instead, it offers a quiet, relaxed atmosphere. There are a few restaurants serving traditional Austrian cuisine. For more vibrant après-ski, visitors can take the gondola to Lech or St Anton.
resortCharacterDescription: Warth and Schröcken are small, traditional Alpine villages with a combined population of around 183 in Warth. The architecture is typical Vorarlberg style, with wooden chalets and a church spire. The villages are not purpose-built; they have retained their original character. Warth is car-free in the centre, and the road between Warth and Lech is closed in winter, adding to the secluded feel.
liftSystemDescription: The Warth-Schröcken lift system includes modern high-speed six-seater chairlifts and gondolas. The Auenfeldjet gondola (built 2013/14) connects Warth-Schröcken to Lech-Zürs, and the Flexenbahn (2016/17) links to St Anton, making the entire Ski Arlberg area accessible. The lifts are efficient with minimal queues, and the piste map is dominated by fast 6-seaters and slick gondolas.
linkedResortsDescription: Warth-Schröcken is part of the Ski Arlberg, the largest connected ski area in Austria, with 300 km of groomed slopes and 200 km of deep snow runs.
